Tafseer:
Alif-Lam-Mim-Sad. A detailed discussion on the abbreviated letters (Huroof-e-Muqatta'at) has already been presented under Alif-Lam-Mim in Surah Al-Baqarah. Here, the letter 'Sad' is added to Alif, Lam, and Mim. We have previously pointed out that the Surahs which share similar names also share a general commonality in their themes. Therefore, if you study this Surah closely, you will realize that many of its discussions resemble those of Surah Al-Baqarah, even though there is a distinction of Makki and Madani between the two, and their addressees are also different.
Here, two possibilities are possible in the composition of the sentence. 'Alif-Lam-Mim-Sad' can be treated as an independent sentence by assuming the omission of the subject (Mubtada). Alternatively, if one wishes to connect it to the succeeding words, it can be regarded as the subject (Mubtada) and 'Kitabun unzila ilaika fala yakun fi sadrika harajum minhu' as its predicate (Khabar). We have adopted the first form, assuming the subject to be omitted in 'Kitabun unzila ilaika' as well. In any case, there is no difference in meaning between the two forms.

"Kitabun unzila ilaika fala yakun fi sadrika harajum minhu"—the word 'haraj' means constriction, distress, and anxiety.
Consolation to the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the limit of his responsibility during the peak of Quraish's opposition:
This verse was revealed as a source of peace and consolation for the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). This period, as is clear from the outline of the Surah's themes, was the peak of the Quraish's opposition. They had stooped to using the lowest of weapons. To harass you, they would present new demands every day. On one hand, there was this intensity of opposition; on the other hand, the Holy Prophet's (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) sense of his duty of dawah was so intense that despite making all efforts, he remained anxious lest there be some shortcoming on his part because of which these people were not accepting such a clear and obvious truth. These two factors combined to place a heavy burden on his heart. The Qur'an has lightened both burdens here. He was enjoined to remain indifferent to the opposition of the Quraish in this manner: this Book is neither presented by you on your own, nor did you bring it down upon yourself by requesting God; rather, it has been sent down to you from God without your seeking or wishing. Why then should you put yourself in distress and anxiety because of the opposition of its opponents? The God Who sent it down will also provide reinforcement and a vanguard for its support and victory. He is neither a weak entity, nor is He detached or unaware of the circumstances. He knows well what things you need to fulfill the responsibility He has placed upon you, and how much strength you require to remove the obstacles from the path. He will provide all these things, so rest assured and do not put yourself in anxiety.
"Litundhira bihi wa dhikra lil-mu'minin"—this defines the limit of the Holy Prophet's (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) responsibility regarding this Book: your duty is only to warn people through it about the consequences of denying the Messenger and the events of the Day of Resurrection. Whether they believe or not is not your concern; your responsibility is only warning and conveyance of the message. The phrase "wa dhikra lil-mu'minin" is semantically conjoined to "litundhira", but it is in the form of a noun instead of a verb. Bringing it in the form of a noun is intended to express a matter of fact. That is, as far as warning is concerned, you should warn these disbelievers, but only the believers will benefit from it as a reminder. This theme has been expressed in various Surahs throughout the Qur'an. We present one example: Ta-Ha (1) "We have not sent down the Qur'an to you to cause you distress (2) but only as a reminder to those who fear (3) a revelation from Him Who created the earth and the high heavens (4)" (Translation: This is Surah Ta-Ha; We have not sent down the Qur'an upon you so that your life becomes miserable for you; this is merely a reminder for those who fear; it has been sent down with great care from the One Who created the earth and the high heavens).

"Ittabi'u ma unzila ilaikum mir rabbikum wa la tattabi'u min doonihi awliya'a, qaleelam ma tadhakkaroon."
Threat of punishment to the disbelievers of Quraish:
Generally, people have considered the addressees of this verse to be the Muslims, but the context and the words of the verse prove that the address is to the disbelievers of Quraish. After consoling the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in the preceding verse, the Quraish are now threatened: follow this which has been sent down to you from your Lord, and do not follow other protectors and partners besides Him; these imaginary protectors and idols will be of no use to you. After this, it is said in a tone of regret and sorrow: "qaleelam ma tadhakkaroon" (how little you take heed), meaning you are such ill-fated people that you rarely take warning.

"Wa kam min qaryatin ahlaknaha faja'aha ba'suna bayatan aw hum qa'iloon."
The meaning of 'Qaryah': The word Qaryah encompasses both the town itself and its inhabitants. For this reason, in using pronouns, demonstrative pronouns, and verbs, sometimes the literal word is considered, and sometimes its meaning. This style is common in every language.
'Qa'iloon' is derived from 'Qailulah'. Qailulah means taking a midday rest; sleeping is not an essential requirement of it. The land of the Arabs is a hot country, which is why at noon people are compelled to rest in their respective houses, camps, tents, and orchards.
A misconception of some commentators: Some commentators, from the words "faja'aha ba'suna bayatan aw hum qa'iloon", conceived the idea that Allah's punishment only comes when people are asleep at night or during the day. However, this is contrary to both history and the description of the Qur'an. In Surah Al-An'am it is said: "Qul ara'aitakum in atakum 'adhabullahi baghtatan aw jahratan hal yuhlaku illal-qawm-udh-dhalimoon" (47) (Say, tell me, if the punishment of Allah comes upon you suddenly/secretly or openly, will any be destroyed except the wrongdoing people?). In this very Surah Al-A'raf, after narrating the stories of the punished nations, the commentary is made in these words: "Afa-amina ahlul-qura ay ya'tiyahum ba'suna bayataw-wa hum na'imoon (97) Awa amina ahlul-qura ay ya'tiyahum ba'suna duhaw-wa hum yal'aboon (98)" (Did the people of the cities then feel secure from Our punishment coming to them at night while they were asleep? Or did the people of the cities feel secure from Our punishment coming to them in the morning while they were playing?). In our view, the phrase "faja'aha ba'suna bayatan aw hum qa'iloon" is not intended to show that God's punishment always comes when people are asleep, but rather to show that God's punishment came secretly in the darkness of the night, and also openly in broad daylight. At whatever time it came, it came; no one could stop it and no one could save themselves from it. Only those were saved from it who had the protection of Allah.

The actual warning: This is the warning referred to in "litundhira bihi". The Quraish are threatened: how many nations and towns are there upon whom, when God willed, He sent His punishment by night or by day, and they were destroyed. None of them could stand against God; instead, every nation surrendered itself to the divine punishment while admitting its guilt. The meaning is that if you do not follow what God has sent down to you, then this same end is to be yours. Today you boast, but at that time all your arrogance will be taken out, and you will admit your guilt with your own mouths. However, at that time, this admission will not be of any benefit to you.

"Falanas'alann-alladhina ursila ilaihim walanas'alann-al-mursaleen."
Detail of the warning: We have mentioned that the Messengers of Allah warn people of two things. First, of the punishment that inevitably comes upon a nation that denies its Messenger. Second, of the reward and punishment with which every person must inevitably deal in the Hereafter. In the preceding verse, warning was given regarding the first thing. Now, they are being informed about this second thing. It is said that a day is coming when We will surely question those nations to whom We sent Our Messengers, and We will also question the Messengers themselves.
The detail of the questioning that will take place with the nations is described in the Qur'an as follows: "Kullama ulqiya feeha fawjun sa'alahum khazanatuha alam ya'tikum nadheer. Qaloo bala qad ja'ana nadheerun fakadhhabna wa qulna ma nazzalallahu min shayin in antum illa fee dalalin kabeer. Wa qaloo law kunna nasma'u aw na'qilu ma kunna fee as-habis-sa'eer. Fa'tarafoo bidhambihim fasuhqal-li-as-habis-sa'eer" (Whenever a group is thrown into it, its keepers ask them, 'Did there not come to you a warner?' They will say, 'Yes, a warner had come to us, but we denied and said: Allah has not sent down anything; you are only in great error.' And they will say, 'If only we had listened or reasoned, we would not be among the companions of the Blaze.' Thus they will confess their sin, so far away be the companions of the Blaze).
The reference to the questioning of the Messengers is given in Surah Al-Ma'idah as follows: "Yawma yajma'ullahu-r-rusula fayaqoolu mada ujibtum" (On the Day when Allah will gather the Messengers and say, 'What response did you receive?').

"Falanaqussanna 'alaihim bi'ilmiw-wa ma kunna gha'ibeen." The meaning is that on that day, We will narrate to the Messengers and their nations the entire past account with complete knowledge and information, detailing how Our Messengers fulfilled the duty of conveying the message, and how those who denied them did so deliberately. It is said: We have not been detached or unaware of those situations and events for even a single moment. Whatever happened, happened before Us. It should be clear that this narration will be for the purpose of cutting off any excuse, so that no room remains for anyone to utter a word.

"Wal-waznu yawma'idhin-il-haqq." The meaning is that on that day, the only thing possessing weight will be the truth. Falsehood will have no weight at all. The scales that Allah Almighty will set up on the Day of Resurrection will weigh the deeds of everyone and show how much portion of truth is in them. Then, those whose scales are heavy—meaning the quantity of truth with them is greater—will be the successful ones, and those whose scales are light will be the losers. Regarding the weightiness and weightlessness of deeds, the Qur'an has also stated this principle: "Qul hal nunabbi'ukum bil-akhsareena a'mala. Alladhina dalla sa'yuhum fil-hayatid-dunya wa hum yahsaboona annahum yuhsinoona sun'a. Ula'ikalladhina kafaroo bi-ayati rabbihim wa liqa'ihi fahabitath a'maluhum fala nuqeemu lahum yawmal-qiyamati wazna" (Say, shall We inform you of the greatest losers as to their deeds? Those whose effort is lost in worldly life while they think that they are doing well in work. Those are the ones who disbelieved in the verses of their Lord and in the meeting with Him, so their deeds have become worthless, and We will not assign to them any weight on the Day of Resurrection) (Al-Kahf). From this, it is known that on the scales of the Resurrection, the only deeds that will have weight are those performed for the pleasure of God and for the Hereafter. Those deeds devoid of this characteristic are neither truth, nor will they have any weight in the divine scale.

"Wa man khaffat mawazeenuhu fa-ula'ikalladhina khasiroo anfusahum bima kanoo bi-ayatina yadhlimoon."
A stylistic feature of the language: We have pointed out in more than one place this stylistic feature of the language: when there is no apparent harmony between the relative pronoun (Sila) and the verb, there is an implication (Tadmeen). That is, we assume an omitted verb that fills the existing gap. The benefit of this is that fewer words are used, but the meaning becomes very vast. Here, if the implication is unfolded, the entire statement would be: "bima kanoo yakfuroona bi-ayatina wa yadhlimoona anfusahum" (because they used to disbelieve in Our verses and wrong their own souls).
The theme of the succeeding verses 10 to 25: First, addressing the Quraish, Allah Almighty reminds them of His favor upon them and reproaches them: how Allah gave them power and authority in this sacred land, secured them from fear, and opened for them the ways of livelihood and sustenance, yet instead of being grateful and obedient to God, they became ungrateful and disobedient.
After this, the story of Adam and Iblis—which was narrated to the Jews in Surah Al-Baqarah—is narrated to the Quraish with some additional details, showing that the oath of eternal enmity Satan swore against Adam and his progeny was fulfilled when he deceived Adam and Eve and had them expelled from Paradise. In the same way, he has fulfilled that oath against you too, and you have completely fallen into his trap, and the consequence of this is obvious. Read the subsequent verses in this light.

"Walaqad makkannakum fil-ardi wa ja'alna lakum feeha ma'ayisha, qaleelam ma tashkuroon."
'Ardh' refers to the sacred land of Haram. 'Tamkeen fil-ardh' means granting authority and power in the land. For example: "Wa kadhalika makkanna li-Yusufa fil-ardh" (And thus We established Joseph in the land of Egypt with authority and power). Although 'ardh' is literally general, since the address is to the Quraish, it refers here to the land of Haram in which the Quraish possessed authority and power. 'Ma'ayish' refers to those economic facilities and blessings which, through the prayer of Prophet Abraham and the blessing of the House of Allah, the Arabs in general, and the Quraish in particular, received in an uncultivable valley. These blessings and favors are mentioned in various places in the Qur'an, and we have referred to them in detail in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. It is also referred to in Surah Al-Qasas: "Awalam numakkil-lahum haraman aminay-yujba ilaihi thamaratu kulli shay" (Al-Qasas: 57) (Have We not established for them a safe sanctuary to which are brought the fruits of all things?). "Qaleelam ma tashkuroon"—this is the primary point being made, for which the preceding statements were mentioned as an introduction. The meaning is that for the sake of the prayers of Abraham and Ishmael and because of the House of Allah, Allah Almighty granted you the blessing of authority and power in this land and opened very vast ways of livelihood, but you turned out to be highly ungrateful, following Satan (as detailed ahead) instead of your Lord, and falling into every trial he wished to subject you to.

The story of Adam and Iblis and its implications: Now, the story of Adam and Iblis is being narrated, which brings to light the history of Iblis and his companions' enmity toward Adam and his progeny, clarifies its actual cause, and expresses the vow Satan made to avenge himself on the children of Adam until the Day of Resurrection. While reading this story, the purpose for which it is being narrated must not be lost sight of.
Satan's primary enmity toward human beings is based on the jealousy he felt due to the command to honor Adam. He refused to comply with this command with great arrogance, as a result of which he was expelled from Paradise in utter humiliation. Eventually, in his anger, he requested Allah Almighty to grant him respite until the Day of Resurrection so that he might try his tricks on Adam and the children of Adam. Allah Almighty granted his request. After this request was granted, Satan challenged Allah Almighty: "I will exert all my effort to turn them away from the path of monotheism (Tawheed), and I will so bewilder them with my strategies, plots, and propaganda that the majority of them will deviate from Your path of monotheism, and it will be proven that this one, whom You preferred over me, is by no means worthy of any preference."
If you reflect on what lies between the lines here, a few things will become very clearly apparent.
First, Satan's actual grudge against man is: why did God prefer man over him? He asked for respite from God solely to prove this preference wrong. Now, what a misfortune it is for man that in this battle which Satan has waged against him, man becomes the helper of Satan and, through his own actions, bears witness in favor of Satan, proving that the honor God deemed him worthy of was not actually deserved, and that Satan's estimation of him was correct.
Second, man in this world is in a battlefield of trial where he faces a confrontation with Satan at every step. Satan has already obtained respite from God to use all his schemes, deceptions, and tricks against man, and this respite is granted to him until the Day of Resurrection. On the Day of Resurrection, it will be decided who won and who lost.
Third, the primary target of all of Satan's efforts to mislead and deviate is the creed of monotheism (Tawheed). This is the straight path (Sirat-e-Mustaqeem) on which he gave the ultimatum to lie in wait and ambush: "I will not rest until I turn man away from this path, and the majority of humans will deviate from it and become ungrateful to God." Above, the Quraish were drawn to this very reality with the words "qaleelam ma tashkuroon", indicating: "What Satan assumed about human beings, you have proven true word for word through your unworthy conduct. Because of this, you have become deserving of the same evil end which God announced in response to Satan's ultimatum: I will surely fill Hell with you and those who follow you."
Once the coherence of the discourse is clear, there is little need to clarify the individual words and components of the speech. Most of these things have already been discussed with full clarity in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. Repeating them would cause unnecessary length. However, we will clarify those things which were not discussed there.
"Walaqad khalaqnakum thumma sawwarnakum"—The meaning of the word 'Khalq': We have clarified the correct linguistic meaning of 'khalq' in another place, which is to make a design or outline of something. This word is used in the Qur'an both individually and in some places along with its other requirements and accessories such as bara' (creation), taswiyah (fashioning), tarkeeb (composition), and tasweer (shaping). Where it is used alone, it encompasses all its requirements and accessories. But where it appears with its other accessories, as here where 'sawwarnakum' follows 'khalaqnakum', it is used in its original linguistic meaning. Here, the two words 'khalq' and 'tasweer' clarify both the initial and final limits of creation. The initial stage of every creation is that its outline is made, and its final and perfect stage is that it is shaped and its features and details are set right.
Here, the addressees, as we pointed out, are the Quraish, and the description before them is about the creation of the human race and the trials destined for man. Prophet Adam is the father of the entire human race; therefore, his story is not merely his own story, but the story of the entire human race.
"Thumma qulna lil-mala'ikatis-judoo li-Adama fasajadoo illa Iblees"—We have discussed the meaning of prostration (Sajdah), the wisdom behind this command, the reason for the inclusion of the Jinn in this command, and other important issues in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. There we also clarified that Iblis is the title of the Jinn who deceived the father of mankind. He was of the Jinn and became rebellious by disobeying God. Those among the Jinn and humans who follow him become his spiritual progeny. It is for such Jinn and humans that the word Satan is used in the Qur'an. Satan, as we clarified in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, is not an independent species of creation.

A stylistic feature of Arabic grammar: "Qala ma mana'aka alla tasjuda idh amartuka". In another place in the Qur'an, this same point is stated as: "Qala ya Ibleesu ma mana'aka an tasjuda lima khalaqtu biyadayy" (He said, 'O Iblis, what prevented you from prostrating to that which I created with My hands?'). If you reflect on both verses, you will find that in one place 'la' (not) is present after 'ma mana'aka', while in the other place it is not. The reason for this difference is that since the meaning of 'la' is already inherent in 'ma mana'aka' itself, it is not necessary to bring it afterward. However, bringing it adds intensity to the reproach. Thus, in this sentence, the heinousness of Satan's refusal to prostrate is fully highlighted. This style also exists in our own language.
From the phrase "idh amartuka" (when I commanded you), it is deduced that Adam was not worthy of prostration in himself, but became worthy of it due to God's command. Thus, the prostration was not primarily and essentially to him, but was to God Himself, as it was in compliance with His command.
"Qala ana khayrum minhu khalaqtanee min nariy-wa khalaqtahu min teen." This shows that Satan's rebellion was based on the pride that honor and nobility belong to lineage and race, and in this respect, he was superior to man: he was created from fire, whereas Adam was created from clay. The Qur'an guides here that the philosophy of associating honor and nobility with race and lineage is one of Satan's inventions, and wherever it exists, it exists as his legacy. What causes honor and success with Allah is solely obedience to Allah's command; besides this, nothing can become a source of gaining honor with God.

"Qala fahbit minha fama yakoonu laka an tatakabbara feeha..."—Arrogance is Shirk: Deliberate rebellion against God's command is arrogance. Whoever falls into this arrogance sets himself up as greater than God or His equal, which is manifest Shirk (associating partners with God). Majesty belongs only to God, and those who claim a share in it are struck with humiliation by God. There is no place in God's Paradise for the arrogant; Paradise is only for the humble and devout. For this reason, Iblis was ordered to leave, and he was punished with eternal humiliation for his crime of arrogance. Further ahead, it is said about similar arrogant people that just as a camel cannot pass through the eye of a needle, the arrogant cannot enter God's Paradise: "Inn-alladhina kadh-dhaboo bi-ayatina wastakbaroo 'anha la tufattahu lahum abwab-us-sama'i wa la yadkhuloonal-jannata hatta yalijal-jamalu fee sammil-khiyad" (Al-A'raf: 40) (Indeed, those who deny Our verses and are arrogant toward them—the gates of Heaven will not be opened for them, nor will they enter Paradise until a camel enters into the eye of a needle). The exact same thing was stated by Lord Jesus: just as a camel cannot go through the eye of a needle, a rich man cannot enter God's Paradise. The only difference between the two expressions is that the Qur'an referred to the actual crime, which is arrogance, while Lord Jesus referred to the cause of the crime, namely wealth, which generally becomes the cause of arrogance. From this detail, let it be clear that Satan's actual crime was arrogance, because of which he was expelled from Paradise. Therefore, those who join him in this crime will have no place in God's Paradise.

"Qala andhirnee ila yawmi yub'athoon. Qala innaka minal-mundhareen."
The actual battle of trial for man: Since Iblis was ordered to leave Paradise in humiliation, he assumed that no opportunity for effort and action remained for him. Therefore, he requested God to grant him respite to prove that human beings are indeed not worthy of the honor bestowed upon them. Allah Almighty granted him this respite. This is the very turning point where human life enters the battlefield of trial. Satan, as is coming ahead, planned to exert all his strength to prove man unqualified and unworthy, while man's success and happiness was determined in proving that he is indeed worthy of it.
Decision of success and failure on the Day of Resurrection: Since this respite for effort and action is available to man only until death, the opportunity for Satan to tempt and mislead is also only until man's death. After death, just as the door of effort and action is closed for man, the way for Satan to try his strength against him is also blocked. However, the decision of who won and who lost is to be made only on the Day of Resurrection. For this reason, Iblis asked for respite "until the Day they are resurrected", which means that by having this request accepted, he had the decision regarding man's honor and superiority postponed until the Day of Resurrection. Now, it is there that the decision will be made whether man is worthy of this golden crown or not. If he is proven worthy, then for him are the eternal blessings of Paradise; otherwise, just as Satan will be in Hell, man too will make Hell his abode.

"Qala fabima aghwaytanee la-aq'udanna lahum siratakal-mustaqeem."
Satan's challenge to Allah Almighty: After getting his request accepted, Iblis gave this challenge to Allah Almighty. He considered Allah Almighty Himself as his actual adversary; therefore, he gave the challenge to Allah Almighty Himself. Thus, in this battlefield, from Satan's perspective, the actual contest is not between Satan and man, but between God and Satan. From the words "fabima aghwaytanee" (because You have sent me astray), it appears that Satan considered his attitude in the matter of not prostrating to be correct. In his view, his deviation was not self-caused but (God forbid) caused by God. It was as if God put him in a trial from which he could not emerge successful; hence, when he went astray, he was forced into this deviation (God forbid) by God Himself.
'Sirat-e-Mustaqeem' (the straight path) refers to the path of monotheism (Tawheed). Man's nature (Fitrah) is in direct connection with God. If unnatural distortions are not created in the path of nature, man cannot adopt any path other than monotheism. For this reason, monotheism is referred to as the straight path in both the Qur'an and other divine scriptures. As long as man remains on this path, even if he is slow-paced and weary, he remains on the right track and eventually reaches the destination sooner or later. On the contrary, if he turns toward any fork of Shirk, he turns away from the actual destination, and then every step he takes forward is in the path of far-off straying. This is the secret why Satan does not get the opportunity to achieve complete victory over man until he diverts him from the highway of monotheism and puts him onto some path of Shirk. Thus, in his challenge, he stated in clear words that he would sit in wait for man on the path of monotheism and try to deviate him from this path.

"Thumma la-atiyannahum mim bayni aydeehim wa min khalfihim..."
Description of Satan's multi-directional attacks: This describes the strength, extent, and comprehensiveness of Satan's attack in his own words. He will attack man from every direction and aspect; he will try to enter him through his observations, perceptions, feelings, and desires. He will poison his thought, philosophy, knowledge, and intellect. He will mix his poison into his research, criticism, writing, composition, literature, and art. He will create corruption in his culture, civilization, economy, social life, fashion, politics, and religion. This same challenge of Satan is narrated in Surah Bani Isra'il in these words: "Qala ara'aytaka hadhalladhee karramta 'alayya la'in akh-khartani ila yawmil-qiyamati la-ahtanikanna dhurriyyatahoo illa qaleela. Qaladh-hab faman tabi'aka minhum fa-inna jahannama jaza'ukum jaza'am mawfoora. Wastafziz manistata'ta minhum bisawtika wa ajlib 'alaihim bikhaylika wa rajilika wa sharikhum fil-amwali wal-awladi wa'idhum, wa ma ya'iduhumush-shaytanu illa ghuroora. Inna 'ibadee laysa laka 'alaihim sultan, wa kafa birabbika wakeela" (62-65) (He said, 'Do You see this one whom You have honored above me? If You delay me until the Day of Resurrection, I will surely destroy his descendants, except for a few.' God said, 'Go, and whoever of them follows you, then indeed Hell will be your recompense—an ample recompense. And incite whomever you can among them with your voice, and assault them with your cavalry and infantry, and partner with them in wealth and children and promise them.' But Satan does not promise them except delusion. 'Indeed, over My servants you have no authority.' And sufficient is your Lord as a Disposer of affairs). This verse shows both the force of Satan's propaganda and its vastness, and it also indicates that he will use political tactics to implement his plans. However, there is an aspect of consolation in this: Allah Almighty has not granted him the authority over man to strip him of his will and choice. Man's will and choice will remain intact. For this reason, those servants of Allah who resolve to stand firm on the straight path will remain firm on it despite all the clamor of Satan, even if they have to risk their lives for it.
The exact meaning of "wa la tajidu aktharahum shakireen" is: "You will not find the majority of them to be Your monotheists." This is because the actual reality of monotheism is that the servant knows every blessing to be a gift of Allah, from His grace, and remains grateful to Him. If he attributes it to other than Allah, then this is Shirk. This issue has been discussed previously and some of its highly important aspects will appear ahead in this Surah. Have a look at verse 10 above.

"Qalakh-ruj minha madh'oomam madhoora, laman tabi'aka minhum..."
God's decisive judgment: Allah Almighty granted Satan the respite he asked for, but at the same time, He expelled him from Paradise in disgrace and humiliation, because there is no place in Paradise for the rebellious and arrogant. In addition, He clarified the end of those humans and Jinn who would follow him: "I will surely fill Hell with all of you, including him." If you reflect on the words, you will find that just as Satan expressed his resolve to mislead humans and win over their majority with great pomp and force, Allah Almighty answered him with absolute indifference and majesty, making it clear that this decision of God is decisive, with no room for any compromise.

"Wa ya Adamuskun anta wa zawjukal-jannata fakula min haythu shi'tuma wa la taqraba hadhihish-shajarata fatakoona minadh-dhalimeen. (19) Fawaswasa lahumash-shaytanu liyubdiya lahuma ma wooriya 'anhuma min saw'atihi-ma wa qala ma nahakuma rabbukuma 'an hadhihish-shajarati illa an takoona malakayni aw takoona minal-khalideen. (20) Wa qasamahuma innee lakuma laminal-nasireen. (21) Fadallahuma bighuroorin falamma dhaqash-shajarata badat lahuma saw'atuhuma wa tafiqa yakhsifani 'alayhima miw-waraqil-jannah, wa nadahuma rabbuhuma alam anhakuma 'an tilkumash-shajarati wa aqul lakuma innash-shaytana lakuma 'aduwwum mubeen. (22) Qala rabbana dhalamna anfusana wa il-lam taghfir lana wa tarhamna lanakoonanna minal-khasireen. (23) Qalah-bitoo ba'dukum liba'din 'aduww, wa lakum fil-ardi mustaqarruw-wa mata'un ila heen. (24) Qala feeha tahyawna wa feeha tamootoona wa minha tukhrajoon. (25)"
Some implications of the story of Adam and Iblis: This narrates the next part of the story, which clarifies that after expelling Satan from Paradise, Allah Almighty directed Adam and Eve: "Live in Paradise in peace, benefit freely from all its blessings, just take care not to approach this tree, otherwise you will wrong your own souls and be deprived of this Paradise." Satan found a way to attack Adam from here. He misled Adam and Eve, saying: "If there is any tree in this garden from which you should benefit, it is the one from which your Lord has forbidden you. You have been forbidden from it only lest you become angels or your life becomes eternal." Satan swore oaths to assure Adam and Eve of his goodwill, and finally induced them to eat the fruit of that tree. As soon as they tasted its fruit, they were deprived of the state of Paradise and began to sew leaves over themselves to cover themselves. At that time, God called out to them: "Did I not warn you that Satan is your clear enemy? He has openly declared his enmity." Upon this, Adam and Eve took warning. They immediately repented and sought forgiveness, which Allah Almighty accepted. However, at the same time, Adam, Eve, and Iblis were all directed to leave: "Now your abode is the earth; therein you will be tested against one another. Then, whoever proves himself worthy of this Paradise will gain Paradise, and whoever deserves Hell will be cast into Hell."
We have shed light in detail in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah on the realities intended to be discovered through the narration of this story. However, we will clarify here those matters which were not discussed there.
"Wa ya Adamuskun..."—The tree (Shajarah) has been discussed in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. From "fakula min haythu shi'tuma wa la taqraba hadhihish-shajarata", it is deduced that Adam and Eve had the freedom to benefit from everything in the entire Paradise, and they were forbidden from only one tree, but that very tree became a trial for them. Satan delivered such a deceptive speech on the benefits and blessings of this forbidden tree that Adam could not remain firm on Allah's covenant. This same technique of Satan is used against the children of Adam in this world. Everything in this world is permissible for man, and there are only a few numbered things which are forbidden. Satan takes only these things and, through his whispering and that of his agents, makes people believe that all your success and progress is hidden only within these things from which you have been forbidden.

"Fawaswasa lahumash-shaytanu liyubdiya lahuma..."—Although Satan had been expelled from Paradise as an outcast, it has passed above that he had obtained respite to tempt and mislead Adam and his progeny. Because of this respite, it appears, there was no obstacle for him to reach Adam and Eve; hence, he took advantage and reached Adam for whispering.
In "liyubdiya lahuma ma wooriya 'anhuma", the 'Lam' is for consequence (Lam-ul-Aqibah). Satan's effort, as he declared in his challenge, was to involve Adam in ungratefulness and disobedience to God, but since its consequence appeared in the form of Adam and Eve being deprived of the garments of Paradise, it was stated in this manner as if Satan's effort was directed toward this very purpose. This deprivation of the garments of Paradise was an indication that Adam now had to arrange all his needs through his own effort and labor. The divine arrangement that existed for everything until then ended after this disobedience.
"Ma nahakuma rabbukuma 'an hadhihish-shajarati illa an takoona malakayni..."—Iblis tempted Adam that by eating the fruit of this tree, they would either reach the status of angels or obtain eternal life. This shows that despite being honored with the prostration of the angels, Adam considered the status of angels to be higher than his own, and he also knew that the life he possessed was not eternal. If this were not so, Satan would not have succeeded in tempting them in the name of these two things.

"Wa qasamahuma innee lakuma laminal-nasireen." 'Muqasamah' is from the grammatical form of Bab-e-Mufa'alah, which generally comes for the meaning of partnership but sometimes comes only for emphasis and exaggeration. Here, the word 'qasama' is used instead of 'aqsama', which indicates that Satan had to make a great effort to establish his trust, swearing repeatedly to assure them that whatever he was saying was out of goodwill, and no bad intention was involved in it.

"Fadallahuma bighuroor." This is an idiom derived from 'Dala-ad-Dalw'. 'Dalla fulanan bighuroor' means he fell him into the deception he wished to involve him in, succeeded in bringing him to his way, and took him in.
"Falamma dhaqash-shajarata badat lahuma saw'atuhuma wa tafiqa yakhsifani 'alayhima miw-waraqil-jannah"—Tasting the tree means tasting the fruit of the tree. The style of omitting the governing noun (Mudhaf) is well-known in Arabic. 'Khasf' means to patch, weave, or join together. This tree, as passed above, was forbidden for Adam (peace be upon him); hence, the punishment they received for eating its fruit was that they were deprived of the garments of Paradise.
Modesty (covering the private parts) is human nature: Since the primary purpose of clothing is covering the private parts, and sudden deprivation of it falls upon man as a disaster in the form of the feeling of nakedness, the entire picture of the event was described with the words "badat lahuma saw'atuhuma". The style of expression in "wa tafiqa yakhsifani 'alayhima miw-waraqil-jannah" expresses the panic and bewilderment that seized Adam and Eve due to this sudden disaster. As soon as they felt that they had become naked, they immediately worried about their private parts, and tried to cover themselves with whatever they could lay their hands on. Thus, finding nothing, they began to patch and weave the leaves of the garden over themselves. This shows that the feeling of covering the private parts is completely natural in human beings. Those who say that these things are merely products of custom are completely wrong. Just as monotheism is nature, and man adopts Shirk artificially, modesty is nature, and man adopts immodesty artificially. A detailed discussion on this will come in its place.
"Wa nadahuma rabbuhuma alam anhakuma..."—This refers to those warnings which passed above in verses 17 and 19. The nature of Satan's enmity was well clarified to Adam, the end of him and his followers was clarified, and the specific tree from which danger could arise was also pointed out.

"Qala rabbana dhalamna anfusana..."—This is the repentance of Adam and Eve which they made after the aforementioned warning from Allah Almighty. This repentance is also mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah, where we discussed it in detail. Through this repentance, Adam won back the lost game. Regarding Iblis, it has passed above that he remained arrogant despite the warning after disobeying God, but Adam and Eve expressed regret over their mistake, sought forgiveness from God, and it has passed in Surah Al-Baqarah that Allah Almighty accepted their repentance and showed mercy to them. In this way, Adam set an example for his progeny that if man stumbles due to Satan's temptation, the way to escape its consequences is repentance.

"Qalah-bitoo ba'dukum liba'din 'aduww..."—All aspects of this verse have been discussed in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. Although Adam and Eve had repented and their repentance was accepted by Allah Almighty, the divine wisdom required that Adam and Eve leave Paradise and, by living in this world, they and their progeny confront Satan and his progeny. Then, those in this field who win against Satan will be the heirs of Paradise. Thus, the contest remained the same which Iblis had challenged, but the field of contest was made this world instead of Paradise, and Paradise was made the reward for those fortunate ones among the children of Adam who emerge successful against Satan.
The phrase "ba'dukum liba'din 'aduww" was discussed in detail in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. Here, the style of the discourse proves that Adam and Iblis were brought down to this field as two warring parties. Satan has been given respite to win over whoever he can among the children of Adam, and the children of Adam have been given the opportunity that those who wish to obtain the inheritance of Paradise must defeat Satan and win Paradise.
The misconception of the Christians: Here, one must remain mindful of the misconception that occurred to the Christians. They think that man also came down to this world as accursed, like Satan, and to obtain salvation from this, they have fabricated a self-made creed of atonement. The Qur'an, both in Surah Al-Baqarah and here, has given very clear guidance that Adam came to this world completely purified from the consequence of his past mistake after his repentance, and his being sent to this world was so that he and his progeny might prove themselves worthy of the honor Allah Almighty bestowed upon them through their resolve and faith against Satan, which was the cause of Satan's jealousy.

"Qala feeha tahyawna wa feeha tamootoona wa minha tukhrajoon:"
The stages of trial for Adam and the children of Adam: This describes the stages through which Adam and the children of Adam must pass in this world. The meaning is that after passing through all these stages, you will return to Us, and at that time We will tell you what you have lost, what you have gained, and whether you returned from this field of contest successful or failed.

Section 4. Theme of verses 26 to 43:
In these verses, first, those things are recalled about which, in view of Satan's enmity, the children of Adam were warned at the very beginning, and the care of which was the duty of every son of Adam to succeed in the upcoming trial, so that they might remain safe from the disaster that befell their parents at the hands of their eternal enemy. After this, pointing to the Quraish, who are the addressees in this Surah, it is said that they ignored these directions, as a result of which Satan tempted them and had their clothes removed in the same way he had removed the clothes of their parents, Adam and Eve. Yet, in their foolishness, they think that they did not adopt this immodesty in obedience to Satan, but in compliance with God's command, and their only argument for this is that they inherited this method from their ancestors.
After this, reference is made to the spirit and the fundamental principle which is necessarily observed in all divine commands, and which is the intellectual and natural criterion to distinguish between divine commands and satanic innovations. Then, testing them on this criterion, it is shown that none of the satanic innovations which the Quraish are calling God's religion today have anything to do with God. These are things they invented themselves under the guidance of Satan and are attributing to God. At the same time, they are threatened that if they do not change this way, they too will face the same end which their like-minded nations faced. Then, Allah Almighty referred to His promise which He made to Adam while bringing him down to this battlefield of trial, to continue the chain of prophets and messengers among his progeny for guidance, warning them that those who follow these prophets will be safe from the trials of Satan, and those who deny them will fall into Hell after completing their term of life.
After this, a highly effective picture of the states in the Hereafter of both the deniers of the prophets and the followers of the prophets is drawn. Regarding the first group, it is stated that when all the former and the latter gather in Hell, they will argue with one another and curse one another. The descendants will curse the elders whose followings they boast of today, saying: 'They destroyed our way; had they not misled us, we would have been guided.' The leaders will curse the descendants, saying: 'They were themselves ill-fated that they did not adopt the path of guidance; what is our fault in this?' The picture of the second group is drawn showing them sitting happily facing one another in Paradise, with no trace of hatred or reproach anywhere, exchanging gifts of peace and blessings, and every corner echoing with songs of praise to God and acknowledgment of the favors of the prophets. Read the subsequent verses in this light.
"Ya banee Adama qad anzalna 'alaikum libasay-yuwaree saw'atikum wa reesha, wa libas-ut-taqwa dhalika khayr, dhalika min ayati-llahi la'allahum yadh-dhakkaroon." (26)
"Ya banee Adama qad anzalna 'alaikum libasay-yuwaree saw'atikum wa reesha." The word 'reesh' is used for the feathers of birds, and it also refers to the clothing of beauty and adornment.
Clothing is meant for both covering private parts and adornment: The primary purpose of clothing is covering the private parts, but beauty and adornment are also included in its purposes. Whatever nature has made contains various aspects, and all these aspects are according to the requirements of our nature. For covering private parts, a loincloth or a rag was sufficient, but nature, as a completion of favor, arranged for us such clothing which is both a cover for private parts, a protection for us from cold and heat, and an addition to our personality, dignity, beauty, and grace. None of these purposes is bad in itself, though just as in everything, excess or deficiency causes defect, so it does in this. By including adornment in the purposes of clothing, the Qur'an has refuted the monastic concept which considers clothing a defilement and nakedness or semi-nakedness a state of religious sanctity.
The inner clothing is Taqwa (righteousness): "wa libas-ut-taqwa dhalika khayr", meaning along with the physical clothing, an inner clothing has also been granted to man, and that is the clothing of Taqwa, which is far superior to this physical clothing. In fact, it is this clothing of Taqwa that highlights the true utility of the physical clothing; rather, if truth be told, man adopts this physical clothing under the impulse of his inner clothing. If this were not present, man remains naked even after wearing clothes, and his clothing, instead of increasing his dignity, either increases his arrogance or his deformity. This clothing of Taqwa is made of modesty, fear of God, and the sense of servitude, and he on whose stature Allah places this mantle of His grace possesses a dignity and beauty worth seeing. He is a holy angel in the clothing of humans; whoever sees him involuntarily cries out: "Ma hadha bashara, in hadha illa malakun kareem" (This is not a human; this is none other than a noble angel).
Recalling those things which were directed to Adam at the very beginning: "dhalika min ayati-llahi la'allahum yadh-dhakkaroon"—this matter which has been recalled is among those things about which directions were given to the children of Adam at the very time when Adam was sent to this world, and the purpose of referring to it is that the Quraish may take warning that the trial in which Satan involved Adam is the same trial in which he has involved them too. It should be kept in mind that when settling Adam in this world, some of the directions given by Allah Almighty to Adam and his progeny are referred to in the Qur'an. For example, in Surah Al-Baqarah it is said: "Qulnah-bitoo minha jamee'a, fa-imma ya'tiyannakum minnee hudan faman tabi'a hudaya fala khawfun 'alayhim wa la hum yahzanoon" (We said, 'Go down from it, all of you. And when guidance comes to you from Me, whoever follows My guidance—there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve'). This exact theme appears ahead in this Surah in verse 35: "Ya banee Adama imma ya'tiyannakum rusulum-minkum yaqussoona 'alaykum ayatee famanit-taqa wa aslaha fala khawfun 'alayhim wa la hum yahzanoon" (O children of Adam, if there come to you messengers from among you relating to you My verses, then whoever fears Allah and reforms—there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve). In our view, here too, reference is to those very things which were directed to the children of Adam at the very beginning, so that they might protect themselves in their future life from those trials of Satan in which he put Adam and Eve. These very things are now being recalled to the Quraish to warn them that they too are caught in the grip of Satan, and he has played the same trick on them which he played on Adam.
The eloquence of the address "Ya banee Adama": Note also the eloquence of the address "Ya banee Adama" (O children of Adam) here. The events and experiences of a father's life are most instructive for his children. His story is not the tale of someone else, but one's own story. Friendship with the father's friends and enmity with his enemies are kept protected by loyal children like an unforgettable tradition of the family. The descendants remember it and pass it on to those after them, advising them to do the same. Among the Arabs, this tradition was so beloved that even the distinction between truth and falsehood was lost in it; the enemy of the father and ancestors was considered an enemy for generations, even if his enmity was justified. How regretful it is then that the children of Adam should forget this enmity of Satan and his progeny with their father—an enmity that was entirely based on malice and jealousy, which was not hidden but completely open, and which was not specific only to Adam and Eve but to their entire progeny until the Day of Resurrection. Then the matter does not end only at forgetting; rather, the unfaithfulness and wickedness of the children has increased so much that there are many who have made this enemy and his companions their friends, well-wishers, and confidants, and are digging the pits of destruction for themselves at his bidding—the very pits in which he wished to cast Adam, and in which Adam had fallen, had the mercy of Allah not saved him. Praise be to the eloquence of the Qur'an that within the two words of the address "Ya banee Adama", it has preserved all these implications. Whichever honorable and loyal son of Adam hears these reminders of the Qur'an with this address, his every vein beats with the spirit of honor and indignation against Satan. Only the shameless and unfaithful remain unmoved even after this address.

"Ya banee Adama la yaftinannakumu-sh-shaytanu kama akhraja abawaykum minal-jannati yanzi'u 'anhuma libasahuma liyuriyahuma saw'atihuma"—this too, as we pointed out, is among those reminders which were made to the children of Adam at the very beginning. From its style of expression, it helps in understanding the trick Satan adopts to destroy the civilization of the children of Adam and eventually deprive them of God's blessing to cast them into the pit of destruction. That is, through his whispering, he first deprives people of that clothing of Taqwa and fear of God which Allah sent down for the children of Adam as an inner honor along with this physical clothing, and which was mentioned above. When this inner garment is removed, that modesty ends which is the actual motivator of this physical clothing. Then this physical clothing begins to seem like a burden. Immodesty creates a longing to be naked in the private parts, the covering of which is a requirement of nature. Then fashion supports it, and through new designs in the cut of clothing, it creates such styles that the sons of Adam and daughters of Eve remain naked, as far as the basic purpose of clothing—covering private parts—is concerned, even after wearing clothes. Then only the aspect of beauty and adornment remains in clothing, and in that too, the actual purpose is to highlight immodesty from the most attractive angle. Then gradually, the intellect becomes so corrupted that nakedness is named civilization and covering clothing is named barbarism and backwardness. Then educated Satans rise and, in the light of history, formulate the philosophy that man's original nature is nakedness, and clothing was adopted under the constraints of customs and traditions. This is the stage when modesty dies out, and the entire civilization is poisoned with the poison of sensuality. Then this immodest society becomes deserving of nature purifying the earth of their existence and bringing others in their place to see how they act.
"Innahu yarakum huwa wa qabeeluhu min haythu la tarawnahum"—this points to the cunning, plot, and mischief-making of Satan and his group: the paths of their attacks and the guises of their appearance are so numerous that you can neither set a guard on all those paths, nor recognize them in every guise. In his army, there are both Jinn and humans. They will lie in wait from where you cannot see, and they will adopt such guises for you that you will not be able to recognize them; you will consider them friends, advisors, well-wishers, guides, leaders, and what not, and they will cut the roots of your religion and faith. You will assume that they are opening the ways of civilization and progress for you, but they will destroy you where you will find no escape. They will know all the weak veins of your inner self, and they will try to hunt you through their inner whispering and cast their nets over you through their outward allurements. The meaning is: do not consider this enemy an ordinary enemy; remain alert to him at all times.
"Inna ja'alna-sh-shayatina awliya'a lilladhina la yu'minoon"—this tells the strategy to remain safe from the attacks of Satan and his group. That strategy is to stand firm on faith. Faith refers to faith in Allah and the guidance He sent down. This points to the matter which has passed in Surah Al-Baqarah. While bringing Adam down to this world, Allah Almighty told him the sole strategy to remain safe from the attacks of Satan: those who follow My guidance will have no danger: "Qulnah-bitoo minha jamee'a, fa-imma ya'tiyannakum minnee hudan faman tabi'a hudaya fala khawfun 'alayhim wa la hum yahzanoon" (We said, 'Go down from it, all of you. And when guidance comes to you from Me, whoever follows My guidance—there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve'). The meaning of the phrase under discussion is that Allah Almighty gives the opportunity for the Satans to dominate only over those who are deprived of faith in God and His guidance. Their hearts are like an empty house; hence, the Satans camp in them. An empty house is taken by the devil. On the contrary, those whose hearts are inhabited by faith in God and His guidance find no opportunity for the Satans to enter. This same point was stated in another place: "Wa may-ya'shu 'an dhikri-r-Rahmani nuqayyid lahu shaytanan fahuwa lahu qareen" (Al-Zukhruf: 36) (And whoever turns away from the remembrance of the Most Merciful—We appoint for him a devil, and he is to him a companion).

"Wa idha fa'aloo fahishatan qaloo wajadna 'alayha aba'ana wallahu amarana biha, qul innallaha la ya'muru bil-fahsha'i, ataqooloona 'alallahi ma la ta'lamoon."
Satan's trap for the Quraish: After recalling the reminders made to the children of Adam, the state of the Quraish and the Arabs is now described: how Satan deceived them and trapped them in his net, and they have made him their friend, although he has dealt with them in the same way he dealt with their parents, Adam and Eve. He had removed the clothes of Adam and Eve in Paradise; he had the clothes of these sons of Adam and daughters of Eve removed in the sacred sanctuary of Allah, and the tragedy is that they consider this the tradition of their ancestors and the guidance of God, although this is not God's guidance but Satan's trial, and in this way, he wished to have them removed from this pure sanctuary by making them naked, just as he had Adam expelled from Paradise.
"Wa idha fa'aloo fahishatan qaloo wajadna 'alayha aba'ana wallahu amarana biha." 'Fahishah' refers to that open immodesty which everyone except the blind and foolish would call immodesty. Our commentators have taken this here to refer to that innovation which had become prevalent in Arab paganism of performing the circumambulation (Tawaf) of the Kaaba naked. This view of the commentators appears correct, because among the immodesties of Arab paganism, this was the one which, as is clear from the words "wallahu amarana biha", they regarded as a religious command. Although the Quraish themselves kept themselves above this, for others, whether men or women, they had established this nakedness as a form of worship. Their decree was that Arabs from outside the Quraish should not perform the circumambulation of the Kaaba in their own clothes; they should either borrow clothes for this purpose from someone of the Quraish, or perform the circumambulation naked. It was as if the clothes of others were included in the defilements and adornments of the world, which would tarnish the sanctity of this worship. This was a heinous form of sensuality and self-indulgence, countless examples of which are found in the history of temples and churches, and which came into existence entirely through the devilry of their priests and custodians, yet were given a status of religious sanctity. The keepers of shrines have also helped Satan greatly in this matter. The result of this innovation was that a sacred worship like Tawaf became a playground for the gaze and mischief of the wicked, and the stories of gaze-play in the sanctuary were featured in their pagan poetry, reading which one is astonished how Satan entered the sanctuary by adopting a sacred religious guise, and with what success he transformed the greatest worship of Allah into his own worship, yet no one even noticed what had happened. This is one aspect of the reality described above in "Innahu yarakum huwa wa qabeeluhu min haythu la tarawnahum".
"Qul innallaha la ya'muru bil-fahsha'i, ataqooloona 'alallahi ma la ta'lamoon." This is a refutation of the above claim: "You attribute this immodesty and indecency to God's command; God never commands immodesty and indecency." God's commands are according to His attributes and the requirements of human nature. When none of His attributes is such that could tolerate this immodesty, how could He command it? Then, when He created human nature such that from the beginning of consciousness to death, man never likes to be naked in front of others, how could He command this same human to become naked in His very sanctuary before all creation and run around in circles before Him naked! What is the connection between God and His pure attributes, man and his sound nature, and this absurd action? Why do you lay such an absurd charge against God? "Ma la ta'lamoon"—that is, a matter for which you have absolutely no authority or proof.

"Qul amara rabbee bil-qist"—We have discussed the word 'qist' with full detail in the commentary of Surah Al-Imran. Since Allah Almighty is "Qā'imam bil-qist" (establishing justice), whatever command He gave to His servants is entirely based on justice. 'Qist' is a comprehensive reality which is the soul of the entire divine law (Shariah). That is, the exact observation of the point of justice and balance in everything. It is not related to only one aspect of life, but to every aspect: in beliefs, deeds, worship, morals, economy, social life, law, politics—in short, in every field of life, this is the fundamental principle on which the divine law is based. With the change of occasion and place, its expressions will change, but the actual reality will remain in its place. For example, see how "wa aqeemoo wujoohakum 'inda kulli masjidin" was based on this very 'qist' here, because when the Creator and Master is God, it is against 'qist' that the forehead of worship be stained with prostration to anyone else. On this too, "kama bada'akum ta'oodoon" was based, because it is the requirement of 'qist' that when He created, provided the means of sustenance, and distinguished between good and evil, He should also take account and give reward and punishment. If He were not to do so, this would not be 'qist' but injustice. See ahead how on this very 'qist', "khudhoo zeenatakum 'inda kulli masjidin wa kuloo washraboo wa la tusrifoo" was based. That is, it is against 'qist' that for the worship of God, man should abandon clothing or abstain from the pleasures of eating and drinking. 'Qist' is that man should wear clothes, and eat and drink, but should not exceed limits in anything; extravagance is against 'qist'. In short, this 'qist' is such a touchstone that whoever is familiar with the wisdom of religion can test on it and know what is from God and what is not from God.
"Wa aqeemoo wujoohakum 'inda kulli masjidin wad'oohu mukhliseena lahud-deen." 'Qist' is a comprehensive reality, as passed above, and its very first requirement is that no other be associated in the rights of God. Worship is solely the right of God; no other is worthy of worship. For this reason, neither can a mosque be built for other than Allah, nor can an intention of turning toward other than Allah be made in any mosque. Prostration is beautiful only for God; hence, a mosque by its nature is specific only for God, as is said: "Wa annal-masajida lillahi fala tad'oo ma'allahi ahada" (And that the mosques are for Allah, so do not invoke with Allah anyone). This exact same thing was stated in the verse under discussion: "Every mosque is only for Allah. For this reason, turn your faces to Him alone." After "aqeemoo wujoohakum", the words "ila-llahi wahdahu" or their equivalent were omitted, because the succeeding words "wad'oohu mukhliseena lahud-deen" fulfill the right of this omission and also express the reality that the worship of God requires not only the sincerity of worship itself, but also the sincerity of obedience.
"Kama bada'akum ta'oodoon"—this is an emphasis and confirmation of the theme of monotheism. The meaning is: perform worship of God alone and obey Him alone because the God Who created you is the One to Whom you must return, and just as you came alone into this world, you will return to Him alone; none of your assumed partners and intercessors will be with you. Praise be to this eloquence of the Qur'an: there are only two words, and in two words, it has clarified the connection between the Hereafter and monotheism, and also stated a highly clear proof of the Hereafter.

"Fareeqan hada wa fareeqan haqqa 'alayhimu-d-dalalah, innahumu-ttakhadhush-shayatina awliya'a..." The meaning is that the actual reality is what has been described above, and Allah has granted the success to believe in this reality to one group, but deviation has become established upon another group. They have taken the Satans as their friends, and these Satans have trapped them in those very deviations in which Iblis had threatened to trap them. Yet, in their ill-fortune, they assume that they are on the path of guidance. The meaning is that if they do not desist from this ignorance of theirs, they will inevitably face the same end destined for such ill-fated people.

"Ya banee Adama khudhoo zeenatakum 'inda kulli masjidin." The address "Ya banee Adama" is to the Quraish and the Arabs. The eloquence in attributing to Adam is the same as we pointed out above, that it recalls that story of Adam and Satan which is the common story of the entire progeny of Adam, and which teaches every son of Adam that Satan is their eternal enemy, and taking him as a friend and following his bidding is taking the father's enemy as a friend.
In "khudhoo zeenatakum", 'zeenah' (adornment) does not refer to luxurious clothing but to mere clothing. The reason for expressing clothing with the word 'zeenah' here is that the philosophy of adopting nakedness in Tawaf was fabricated on the premise that clothing is included in beauty and adornment, and beauty and adornment are not worthy of this worship. A degree of asceticism and simplicity in Hajj and Ihram has indeed been coming since the time of Abraham, and this is among the characteristics of Hajj, but the Arabs in the era of paganism invented this innovation, along with many others, of stretching the simplicity of Ihram to the limit of nakedness for both men and women. The Qur'an corrected this innovation. It said: "This nakedness is immodesty. Wear your clothes at the time of attending every mosque." Just as no mosque can be for other than Allah, there can be no mosque for which it is made a condition that man must attend it by removing his clothes. By saying "kulli masjidin" (every mosque), this command was made general, leaving no distinction between Haram and non-Haram. This is a complete refutation of that monasticism which establishes nakedness as a source of near-ness to God and reaching God.
"Wakuloo washraboo wa la tusrifoo, innahu la yuhibbul-musrifeen." Just as clothing is not against Taqwa and piety, eating and drinking and benefiting from Allah's blessings are also not against piety. What is against piety and Taqwa is extravagance (Israf). This is because this is against that 'qist' which is, as explained above, the soul of all shariah and all divine commands. Allah Almighty is "Qā'imam bil-qist"; therefore, He loves "muqsiteen"—those who stand firm on justice and moderation—and does not love "musrifeen"—those who exceed the limits of justice and moderation. This lack of balance can be in the form of excess, or in the form of deficiency, and both are against God's preference. He neither likes that man should make eating, drinking, and wearing his sole purpose and remain busy in its activities night and day, nor does He like that he should abandon these things like monks and ascetics. Wastefulness and deprivation are both paths carved out by Satan; God prefers justice and moderation in every aspect of life.
The actual point of moderation: The question of what is the point of justice and moderation and what is the limit of extravagance in these things is left to man's sound intellect and sound nature, because no legal boundary can be set for it. It can vary according to persons and situations. A single standard cannot be fixed for a rich man and a poor man. However, Islam's demand from every rich man is that the wealth he possesses contains the rights of others too; therefore, benefiting from it is permissible for him, but extravagance and wastefulness are not allowed, because Allah Almighty does not love the extravagant. The words "does not love" are not ordinary, because those whom God does not love are inevitably despised by Him.

"Qul man harrama zeenatallahil-latee akhraja li'ibadihi wat-tayyibat..." This question is in the nature of refutation and denial. The meaning is: "At whose bidding have you forbidden the clothing and pure sustenance provided by Allah?" The Provider of these things is God; so where does anyone else get the right to forbid them? The phrase "allatee akhraja li'ibadihi" (which He has brought forth for His servants) is imported here as a proof, meaning: "These things themselves bear witness through their state that the Provider has provided them for the servants to use; so what is the meaning of imposing any undue restriction upon them?" Only their Provider can impose a restriction upon them, and for that, it is necessary that you possess some authority or proof.
"Qul hiya lilladhina amanoo fil-hayatid-dunya khalisatany-yawmal-qiyamah..."—In this portion, that style of omission is observed of which we have explained in more than one place: sometimes opposite words are omitted because what is mentioned itself becomes a proof of what is omitted. Here, the words opposite to "lilladhina amanoo" and "khalisatan" are omitted. If you unfold this omission, the meaning of the verse will be: "All the blessings of Allah belong primarily to the believers in this world too, but Allah has associated the disbelievers with them in it. As for the Hereafter, there they will belong 100% to the believers, and the disbelievers will have no share in them. However, their benefiting from them in the world is not contrary to faith and piety." Those who consider Allah's blessings a trap of Maya and make abandonment of them a condition for near-ness to God are incorrect. The correct view is: benefit from Allah's blessings, remain grateful to Allah, adopt moderation and middle path, and pay the rights of Allah and His servants.
"Kadhalika nufassilul-ayat" is as an expression of favor, and in "liqawminy-ya'lamoon", the verb is in the meaning of the intention of the verb.

"Qul innama harrama rabbiyal-fawahisha..." Now, they are told what things God has actually forbidden, and the style of telling them is adopted in such a way that it automatically follows that the things God has forbidden are not only kept permissible by you, but made into religion, while you are playing the drama of piety by forbidding good, permissible, and pure things.
Among these forbidden things are "fawahish" (immodesties), whether they are open or secret. The clarification of open and secret has passed under Al-An'am verse 151.
After this is "ithm" (sin) and "baghy" (rebellion). The research of both these words has also passed previously. 'Baghy' means transgression and rebellion, namely, transgression and rebellion against God's limits and commands. The condition "bighayril-haqq" (without right) with it does not mean that some 'baghy' can be with right; rather, this highlights the hideousness of 'baghy'—that every 'baghy' is unjust in itself. It is not permissible for anyone to act arrogantly against God and assault His limits. Just as this word is used with the crime of killing prophets, it is used here as well.
"Wa an tushrikoo billahi ma lam yunazzil bihi sultana"—that is, as far as believing in God is concerned, it is an obvious requirement of intellect and nature, and even a polytheist believes in God. As for the matter of God having a partner, it requires a proof, and a proof that is a definitive argument (Burhan), because associating anyone with God's divinity is to upset the entire system of intellect, nature, and justice. Believing in such a major thing without any divine text or rational/natural proof is not justified.
"Wa an taqooloo 'alallahi ma la ta'lamoon." Speaking against Allah refers to fabrications against Allah, i.e., determining halal and haram from one's own desires, creating innovations in pursuit of desires, fabricating shariah according to one's own wishes, and attributing all these things to God is impermissible. Therefore, it is necessary that messengers come from Him and they be followed.

"Wa likulli ummatin ajal, fa-idha ja'a ajaluhum la yasta'khiroona sa'ataw-wa la yastaqdimoon." We have submitted in the introductory discussions of the Surah that this Surah is primarily a Surah of warning. These matters discussed above are merely in the nature of a charge sheet or completion of proof. For this reason, the speech repeatedly returns to its original theme. Just as in verse 29 above, "kama bada'akum ta'oodoon" recalled the Hereafter, this verse is reminding the Quraish of Allah's sunnah: "Despite all your mischief, if you are not being seized, do not think that there will be no seizure at all. This sunnah of Allah will surely be fulfilled upon you, just as it was fulfilled upon all rebellious and transgressing nations, as described in verses 4-9." This respite you received is because Allah has fixed a term for the destruction of every nation. When it is completed, it will neither be delayed by an hour nor advanced.
Different scales of term (Ajal) for individuals and nations: Here, the word 'ajal' is used for this fixed term. It must be remembered that God has kept different scales of 'ajal' in the case of individuals and nations. The scales for individuals are completed by years, months, days, hours, and minutes; when they are completed, the individual ends. The case of nations and communities is different. Their account is based on their spiritual and moral decline. There is a specific limit of moral decline and corruption which is the sign of a nation's vessel being filled. When a nation falls to that limit, its ship sinks. Just as the time of an individual's death is known to Allah alone, so is the knowledge of when a nation has reached that final limit of moral decline such that its remaining on the face of the earth is against divine wisdom. Here, that divine sunnah must be kept in mind which we have clarified in various places: the sending of a messenger to a nation is the final form of completion of proof from Allah Almighty; therefore, when a nation denies its messenger, Allah Almighty inevitably destroys that nation. A detailed discussion on this issue will come, if Allah wills, in the commentary of Surah Yunus.

"Ya banee Adama imma ya'tiyannakum rusulum-minkum yaqussoona 'alaykum ayatee..."
"Ya banee Adama imma ya'tiyannakum rusulum-minkum..."—This is the recalling of the promise which Allah Almighty made for Adam and his progeny at the time when the event of Adam's expulsion from Paradise occurred. In Surah Al-Baqarah, it is mentioned as follows: "Qulnah-bitoo minha jamee'a, fa-imma ya'tiyannakum minnee hudan faman tabi'a hudaya fala khawfun 'alayhim wa la hum yahzanoon" (We said, 'Go down from it, all of you. And when guidance comes to you from Me, whoever follows My guidance—there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve'). Allah Almighty made this promise with Adam and his children to save them from the mischief-making of Satan and his progeny: "If you wish to remain safe from the trials of Satan, follow My messengers." If you reflect on the story of Adam and Iblis narrated above in this Surah, the intellectual and natural necessity of this arrangement is present within it. In the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, we clarified that Allah Almighty refuted the assumption of the angels regarding the progeny of Adam by referring to the prophets and reformers to be born among the children of Adam. This is the promise which Allah Almighty fulfilled by continuing the chain of prophets and messengers in the race of Adam. This same promise is recalled to the Quraish here. The meaning is: Satan has taken you completely in his grip. The only way of safety from his trials is the one told at the very beginning: those who follow the messengers will remain safe from the evil of Satan. So if you want safety from the trials of Satan, follow this Messenger who has come to you reciting Allah's verses.

"Walladhina kadh-dhaboo bi-ayatina wastakbaroo 'anha ula'ika as-habun-nari hum feeha khalidoon."
"Walladhina kadh-dhaboo bi-ayatina wastakbaroo 'anha..."—The preposition 'an' after 'istakbaroo' is a proof that the word 'istakbar' here also comprises the meaning of turning away (A'raadh). The meaning is: Our messengers will come reciting Allah's verses, so those who deny them and turn away from them arrogantly will fall into Hell.

"Faman adhlamu mimman-iftara 'alallahi kadhiban aw kadh-dhaba bi-ayatihi..." In the preceding verses, as we pointed out, there is a reminder. Now, this is stated directly regarding the Quraish: who can be more unjust and ill-fated than those who invent lies against Allah or deny His verses? "Ula'ika yanaluhum naseebuhum minal-kitab"—they will complete the days of life written in the decree in this world, until Our angels will bring death to them and ask them: "Where are those whom you used to invoke besides Allah? Invoke them now!" This is the clarification of that fabrication against Allah which passed above. The reason for calling Shirk a fabrication against Allah has been clarified in another place. The word 'rusul' (messengers) is used for angels in more than one place in the Qur'an. It should also be kept in mind that there is no major difference in the linguistic meaning of 'rasool' and 'malak'. Bringing the word 'rusul' in the plural here is a proof that this word encompasses the Angel of Death and his entire staff. "Qaloo dalloo 'anna"—this is the expression of their confession, and through this confession, they themselves will bear witness to their disbelief with their own tongues. No other witness's testimony will be required.

"Qalad-khuloo fee umamin qad khalat min qablikum minal-jinni wal-insi fin-nar, kullama dakhalat ummatul-la'anat ukhtaha, hatta idhad-darahoo feeha jamee'an qalat ukhrahum li-oolahum rabbana ha'ula'i adalloona fa-atihim 'adhaban di'fam-minan-nar, qala likullin di'fuw-walakil-la ta'lamoon." (38)
"Qalad-khuloo fee umamin..."—This describes the end that will come before them. When the subject of the verb 'qala' (He said) is omitted in such situations, it indicates disregard and indifference.
Two vessels are mentioned here: "fee umamin" (among nations) and "fin-nar" (in the Fire). The first is intended to express who their companions will be: "Go, join those Jinn and humans who went astray before you by following Satan." The second tells their abode: "The resting place of all of you will be Hell." Thus, the companions are the worst, and the place is the worst.
"Kullama dakhalat ummatul-la'anat ukhtaha, hatta idhad-darahoo feeha..."—'Iddarahoo' is originally 'tadarahoo'. In Arabic, such changes in the form of words sometimes occur. 'Tadarahal-qawm' means the latter of the nation joined the former. In "qalat ukhrahum li-oolahum", the preposition 'li' is in the meaning of 'fee' (about). Examples of this have passed previously. That is, the latter will say about their former.
'Di'f' means double, and when a context is present, it is also used for more than that.
Description of the mutual cursing among the people of Hell: This is a picture of the mutual fighting that will take place among them after they gather in the enclosure of Hell. In the world, they remained followers and leaders, companions and helpers of one another. They sang praises of one another and lifted their banners. They presented salutes and addresses. But there in Hell, upon facing one another, every group will rain curses upon the other. The followers will say to the leaders: "Curse be upon you; you ruined our affair." The leaders will say: "You were yourselves ill-fated that you followed our path; what is our fault in this?"
This situation is described for the very first moment when they face one another. After this, when all gather, the latter will petition God against their former: "O Lord, we went astray at their hands; therefore, give them a double punishment." It will be said: "For each of you is double, but you do not know." This is for both the former and the latter, but you do not know.
It must be kept in mind here that whether it is good or evil, both are contagious things by nature. They do not remain limited to the person of their performer; rather, their effects are transferred to others, so much so that through inheritance, a particle of good can become like a mountain, and a seed of evil can take the form of a vast forest. This same principle is stated in the Hadith in which the Prophet pointed out the extent and increase of the consequences of deeds of those who establish a good or bad precedent. His statement is: "Whatever unjust killing takes place in the world, a portion of its sin is gathered in the account of Adam's son Qabil, who was the first to establish the way of unjust killing." In the portion under discussion, this reality is pointed out: "Both the former and the latter among you are going to receive, along with your own sins, a share of the sins of those for whom you established the precedent. You are crying out that your former established a bad precedent for you, so they should have a greater punishment. They will indeed receive a greater punishment, but how will you escape the consequences of the bad precedent you established or left for those after you? The scale that is for them is the scale for you. If, along with their bad way, you also had knowledge and estimation of the effects of your own bad way, you would admit that you and they are equal criminals. But you do not know the correct estimate of the horrors of the crop of evil you sowed. Now it will come before you."

"Wa qalat oolahum li-ukhrahum fama kana lakum 'alayna min fadlin fadhooqul-'adhaba bima kuntum taksiboon."
This will be the answer of the former to the latter: "If we established a bad precedent for you, what good precedent did you establish for others? The point would be valid if you had established some good precedent.
