In that case, you were indeed worthy of preference and superiority over us, but when you too have come performing the same deeds we performed, what is the difference between us and you? Just as we taste the fruit of what we did, you too taste the fruit of what you did."

"Inn-alladhina kadh-dhaboo bi-ayatina wastakbaroo 'anha la tufattahu lahum abwab-us-sama'i wa la yadkhuloonal-jannata hatta yalijal-jamalu fee sammil-khiyad, wa kadhalika najzil-mureemin."
We have clarified the benefit of the preposition 'an' with the word 'istakbar' above.
"La tufattahu lahum abwab-us-sama'i" can have two meanings. First, that the gates of the Heaven of Paradise will not be opened for them. Second, that unlike the believers, they will not be welcomed in Heaven; rather, they will be driven like criminals and ruffians to see their evil end in utter humiliation and disgrace. The rule is that for those whose honor is intended, the gates are opened with great care; those welcoming them stand on both sides of the gate, welcoming them with slogans of welcome, well done, peace, and greetings. On the contrary, when humiliation is intended, they are thrust like prisoners through the window of the prison gate. Thus, "la tufattahu" does not mean the denial of the act itself, but the denial of its necessary concomitant, examples of which are numerous in Arabic literature.
"La yadkhuloonal-jannata hatta yalijal-jamalu fee sammil-khiyad." This is a style of expression called linking to the impossible (Ta'leeq bil-Muhal). The meaning is that just as it is impossible for a camel to enter the eye of a needle, it is impossible for these arrogant people to enter Paradise. This style of expression is also used in ancient scriptures. In the Gospel it is said: "And Jesus said to his disciples, 'Truly, I say to you, only with difficulty will a rich person enter the kingdom of heaven. And again I tell you, it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God'" (Matthew 19:23, 24). The only difference between the expression of the Qur'an and the Gospel is that Lord Jesus referred to the cause of arrogance, namely wealth, while the Qur'an referred to the actual crime, namely arrogance. We have clarified the reality of arrogance in the story of Adam and Iblis above, and also clarified that Iblis's expulsion from Paradise was caused by arrogance; therefore, those who possess even a trace of arrogance will not smell the fragrance of Paradise. Lord Jesus said: "Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven."

"Lahum min jahannama mihaduw-wa min fawqihim ghawash, wa kadhalika najzid-dhalimeen."
'Mihad' means bedding, and 'ghawash' is the plural of 'ghashiyah', which means that which covers. Here, it refers to a blanket/cover. The meaning is that for them, from above and below, Hell itself will be their bedding and cover.

"Walladhina amanoo wa 'amilus-salihati la nukallifu nafsan illa wus'aha ula'ika as-habul-jannati hum feeha khalidoon."
Now, on the principle of comparison, the state of the believers is described: how they will be happy and content in Paradise, pleased and satisfied with one another, and full of gratitude to Allah for the success of guidance and the leading of the Messengers. In the verse, the phrase "la nukallifu nafsan illa wus'aha" is as a parenthetical sentence, which provides reassurance that Allah has not placed any responsibility upon His servants beyond their capacity. Let no one think that this is such a burden that cannot be lifted. This responsibility is only to the extent that is within the capacity of the servants.

"Wa naza'na ma fee sudoorihim min ghillin tajree min tahtihimul-anhar, wa qaloona-l-hamdu lillahil-ladhee hadana lihadha wa ma kunna linahtadiya lawla an hadanallah, laqad ja'at rusulu rabbina bil-haqq, wa noodoo an tilkumul-jannatu oorithtumooha bima kuntum ta'maloon."
A specific stylistic feature of the language: "Wa naza'na ma fee sudoorihim min ghillin"—in this portion, that style of language is observed of which we have explained in more than one place: sometimes a word is used, and what is intended is its necessary concomitant. For example, it is said: "Fal-yawma nansahum kama nasoo liqa'a yawmihim hadha" (So today We will forget them just as they forgot the meeting of this Day of theirs). Obviously, 'forgetting' here refers to its necessary concomitant, namely, disregard; otherwise, what is the meaning of Allah forgetting something? He never forgets anything. Similarly, He said: "Fadh-kuroonee adhkurkum" (So remember Me, I will remember you)—I will remember you, meaning My eye of favor will remain upon you, I will help you at every step, fulfill every promise I made to you, and bless you with My eternal grace.
On this same style, "Wa naza'na ma fee sudoorihim min ghillin" refers here to its necessary concomitant, which is that the believers will meet one another in Paradise with warmth and love, welcome one another, sit facing one another, and exchange love and affection, with no trace of grudge or malice between them. This same theme is expressed in other places with the words "'ala sururim-mutaqabileen" (on thrones facing one another). That is, they will sit on thrones facing one another because their hearts will be completely clean from one another. Sitting with faces turned away is a proof of mutual grudge, and sitting eye to eye is a proof of mutual trust and love. The Qur'an has expressed this state of the people of Paradise here with "Wa naza'na ma fee sudoorihim min ghillin", and this is described exactly in contrast to that state of the people of Hell described above. There, curses will be exchanged and fights will take place, while here, the assembly will be fragrant with the sweetness of love and affection.
The spirit of gratitude of the people of Paradise upon reaching the destination: "Wa qaloona-l-hamdu lillahil-ladhee hadana lihadha"—this is the gratitude which a traveler, reaching the destination after a long and arduous journey, expresses. In "hadana lihadha", there is an indication of that success of guidance which is granted to the seekers of truth at every step, and of that leading which Allah Almighty provided through His prophets and messengers. In "wa ma kunna linahtadiya lawla an hadanallah", there is an indication of the arduousness of this journey, and also of the fact that the success the people of Paradise will achieve will be so much greater than their expectations, estimations, and hopes that they will consider it entirely the grace of God, with no thought of their own effort or strategy in it.
"Laqad ja'at rusulu rabbina bil-haqq." After the gratitude to Allah, this is an acknowledgment of the great services of the prophets and messengers and the truth of every word they said. That is, the people of Paradise, upon seeing every news given by the messengers in the form of reality, will cry out: "Whatever they said proved to be true, word for word." In this, there is a warning for those whose denial and arrogance were mentioned above.
"Wa noodoo an tilkumul-jannatu oorithtumooha bima kuntum ta'maloon." This will be a general announcement from Allah Almighty to the people of Paradise: "This Paradise is the one you have been made to inherit as a reward for your deeds." The feeling of the people of Paradise themselves has been narrated above that they will consider this Paradise as the fruit of God's grace and favor rather than their own effort and deeds, but Allah Almighty will declare it the fruit of their effort and deeds. This is the pinnacle of the completion of favor. This verse has raised the status of the servants' deeds so high that a higher status cannot even be conceived. Whatever we gain in the world, we gain through God's grace alone. In the Hereafter too, we will gain through God's grace alone, but the Generous Lord will declare it our right and the fruit of our labor. Who can estimate this eternal kingdom about which everyone will have the consciousness that he made it with his own efforts, and that it is everlasting! Man not only wants to obtain blessings, but also wants those blessings to be his own. Without this feeling, he cannot enjoy any blessing properly. Allah Almighty will fulfill this requirement of his nature in Paradise too.
A subtle allusion: The use of the word 'inheritance' (Wiraathah) in this verse contains a subtle allusion to the story of Adam's expulsion from Paradise mentioned above. There, the direction is present that only those among the children of Adam will inherit their father's Paradise who stand firm on the straight path of faith and good deeds despite all the trials of Satan. Here, by using the word "oorithtumooha", it is as if congratulations are offered: "Indeed you won the game, and now you are worthy of being made the heirs of this Paradise."

Section 6. Theme of verses 44 to 53: In these verses, the questioning and answering that will take place between the people of Paradise and the people of Hell is mentioned. The people of Paradise will ask the people of Hell: 'We found the promises Our Lord made to us to be true; tell us, did you also see with your eyes what you were informed of?' They will answer: 'Yes, we saw it.' After this, an announcer will announce Allah's curse upon them.
Then, it is described that from the heights of Al-A'raf, a group of prominent men of the community will be shown the states of Paradise and Hell, so that they may see how the promises of Allah for which they lived and died were fulfilled. These companions of Al-A'raf (As-habul-A'raf) will recognize both the people of Paradise and the people of Hell by their distinguishing marks. They will congratulate the people of Paradise on their success and reproach the leaders of the people of Hell, saying: 'Tell us, of what use was your assembly and all your pride? Where are those about whom you used to swear that they would never receive any mercy, and where are you burning?'
After this, it is described that the people of Hell will be crying out for water and pleading with the people of Paradise: 'Look toward us too,' but they will answer: 'Allah has forbidden these things for the disbelievers.'
At the end, warning is given that this state of Paradise and Hell being narrated is not empty words. It is a great favor of Allah that He sent down a Book to inform them, detailing in the light of His definitive knowledge whatever is to come, so that those who wish to believe may adopt this guidance and make themselves deserving of mercy. But they, because of their arrogance, are waiting for these things to appear in the form of events; but that time will not be for believing, but for beating their heads in regret. Read the subsequent verses in this light. It is said:
"Wa nada as-habul-jannati as-haban-nari an qad wajadna ma wa'adana rabbuna haqqan fahal wajadtum-ma wa'ada rabbukum haqqan, qaloo na'am, fa-adh-dhana mu'adh-dhinum-baynahum al-la'natullahi 'aladh-dhalimeen."
The pinnacle of human faculties in Paradise: This verse gives a very slight conception of the transformation that will take place in human faculties and abilities upon reaching Paradise. In this world, our faculties of hearing, sight, perception, and communication are highly limited. Ordinary things become obstacles in the way of these faculties of ours. But in the Hereafter, these obstacles will be removed. From the world of Paradise, whenever they wish, the people of Paradise will address the people of Hell and converse with them.
For the man of this scientific age, this should not be surprising at all. When today man, by discovering only a few subordinate laws of nature, has invented such binoculars with which he can see the flame of a candle burning at a distance of thousands of miles, made such phones through which the President of Pakistan can speak to the President of America whenever he wishes, made such televisions on which people of one country can see and hear a speaker addressing a gathering in a distant country, and made such instruments which can inform him of the pulse and heartbeat from a distance of millions of miles, what is the reason to be surprised at the matters of that world where all these natural laws which bind us today will change, and new heavens and earth will be created in place of these heavens and earth!
The exposure of the people of Hell: This question of the people of Paradise to the people of Hell, which is narrated here, cannot obviously be for the purpose of conveyance of message and warning, because its time would have passed; its purpose is merely the exposure of the people of Hell. In response to it, the confession of the people of Hell will be like the final confession of a criminal, after which nothing remains between him and his punishment. Thus, an announcer will announce Allah's curse upon these wrongdoers, and this announcement will be equivalent to the opening of the chapter of punishment.

"Alladhina yasuddoona 'an sabeelillahi wa yabghoonaha 'iwajan wa hum bil-akhirati kafiroon."
Generally, people have considered this portion to be part of the announcer's announcement, but in my view, the announcer's announcement ends at the word 'dhalimeen'. After this, this portion is as a parenthetical clause which Allah Almighty attached to it as a clarification, so that the speech does not remain merely a tale of the future but applies fully to the present as well. Through this clarification, it is explained who the 'dhalimeen' (wrongdoers) are. It is said: "Those who are blocking people from Allah's path today, striving to create crookedness in it, and denying the Hereafter." Through this clarification, the announcement to take place in the Hereafter was applied exactly to the Quraish of the time. There are many such parenthetical clauses in the Qur'an; examples have passed previously, and highly eloquent examples will come ahead. An example of this is also coming ahead in verse 51. It is through these parenthetical clauses that general principles, future events, or past stories adopt the garb of the present. For this reason, special attention must be paid to them; otherwise, the coherence of the speech is disrupted, and one has to resort to such forced interpretations in explanation which are rejected by both taste and the rules of grammar. Thus, here too, those who did not consider it a parenthetical clause had to resort to forced interpretation in the portion "wa hum bil-akhirati kafiroon", taking it in the meaning of "wa hum bil-akhirati kanoo kafireen", which is a kind of addition in the Qur'an.
In "alladhina yasuddoon", the word 'sadd' is used in both intransitive and transitive meanings, i.e., turning away and blocking. Translation of such words presents difficulty. In my view, the cautious method regarding such words is that if the context is clear, the translation should be according to the context; otherwise, the transitive meaning is preferable, because the intransitive meaning is inherent in the transitive anyway.
In "alladhina yabghoonaha 'iwaja", 'iwaj' refers to all those deviations and crooked thoughts which man adopts by turning away from God's path, but in the light of the parallels of the Qur'an, in my view, its meaning is to create crookedness in God's straight path—namely, monotheism—to carve out paths of Shirk. The clarification of these parallels will come in detail, if Allah wills, in a suitable place.

"Wa baynahuma hijab, wa 'alal-A'rafi rijaluny-ya'rifoona kullam-biseemahum, wa nadaw as-haba-l-jannati an salamun 'alaykum, lam yadkhulooha wa hum yatma'oon." (46)
'Hijab' refers to the wall between Paradise and Hell: "Wa baynahuma hijab, wa 'alal-A'rafi rijaluny-ya'rifoona kullam-biseemahum." 'Hijab' refers, as is clear from another place in the Qur'an, to the wall that will be set up between Hell and Paradise. In Surah Al-Hadid it is said: "Faduriba baynahum bisoor" (Al-Hadid: 14) (And a wall will be placed between them). Who can estimate the length and width of a wall that will act as a barrier between the entire world of Paradise and the entire world of Hell, when the likeness of the vastness of Paradise alone is described by the Qur'an as the vastness of the heavens and the earth!
The meaning of Al-A'raf: 'A'raf' is the plural of 'Urf'. 'Urf' refers to the crest on a horse's forehead and the comb of a rooster. From here, this word was used for any minaret, tower, or watchtower built on a high wall or mountain, from where all directions can be observed at a single glance. It is clear from the style of the Qur'an that the wall to be set up between Paradise and Hell will have these 'A'raf' (towers/minarets) on it, from where all the scenes of Paradise and Hell can be observed.
Meaning of 'Rijal': The word 'rijal' (men) is used in its general meaning, but those who possess a taste of Arabic know that it generally refers to prominent and distinguished persons. For example: "Rijalul-la tulheehim tijaratuw-wa la bay'un 'an dhikrillah" (Al-Nur: 37) (Men whom neither commerce nor sale distracts from the remembrance of Allah); "Minal-mu'mineena rijalun sadaqoo ma 'ahadullaha 'alayh" (Al-Ahzab: 23) (Among the believers are men who have proven true to what they pledged to Allah). Here too, in verse 48, this word is used for the leaders of disbelief: "Wa nada as-habul-A'rafi rijalany-ya'rifoonahum biseemahum qaloo ma aghna 'ankum jam'ukum wa ma kuntum tastakbiroon."
Regarding the word 'kullan': We have stated in another place that when the word 'kull' (all/each) appears after the mention of groups or persons in this manner, it becomes definite in meaning. That is, it refers to those very groups or persons mentioned above. Here, the people of Paradise and the people of Hell were mentioned above; so it refers to both those groups as groups.
The people of Paradise and the people of Hell will be distinguished by their prominent marks: 'Seema' means mark or sign. For example: "Seemahum fee wujoohihim min athari-s-sajood". Both the Qur'an and Hadith contain indications that on the Day of Resurrection, the people of faith and the people of disbelief will be distinguished by the effects of their deeds. In Sahih Muslim, there is a Hadith that people asked the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): 'How will you recognize those of your Ummah who come after you?' He said: 'If a man has horses with white patches on their foreheads and legs among other horses, would he not recognize them?' They said: 'Yes, O Messenger of Allah.' He said: 'Similarly, the people of my Ummah will appear on the Day of Resurrection with their faces, hands, and feet shining from the effects of Wudu.' Regarding the wife of Abu Lahab, it is mentioned in the Qur'an itself that on the Day of Resurrection, she will have a rope around her neck like the rope which wood-gathering maids place around their necks to gather wood. Some indications of this nature are also present in the Ahadith related to Al-Mi'raj (the Ascension). In short, it is clear that whether they are the people of faith or the people of disbelief, both groups will be distinguished by their prominent marks in their respective places, and the companions of Al-A'raf will recognize the truthful, martyrs, and righteous of the people of Paradise through these marks, and also the leaders and corrupt of the people of Hell.
After the explanation of the components of the speech, the only question worthy of consideration remaining is: who will be the companions of Al-A'raf? Ibn Jarir has narrated four views in response to this question. First, that they will be those whose good and bad deeds are equal on the scale; hence, their decision will be pending as to whether they are sent to Hell or Paradise. Second, that they will be the group of scholars and jurists. Third, that they will be those who participated in Jihad without the permission of their parents. Fourth, that they will be angels. Of these, the latter two views are completely weak, with no support in the Qur'an; so they require no discussion.
The first view, though highly famous (so much so that the Persian hemistich 'Ask the people of Hell that Al-A'raf is Paradise' has gained the status of a proverb in our literature), is also weak from several aspects. First, the word 'rijal' is used for them here. This word, as we pointed out above, when it appears in this manner, refers to prominent persons. The question is: those whose state is described as having good deeds that are not even heavy enough to outweigh their bad deeds—what is their prominent quality because of which they are referred to with this word?
Second, those whose good and bad deeds are equal do not necessarily have to be only men; they could be women too. So why was the word 'rijal' used for them, and not some comprehensive word like 'ta'ifah' (group) or 'ummah' (community)?
Third, not a single word here indicates that this is a description of a group whose matter is pending, nor does it indicate what the purpose of showing them this sight of Al-A'raf is, although the occasion requires this to be clear.
Fourth, the manner in which these people will address the people of Paradise and the people of Hell, the things they will say, and the honor shown to them is contrary to their being a group whose own salvation is pending and whose performance is such that their good and bad deeds are equal. It is clear from the description of the Qur'an that these people will congratulate the people of Paradise, reproach the leaders of the people of Hell, saying: 'You used to boast and act arrogantly in the world; tell us, where is your assembly and all your wealth now?' They will rebuke them: 'You considered yourselves the sole monopolists of all God's blessings, and did not consider poor Muslims worthy of any favor; now look where you are and where they are!' At the end, they will give the people of Paradise the glad tidings of stability and eternity. Reflect on how all these things can come from the tongues of those whose own salvation is pending, and whether such statements are morally beautiful from those whose own performance is not very significant.
For these reasons, in our view, this view possesses no importance despite its fame. The correct view in our estimation is the second one. Ibn Jarir has attributed this view to Mujahid, whose status in Tafsir is well-known. By scholars and righteous, Mujahid obviously meant those who were the flagbearers of truth, invitees to good, and preventers of evil in the struggle between truth and falsehood in the world; those who bore the wounds of the people of falsehood in support of truth, and stood as shields in defense of the oppressed. Such scholars and jurists, or in other words, the prominent men of the community, are indeed worthy of this honor on the Day of Resurrection that Allah Almighty show them the sight of both Paradise and Hell from the heights of Al-A'raf, so that they may see the final end of both truth and falsehood with their own eyes, congratulate the companions of truth with their tongues, and reproach the enemies of truth.
"Wa nadaw as-haba-l-jannati an salamun 'alaykum, lam yadkhulooha wa hum yatma'oon." These people will first send the message of peace and congratulations to the people of Paradise from the heights of Al-A'raf. From "lam yadkhulooha wa hum yatma'oon", it is clear that they will be shown this sight before being sent to Paradise, so that they may see the truth of Allah's promise with their own eyes before entering Paradise. In "wa hum yatma'oon" (while they hope), their humility shines through. Despite all this honor indicating their status with Allah, they, out of their humility, will consider themselves only as hopefuls of mercy. Thus, the words are used exactly according to their mental state. It should be kept in mind that those who know the majesty of Allah never raise themselves above the level of hope and desire; it is the trait of the narrow-minded to get carried away with a little. Prophet Abraham, the Friend of Allah, says: "Walladhee atma'u ay-yaghfira lee khatee'atee yawmad-deen" (Al-Shu'ara: 82) (And Who I hope will forgive my mistake on the Day of Recompense). Our Prophet once said: 'No one will enter Paradise by his deeds alone.' People asked: 'Even you, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'Yes, even I, unless Allah covers me with His mercy.'

"Wa idha surifat absaruhum tilqa'a as-habin-nari qaloo rabbana la taj'alna ma'al-qawmidh-dhalimeen." This style of expression is a proof of honor and respect. First, they will observe the successes of the people of Paradise and become completely absorbed in its observation. Then, their attention will be drawn toward the people of Hell: 'Have a look at the end of the enemies of truth too.' As soon as their eyes fall upon them, the prayer of seeking refuge will come from their tongues: "Rabbana la taj'alna ma'al-qawmidh-dhalimeen" (Our Lord, do not place us with the wrongdoing people). Just as above "wa hum yatma'oon" reflected their humility, this prayer is a proof of their perfect fear of God and of the horror of the scene of Hell.

"Wa nada as-habul-A'rafi rijalany-ya'rifoonahum biseemahum qaloo ma aghna 'ankum jam'ukum wa ma kuntum tastakbiroon."
The address of the companions of Al-A'raf to the leaders of the disbelievers: This address of the companions of Al-A'raf, as is clear from the words, will be to the leaders of the people of Hell, because pride in assembly and wealth belonged to them. In Hell, these people will be distinguished by their prominent marks; so the companions of Al-A'raf will recognize that this is Abu Lahab, this is Abu Jahl, this is so-and-so, and this is so-and-so, and will address them with this question as an exposure. The pride of the leaders of disbelief of every era in their wealth, resources, and numbers is mentioned in detail in the Qur'an. We have taken 'ma' here in the interrogative meaning. It contains more force and is supported by the parallels of the Qur'an. Furthermore, the succeeding sentence, which is related to it, is clearly interrogative. If it is taken in a different meaning from the succeeding sentence, harmony will not remain in the speech.

"Aha'ula'illadhina aqsamtum la yanaluhumullahu birahmatin, udkhulul-jannata la khawfun 'alaykum wa la antum tahzanoon."
"Aha'ula'illadhina aqsamtum la yanaluhumullahu birahmatin." This question will be asked by the companions of Al-A'raf to the leaders of disbelief, pointing toward the inhabitants of Paradise: 'Tell us, are these not the ones about whom you used to swear that they would never receive any favor from God?' It is mentioned in the Qur'an that the chiefs of Quraish used to present this as a major argument against Islam: 'If there was any good in it, would its followers be these poor, starving, and enslaved people? All the blessings of God were bestowed upon us; so why would their selection be made for it?' The companions of Al-A'raf will ask them keeping this pride of theirs in view: 'Tell us, those whom you did not consider worthy of any favor and mercy—where are they and where are you?'
"Udkhulul-jannata la khawfun 'alaykum wa la antum tahzanoon." The above question will be asked by the companions of Al-A'raf addressing the leaders of disbelief, and this statement will be made by them addressing the people of Paradise as a congratulation, which will also expose those who insulted them.
The verb is in the meaning of stability and continuity: Here, someone might have this question in mind: the people of Paradise will already be settled in Paradise, so what is the meaning of addressing them with "udkhulul-jannata" (enter Paradise)? Probably to escape this question, commentators have resorted to great difficulty in determining the addressee and speaker of this sentence. In our view, they had to resort to all this difficulty because they did not keep in mind this style of Arabic: the verb in Arabic does not always point only to its initial meaning; in some situations, it points to stability and continuity. For example, see this same "udkhuloo" used in Surah Yusuf, which is not in its initial meaning but in the meaning of congratulation, welcome, stability, and continuity: "Falamma dakhaloo 'ala Yusufa awa ilaihi abawayhi wa qala-d-khuloo Misra in sha'allahu amineen" (Al-Yusuf: 99) (And when they entered upon Joseph, he took his parents to himself and said, 'Enter Egypt, if Allah wills, in safety'). This is the mention of the occasion when all the brothers of Joseph, according to his direction, presented themselves before Joseph with their parents, and Prophet Joseph welcomed them in his court. Obviously, there was no question of their entering Egypt at that time; they had already entered Egypt. At that time, Joseph's saying "Enter Egypt in safety" can only be in the meaning of congratulation, welcome, and glad tidings of stability. In our view, the statement of the companions of Al-A'raf to the people of Paradise "udkhulul-jannata la khawfun 'alaykum yawma wa la antum tahzanoon" is in this exact same meaning. That is: 'Those obsessed with the pride of wealth and status considered you highly contemptible and humiliated, but Allah, despite them, blessed you with the honor of Paradise; remain honored in it, now there is neither any fear for you nor any grief.'

"Wa nada as-habun-nari as-haba-l-jannati an afeedoo 'alayna minal-ma'i aw mimma razaqakumullah, qaloo innallaha harramahuma 'alal-kafireen."
A specific meaning of the word 'Tahreem' (forbidding): The forbidding mentioned in "harramahuma" is not in the meaning of legal (shari'i) forbidding, but in the meaning of: "Fa-innaha muharramatun 'alayhim arba'eena sanah" (Al-Ma'idah: 26) (For indeed, it is forbidden to them for forty years). That is, Allah Almighty has definitively deprived them of this land for forty years. Obviously, when Allah Almighty definitively deprives someone of something, that thing can neither reach him nor can he find it. From the answer of the people of Paradise, it appears that they have no excuse in fulfilling the request, as they have an abundance of every blessing, but Allah Almighty has deprived the people of Hell of these blessings; so neither can these reach them, nor can they benefit from them.

"Alladhina-ttakhadhoo deenahum lahwa-wa la'ibaw-wa gharrathumul-hayatud-dunya, fal-yawma nansahum kama nasoo liqa'a yawmihim hadha wa ma kanoo bi-ayatina yajhadoon."
A highly appropriate parenthetical clause: This verse is as a parenthetical clause. In it, Allah Almighty clarified on His own the answer of the people of Paradise "harramahuma 'alal-kafireen", explaining who the disbelievers are. Through this parenthetical clause, the speech became exactly matching the situation. It was made clear to the Quraish that they should not think that this is merely a tale of others, but it is their tale too. An example of this kind of parenthetical clause passed above in verse 45.
"Alladhina-ttakhadhoo deenahum lahwa-wa la'iba"—that is, what Allah sent down to them as religion, they dismissed in jest and mockery. Everything has a place and status. Religion comes to determine the correct perspective in every aspect of life so that people, instead of falling into the pit of destruction, adopt the path of success. But those who consider life a playground of children become so blind to their desires that they treat the most serious reality contrary to their desires as a joke and try to dismiss it as a joke. Playing games with the father's beard.
"Wa gharrathumul-hayatud-dunya"—this explains the reason for this careless behavior: why they remained blind to the highly serious realities of life. It is said: "The worldly life deceived them." They saw that they were eating, drinking, enjoying, boasting, and no questioning was taking place; so they assumed that the world was created for this alone. If some servant of Allah drew their attention to the fact that after this, a day of accounting is also to come, they attacked him: 'This is a madman and eccentric, ruining our freedom and our enjoyment.'
"Fal-yawma nansahum kama nasoo liqa'a yawmihim hadha"—here, 'nansahum' (We forget them) is used in the meaning of 'We disregard them', and this use of the verb is on the style of which we have explained in another place: the use is apparently a verb, but what is intended is its necessary concomitant. Allah Almighty does not forget anything. This forgetting is an expression of disregarding.
It is clear from the parallels of the Qur'an that 'liqa' (meeting) is sometimes annexed to its object, and sometimes to its vessel, but the purpose in both forms is the same. That is, meeting with the Lord in the Hereafter. They forgot the meeting of this Day of theirs, meaning they forgot the meeting of their Lord on this Day.
"Wa ma kanoo bi-ayatina yajhadoon"—since this sentence is conjoined to the preceding sentence, it is actually in the meaning of "kama kanoo". Within 'kama', along with likeness and comparison, the meaning of causality is also found; so if its translation is made as: "and because they used to deny Our verses", this translation will be completely correct.

"Walaqad ji'nahum bikitabin fassalnahu 'ala 'ilmin hudaw-wa rahmatal-liqawminy-yu'minoon."
"Walaqad ji'nahum bikitabin fassalnahu 'ala 'ilmin"—The pronoun 'hum' (them) refers to the Quraish. This is Allah Almighty's expression of His great favor upon them in the form of the Qur'an. The description of this Book is stated here: "In it, We have explained in the light of Our definitive knowledge all those matters, the knowledge of which is necessary for the success of the world and the Hereafter." Through this explanation, there is also an indication of those details which passed above regarding the people of Paradise, the people of Hell, and the companions of Al-A'raf. Although they are related to the Hereafter, let no one consider them as empty and imaginary stories (fiction); rather, they are realities to come, stated entirely on the basis of definitive knowledge. Here, the indefiniteness of the word 'ilm' (knowledge) is a proof that it is for highlighting its status. That is, they are based on the great, vast, and all-encompassing knowledge of God. Every word of them is definitive and unalterable. God knows the matters of the Day of Resurrection in the same way He knows the matters of yesterday and today. For this reason, if anyone tries to dismiss them as imaginary stories and make a joke of them, let him think that the day is not far when he will see every single word with his own eyes and cry out: "Whatever news the Messengers of Allah gave proved to be true."
"Hudaw-wa rahmatal-liqawminy-yu'minoon"—the two words of guidance (Huda) and mercy (Rahmah) have encompassed both the beginning and the end, the world and the Hereafter. That is, this Book is guidance for people in the world, and the fruit of adopting this guidance is mercy in the Hereafter. The verb 'yu'minoon' (they believe) is, as we clarified in another place, in the meaning of the intention of the verb. Translate it as: "for those who wish to believe."

"Hal yandhuroona illa ta'weelah, yawma ya'tee ta'weeluhu yaqoolulladhina nasoohu min qablu qad ja'at rusulu rabbina bil-haqq, fahal lana min shufa'a'a fayashfa'oo lana aw nuraddu fana'mala ghayralladhee kunna na'mal, qad khasiroo anfusahum wa dalla 'anhumma kanoo yaftaroon."
"Hal yandhuroona illa ta'weelah"—We have discussed the word 'ta'weel' (fulfillment) in detail in the commentary of Surah Al-Imran. The meaning is: "These matters which the Qur'an is narrating are indeed unalterable realities, but since they have not yet appeared in the form of events and are hidden behind the veil of the future, the deniers consider them empty threats and are waiting for them to appear in the form of events to believe after seeing them."
"Yawma ya'tee ta'weeluhu yaqoolulladhina nasoohu min qablu qad ja'at rusulu rabbina bil-haqq." The meaning is that when these things appear in the form of events, the eyes of those who have bandages over them today will open, and they will cry out: "Whatever matters the Messengers of our Lord warned us of proved to be reality." At that time, they will say in regret: "Is there any intercessor to intercede, or is there any way for us to return to the world and do some good?" But this regret of theirs will remain only regret, because the time for doing good would have passed. The time they received to do good was wasted by them in doing evil, and they relied on false intercessors. "Qad khasiroo anfusahum wa dalla 'anhumma kanoo yaftaroon." The actual price of life, as clarified in Surah Al-Asr, is to do good. Whoever did not do good ruined his life and, with highly valuable capital, purchased destruction for himself. In "dalla 'anhum" (lost to them), there is an indication that their fabricated deities will all prove to be dreams and imaginations, because their reality was nothing more than that they fabricated them from their own minds and attributed them to God: 'God has made them His partners and intercessors.'

Section 8. Theme of verses 54 and 58: Above, the discussion ended at the baselessness and evil end of Shirk. In the subsequent verses, the theme of monotheism is clarified and completed: the Creator of the heavens and the earth is God, and He has not sat detached from this universe after creating it; rather, established upon His throne of authority, He is ruling over the entire universe. Day and night, sun and moon, stars and planets, all are in motion night and day in obedience to His commands. The One Who created is the One Whose command and rule is running over the entire universe; so how can anyone else be entitled to have his command run in the universe created by God? Furthermore, this universe itself bears witness through its existence that the Entity Who created it is highly beneficial and full of blessings and mercy; so invoke Him alone openly and secretly, and turn to Him in hope and fear at all times. Associating anyone with God's divinity is to act rebelliously against God and create corruption in His land, and God never loves those who turn away from Him and create corruption in His land.
After this, a parable of rain is presented through which three realities are clarified simultaneously. First, that God's mercy is highly close to His righteous servants; so one should turn to God alone in hope and fear at all times, and not seek the support of others by considering God and His mercy far away. It is God alone Who, after the land becomes dry and you become hopeless, raises the clouds of His mercy and floods the entire land. Second, that just as you see the land, after becoming completely dry and lifeless, coming to life with a single shower of rain, so will Allah Almighty, whenever He wills, raise you alive after your death and decay. Third, that just as the effect of rain appears in different forms on lands of different capacities—fertile land flourishes, while barren land produces only thorns—so will the guidance and mercy sent down upon this earth in the form of the Qur'an produce different effects on natures of different capacities. Those who protected their nature from being corrupted will benefit from it, and their hearts will shine with the light of faith; but those in whom no trace of good remains will produce only the thorns of disbelief and hostility. Read the verses:
"Inna rabbakumullahulladhee khalaqas-samawati wal-arda fee sittati ayyamin thummas-tawa 'alal-'arshi yughshil-laylan-nahara yatlubuhu hatheethaw-wash-shamsa wal-qamara wan-nujooma musakh-kharatim-bi-amrih, ala lahul-khalqu wal-amru, tabarakallahu rabbul-'alameen."
He Who is the Creator is the Lord: "Inna rabbakumullahulladhee khalaqas-samawati wal-arda fee sittati ayyamin." We have clarified in another place that the Arabs indeed acknowledged Allah Almighty as the Creator of the heavens, earth, and all other things, but they had made other lords besides Allah. Their concept was that Allah Almighty, after creating the world, divided the different departments of its management and administration among His other agents, and now the actual masters of these departments are those agents; hence, their worship is necessary. They are the medium of near-ness to God, and they have actual authority over the treasures of sustenance, grace, wealth, and children. If they are not pleased, work cannot be done through Allah Almighty alone. It was these agents whom they gave the status of lords, partners, and intercessors, and though theoretically God possessed the status of the Creator of the universe and the Lord of lords in their mythology, practically all their attachment was not with the Lord of lords but with these fabricated lords. Here, Allah Almighty corrected them on this deviation of theirs: the One Who is the Creator of the heavens and the earth is also the Lord; when the Creator is He, and you do not deny this, under what logic have you made others your lords?
The wisdom of gradual creation and evolution in the creation of the universe: "fee sittati ayyamin"—by 'days' (Ayyam) here is not meant our twenty-four-hour days; rather, these are divine days. God's affairs are implemented according to His own days, which, as stated in the Qur'an, are equal to a thousand years of our reckoning, and also to fifty thousand years. For this reason, six days refer to six periods. The creation of the world in six periods is mentioned in both the Torah and the Qur'an; rather, as far as its gradual evolution is concerned, modern philosophy also claims it with great emphasis. For this reason, the evolution of this universe is not a matter of dispute between the ancient and modern views; however, the manner in which the theory of evolution is presented by its flagbearers contains many logical gaps which cannot be filled until those intellectual and natural principles are accepted which the Qur'an has stated for this evolution. We will discuss this issue, if Allah wills, in its place.
Proofs of monotheism: Allah Almighty, instead of creating this entire universe in six days or six periods, could have created it in a single moment with His word "Kun" (Be). This was not far from His power. But His wisdom required that it be created in six periods. Just as God has highlighted His power in every department of His creation and administration, He has also highlighted His wisdom, rububiyyah (lordship), and mercy; and the highlighting of these aspects of His is as necessary for the intellectual and spiritual perfection of man as the highlighting of His perfect power. We have clarified in the commentary of Surah Al-An'am that it was not far from God's power to rain bread directly from heaven for our food; so why was it necessary that winds blow, clouds rise, rain fall, fields be ploughed, wheat be sown, sprouts emerge, stalks grow, leaves and fruit appear, crop yield, ears of corn show, grains form, and then hot and dry winds blow to ripen these grains, thus taking six months of hot and cold stages for the grain of wheat to reach the farmer's store from the field? All this is because in this way, every single thing of this universe became a book of God's signs, creation, administration, and wonders of power and wisdom. Whichever corner of it man casts his eyes upon, if his eyes are open and his intellect is awake, a school of gnosis of God opens up. Every single thing changes so many forms and adopts so many garbs to draw our attention to itself, so that we may see God's signs in them and take warning from them. What is the state of every particle of this world is the state of this world as a whole. This too did not stand up suddenly as an accident, but its Builder completed it in different stages with great gradualness, wisdom, and care, until it became adorned with all necessary requirements for man to dwell in it. This care and gradualness bear witness that this is not an accidental happening or a play, but a purposeful factory, and it is necessary that one day that purpose and goal appear. We will discuss this point in detail under verse 7 of Surah Hud, where the Hereafter is argued from this aspect of care.
"Thummas-tawa 'alal-'arsh." This is stated as an obvious result of creating the heavens and the earth with this state of care: He Who made and adorned this entire factory with this gradualness and care—how is it possible that after creating it, He should sit completely detached from its administration and control in some corner? The obvious requirement of this creation is that after creating it, He should sit upon its throne of government and administer all its affairs. Thus, to clarify this reality, "yudabbirul-amr" (He administers the affair) is associated with "istawa 'alal-'arshi" in some places. God creating but then not administering it is a highly foolish charge against God. If a king gains a country with great care but then sits in some corner without arranging peace and justice in it, allowing mischief-makers to create corruption, all creation will call him an unqualified king. How then can such a thing be believed about the Creator and Master of the heavens and the earth? This is a refutation of the polytheists' assumption that God is the Creator, but after creating the heavens and the earth, He handed over the administration of the world to His other partners and sat apart Himself. At the same time, this is also a refutation of those short-sighted philosophers who give God only the status of a detached Prime Cause, who created a motion as the first mover but then had nothing to do with what results from this motion of His and whose responsibility it is to control it. It is said: "God, after creating the universe, is established upon the Throne and is administering the universe." The Throne ('Arsh) is an expression of authority, and when 'ala' follows 'istawa', it means establishment.
"Yughshil-laylan-nahara yatlubuhu hatheetha." 'Hatheeth' means fast and active. This is the clarification of that administration and control which is inherent in "istawa 'alal-'arsh". The meaning is: "Whatever motion is taking place in this system of the universe is taking place solely through the administration and control of its Creator. It is He Who covers the night over the day, and at His command, it is actively pursuing it." "Wash-shamsa wal-qamara wan-nujooma musakh-kharatim-bi-amrih"—it is He Who created the sun, the moon, and all other stars; "musakh-kharatim-bi-amrih", meaning they are bound to their respective appointed duties and limits by God's command, performing their duties night and day with full activity. There is no possibility of their being negligent for even a single moment or exceeding their limits by a hair's breadth.
He Who is the Creator has the right to rule: "ala lahul-khalqu wal-amru"—this is both a statement of fact and an expression of right. That is, the One Who created this universe is the One Whose command and rule is running in every corner of it; every particle is active night and day in compliance with His commands with full enthusiasm, and this is the right too: that His command alone should run in every corner of it, because on what basis of entitlement can anyone else's command run in the universe created by Him?
Here, it should be kept in mind that through the word 'hatheetha', it is expressed that everything is performing its assigned duties with full enthusiasm, with no trace of half-heartedness or coldness. This is an indication that for man too, as a creature of God, this is the beautiful attitude: to be active in His servitude and obedience in the same way. Second, it is worth noting that here the activity of the night is mentioned but the activity of the day is not, whereas in another place, the day is mentioned along with the night. For example: "Wa huwal-ladhee ja'alal-layla wan-nahara khilfatal-liman arada ay-yadh-dhakkara aw arada shakoora" (Al-Furqan: 62). The reason for this is that here, after the mention of the night, the mention of the sun followed, which automatically clarified the opposite aspect.
God is unique in both His majesty and His mercy: "tabarakallahu rabbul-'alameen"—the grammatical form of 'tafa'ul' contains the meaning of the highest degree of emphasis. For this reason, "tabarakallah" means: "Highly blessed and merciful is the Entity of Allah." Above, the descriptions of creation and administration which Allah has clarified show, as we pointed out, both His power and majesty, and His mercy, lordship, generosity, and favor. This is the removal of that misconception in which polytheist nations generally fell: they raised the conception of God's majesty and power so high that His attributes of mercy and blessing became completely suppressed under it. The result of this misconception was that direct connection and mediation with God was deemed impossible for servants, and then such means and mediators were sought which could become a source of achieving purposes from God. We have explained in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah that in the matter of divine attributes, this deviation is one of the greatest factors of Shirk. The polytheists began to worship many fabricated deities, especially the angels, on the premise: 'These are the beloved daughters of God; if they remain pleased with us, they will keep their Father pleased with us, and then the entire world will be merciful to us.' The Qur'an, through the words "tabarakallahu rabbul-'alameen", drew attention to the reality that just as this universe bears witness to the boundless majesty and power of its Creator, it is also a witness to His boundless blessing and mercy. So there is no need of any mediator to ask Him; turn to Him in hope and fear, at all times, because just as He is unique in His majesty, He is unique in His mercy.

"Ud'oo rabbakum tadarru'aw-wa khufyah, innahu la yuhibbul-mu'tadeen."
"Ud'oo rabbakum tadarru'aw-wa khufyah." 'Tadarru' is from 'dhara'at'. Its meaning is humility, pleading, laging, and expressing submissiveness. This expression is through actions and gestures, and also through words and phrases. Its most effective form is when there is complete harmony between words and actions, the best example of which in Islam is prayer (Salah). Standing with respect after performing Wudu, binding hands, bowing head, bending knees, placing nose and forehead on dust—these are the actions and gestures of 'tadarru''; and the prayers and glorifications recited with these different actions are the spiritual expressions of this 'tadarru''.
'Khufyah' means secretly, which is among the etiquettes of 'tadarru'', guaranteeing both the sincerity of 'tadarru'' and its dignity. Whatever is done secretly remains safe from the trial of ostentation (Riya); and since God hears and knows everything, there is no need of shouting to make Him hear. It should be kept in mind that the word 'khufyah' is only to block the way of ostentation and bad manners. This does not deny the loud voice (Jahr) adopted in congregational prayers or sometimes by a servant in his individual supplications. This is not a denial of 'jahr' but only an emphasis on moderation. The clarification of this theme will come, if Allah wills, under the verse of Bani Isra'il: "Wa la tajhar bisalatika wa la tukhāfit biha wabtaghi bayna dhalika sabeela".
The lesson of the system of the universe to man: After the explanation of the words, now reflect on the occasion and meaning of the verse. Above, the reality was clarified that God alone is the Creator of the heavens and the earth, the stars and planets are in motion by His command, creation and command are in His authority, and He is a highly beneficial and blessed Entity. Now, "ud'oo rabbakum" states the right and duty which this great and generous Lord has upon His servants. That right and duty is: "Invoke your Lord pleadingly and secretly." That is, this arrogance and pride being shown by you is not beautiful for you. When everything of this universe is submissive and bowing before God, active in compliance with His commands, what is your status to act arrogantly and raise your head before God?
"Innahu la yuhibbul-mu'tadeen." God never loves those who exceed His limits. This system of the universe bears witness that He does not allow anything to deviate from its limits, and the history of this world also bears witness that He never gave those who act arrogantly and exceed limits a respite beyond a specific limit.

"Wa la tufsidoo fil-ardi ba'da islahiha wad'oohu khawfaw-wa tama'a, inna rahmatallahi qareebum-minal-muhsineen." This is the confirmation of the above theme from the negative aspect. That is, do not adopt deviation from your Lord to create corruption in this land. The Qur'an has clarified from different aspects that if there were other deities in the heavens and the earth besides Allah, they would have been ruined; they are established only because no other will is running in them except the will of Allah. The necessary requirement of this cosmic monotheism is that the servants, in their sphere of choice, perform worship and obedience to Allah alone, without associating anyone in this worship and obedience; otherwise, the entire system of justice and shariah of this land will be ruined. Just as cosmic monotheism is indispensable for the existence of this universe, God's legislative monotheism is also necessary for the peace and justice of this land. Making another deity and master in God's country is to create corruption and rebellion in His country, which is the greatest crime.
The condition "ba'da islahiha" (after its reform) with "la tufsidoo" is to highlight the heinousness of this act. That is, creating corruption in the country is the greatest crime in itself, but this crime becomes graver when it occurs after reform, because this is not ruining a ruined thing, but ruining a set thing.
The basis of reform and corruption of the universe: It should be kept in mind here that the Creator of the universe, when He made this world, did not leave it in a state of chaos and insecurity after making it; rather, before sending Adam and his progeny to this world, He took from them the covenant of monotheism—namely, His worship and obedience. This is mentioned ahead in verses 172 and 173 of this Surah. Then, He promised the progeny of Adam, as passed in verses 35 and 36: "For your guidance, I will send My Messengers; follow them. Those who follow them will succeed, and those who act arrogantly and turn away from them will be destroyed." Then, according to His promise, Allah Almighty repeatedly sent His Messengers, details of whom are coming ahead in verses 59 to 92. In the stories of these Messengers, as will be clear from verses 69, 74, 85, 100, and 102, it is shown that whenever different groups of the children of Adam deviated from God's straight path to create corruption in this world, God warned them through His Messengers; and when they did not care for this warning, God cut their roots and handed over the authority (Khilafah) to others to see how they perform. In this way, this world has been ruined repeatedly through the efforts of Satan and his progeny, and reformed repeatedly through the prophets and reformers. Reflecting on the matter from this aspect, it is known that whichever nation received the authority of its predecessors in this world, received it in the form of reform after destroying a corruption; and if the nation receiving authority created corruption in it, it did not ruin a ruined thing, but ruined a set thing, and this makes its crime graver.
A misconception of social reformers: As far as the Messengers are concerned, the aforementioned principle regarding them and their nations is completely unalterable; through them, the truth appears shining like the sun; so the system destroyed by them was falsehood, and the one established was truth. After the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), the chain of messengership ended, but this does not mean that today the law of nature regarding the rise and fall of nations has changed. Today too, if a nation is destroyed, it is not destroyed by chance; and if a nation rises, it does not rise by chance; rather, moral factors alone work in the fall of one and the rise of another. But a nation's rising on the support of a few life-giving moral factors is one thing, and a system being truth is another. A nation's rise is indeed a proof that it possesses more life-giving moral factors than the defeated nation, but this cannot be a proof that this nation and its system are 100% truth. Many of our social reformers fell into a grave misconception in understanding this principle, because of which they fell into excess and deficiency. Those who were caught in national prejudice always attributed the dominance of the dominant nation to its high-handedness and cunning; they, because of their prejudice, could neither see the moral superiority present in the dominant nation, nor did their eyes fall on the moral weakness found in themselves. Similarly, those who were of intimidated minds considered every dominant's dominance as a proof of its being truth, and whatever corruption and falsehood was created in the world at its hands was regarded as the system of truth, singing its praises. The effect of this excess and deficiency on the history of nations was that it became compiled in a completely wrong manner, making it impossible to derive correct results and benefit from them in social reform. Here, we suffice on this indication. We will discuss this with explanation, if Allah wills, in the commentary of Surah Al-Rum.
In both hope and fear, the resort is God alone: "wad'oohu khawfaw-wa tama'a, inna rahmatallahi qareebum-minal-muhsineen"—this is the opposite sentence of "ud'oo rabbakum tadarru'aw-wa khufyah". In that, the state of invoking God was told, which is the opposite of arrogance. Now, the motivator of invoking God is clarified, through which the root of every trace of Shirk is cut. Arrogance makes one independent of God. If its head is crushed, poverty is created in man, and this poverty connects the servant with God. This poverty has two aspects: one fear, the other hope (translate them as fear and hope). Man's inner motivators are also divided into these two categories, and if you analyze his external factors and motivators, they too fall either under fear or under hope. We want many things, material and spiritual, have desires for them, wish for them; similarly, there are many things, material and spiritual, which we want to avoid, fear, and repel. For both these states, the Qur'an states the requirement of monotheism: man should make Allah alone his resort and master. Whatever he wants, he should refer to God; from whatever he fears, he should seek refuge in God, because the Giver is He, and the Preventer is He; besides Him, no one can bless with any good or save from any evil. Furthermore, the true criterion of knowing good and evil is also Him. If in both hope and fear, God alone is the resort and His pleasure is sought, man cannot face any difficulty in determining what is to be wanted and how it is to be wanted, and what is to be avoided and how it is to be avoided. It should be kept in mind that fear and hope are both highly dangerous valleys. Satan ambushes man from both of these, and involves people in some form of open or hidden Shirk.
From the style of expression in "inna rahmatallahi qareebum-minal-muhsineen", it is deduced that those who make their Lord their resort in both hope and fear are indeed "muhsin" (good-doers), and Allah's mercy is highly close to such good-doers. It is known that to excel to the status of Ihsan (perfection), it is necessary that man be devoted to his Lord in both states of fear and hope. If this devotion is not achieved, he is far from the status of Ihsan; and whoever is far from the status of Ihsan is far from God's mercy. God's mercy is close to the good-doers. Whenever they invoke Him in some hope or fear, He blesses them with His mercy. Here, the rule of grammar is observed: since the femininity of the word 'rahmah' (mercy) is not real, it was not necessary to bring the predicate in the feminine. Also, the grammatical weight of 'fa'eel' comes equally for masculine and feminine in some situations.

"Wa huwal-ladhee yursilur-riyaha bushram-bayna yaday rahmatihi, hatta idha aqallat sahaban thiqalans-suqnahu libaladin-mayyitin fa-anzalna bihil-ma'a fa-akhrajna bihi min kullith-thamarat, kadhalika nukhrijul-mawta la'allakum tadhakkaroon."
The parable of rain for making God the resort in both hope and fear:
"Wa huwal-ladhee yursilur-riyaha bushram-bayna yaday rahmatihi"—by 'mercy' (Rahmah) here is meant rain. This word is used for rain in more than one place in the Qur'an. This is a parable of the fact that in both states of fear and hope, one should turn to Allah alone, because mercy always descends from Allah's side. Rain, on which the life of the entire world depends—no one can drop a single drop of it on this earth; it is God alone Who first blows the winds carrying glad tidings, which lift the heavy clouds on their shoulders, then God drives them to dry regions and rains water from them, from which all kinds of fruits and crops are produced. In another place it is said: "Wa huwal-ladhee yunazzilul-ghaytha mim-ba'di ma qanatoo wa yanshiru rahmatah" (Al-Shura: 28) (And He is the One Who sends down the rain after they have despaired and spreads His mercy). "Hatta idha aqallat sahaban thiqalans-suqnahu libaladin-mayyitin fa-anzalna bihil-ma'a"—'iqlaal' means to lift something as if it has no weight; 'sahab' is the plural of 'sahabah' but is singular in form; hence, the pronoun 'suqnahu' is singular. In "fa-anzalna bihi", the preposition 'bi' in my view is locative. Here, Allah Almighty has drawn attention to His power and wisdom: "Look, how winds lift the heavy clouds as if they are cotton swabs, and then it is We Who drive them wherever We wish and flood whichever place We wish; no one has the power to turn them in his preferred direction. So in hope and fear, turn to Him alone; there is no other resort in this universe."
Recalling the Resurrection through the parable of rain: "kadhalika nukhrijul-mawta la'allakum tadhakkaroon"—this draws attention to another reality from the parable of the rain. That is: "Just as you see that by sending rain, We bring a completely dead and lifeless land to life, so will We raise all the dead alive one day." The God of Whose power you see these signs every day—why do you consider raising the dead far from His power? We have displayed these signs in this land solely so that you may obtain a reminder for the Hereafter. In "la'allakum tadhakkaroon", the purpose of this attention-drawing is stated.

"Wal-baladut-tayyibu yakhruju nabatuhu bi-idhni rabbih, walladhee khabutha la yakhruju illa nakida, kadhalika nusarriful-ayati liqawminy-yashkuroon."
We have clarified in another place that the words 'tayyib' and 'khabeeth' are used for both spiritual/moral and material purity/impurity. Here, the occasion proves that 'baladut-tayyib' refers to fertile and capable land, and 'alladhee khabutha' refers to barren and salty land. Then, on the principle of comparison, the word opposite to 'nakida' is omitted in the first part of the sentence. 'Nakida' means defective and scarce; hence, in the first part of the sentence, the meaning of 'pure' and 'abundant' is omitted.
Another reality pointed out from the parable: Now, see a third reality pointed out from this parable of rain: God's cloud of mercy rains equally on dry and wet places, but the benefit is received according to capacity. Just as you see that when rain falls, fertile land flourishes, but barren and salty land either produces nothing or produces only some thorns; similarly, the benefit of the mercy sent down from heaven in the form of the Qur'an will not reach everyone equally, but according to capacity. Those whose natural capacities are alive will flourish like orchards; but those who have wasted their capacities—do not expect anything from them except the thorns of hostility. This reality is clarified in a Hadith in which the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) said: 'The likeness of the guidance and knowledge with which Allah has sent me is like a rain falling on a land. A part of it was fertile; it accepted the water and produced vegetation and grass in abundance. And another part of it was hard; it held the water, and Allah benefited people through it: they drank, irrigated, and sowed. And a part of it was barren; it neither held water nor produced vegetation. This is the likeness of those who understand the religion and benefit from what Allah has sent me with, so they learn and teach; and the likeness of those who do not pay attention to it and do not accept the guidance of Allah with which I have been sent' (Al-Bukhari and Muslim).
See how many realities became clear from a single parable of rain! The proof of monotheism appeared, the likeness of the Resurrection and Hereafter was found, and the sunnah of Allah in guidance and deviation was highlighted. In other words, all the issues discussed from the beginning of the Surah to here became uncovered. We have mentioned that the Creator has made this universe in such a form that if man has seeing eyes, every leaf and branch is bearing witness to those realities which the Qur'an is inviting to; but where are the seeing eyes and hearing ears!
"Kadhalika nusarriful-ayati liqawminy-yashkuroon"—'tasreef' means to rotate/circulate. In the Qur'an, this word is used for winds. The same word is used here by the Qur'an for verses. Just as through the rotation of winds, He highlights different aspects of power, wisdom, mercy, and punishment in this universe, so that people may understand, recognize, and appreciate them. In "liqawminy-yashkuroon", there is an indication that this rotation of verses is a great favor, provided there are people to appreciate it. The actual reality of the word 'shukr' we have clarified in another place is appreciation. The utility of every blessing depends on this appreciation. If this appreciation is not present, just as playing a lute before a buffalo is futile, so is composing a hundred colors of themes before such oxen futile!

Section 10. Verses 59 to 93:
Now, the theme of warning, which has been coming from above, is clarified through historical proofs, taking each of the past nations of Arabia to show how Allah granted them authority in this land, but upon gaining authority, they adopted ungratefulness, created corruption after reform, and ruined justice. Eventually, Allah sent His Messenger among them who invited them to monotheism and justice, but they, because of their pride, not only rejected the Messenger's word but also turned hostile to him, as a result of which Allah destroyed that nation after giving a specific limit of respite. The purpose of narrating this history to the Quraish is: their case too is in the same court in which the cases of all these nations were decided and they reached their end. There is no reason that if they adopt the same way, the decision of this impartial court for them would be different; God's law is one for all. When God sends His Messenger to a nation, only two paths remain for that nation: either it accepts reform, or destruction; no other path remains.
It should be clear that the Arabs were familiar with the stories of the nations coming ahead, but this familiarity was in the form of vague and dim traditions; especially their moral aspect was completely vague. The Qur'an removed the veil of this vagueness and revived history, inviting them to listen to these stories with open ears and take warning from them: this is not merely the tale of others, but it is their tale too. Read the subsequent verses in this light. It is said:
Prophet Noah (peace be upon him) and his dawah: "Laqad arsalna Noohan ila qawmihi..." Since in this Surah the Quraish are being warned, those nations are taken first which became famous in the north, south, or north-west of Arabia, and which met their end as a result of denying the Messengers. The order of description is completely historical; hence, the nation of Noah was taken first, highlighting from the story of every nation only that portion which was necessary for the purpose of warning.
The common reality of the prophets' dawah: "Faqala ya qawmi'budullaha ma lakum-min ilahin ghayruh." We have mentioned above that the root of all corruption in the land is Shirk. A nation falling into Shirk means that it has deviated from the straight path of nature in thoughts, beliefs, and consequently, in deeds and morals; and its growth in the land is no longer the growth of good and reform, but of evil and corruption; and as long as this nation remains, corruption alone will flourish through its hands in every field of individual and social life. For this reason, the Messengers of Allah begin their reformative dawah from this fundamental point, and this is such a characteristic of the prophets' dawah that it is impossible for any prophet's dawah to be found without it.
"Innee akhafu 'alaykum 'adhaba yawmin 'adheem." This is the actual warning which Prophet Noah warned his nation of: "If you do not repent from Shirk and adopt the path of worship and obedience of Allah alone, then know that the punishment of a horrific day is about to descend upon you." By 'horrific day' here is meant the horrific day of worldly punishment.
The Messengers of Allah warn of two punishments. We have mentioned above that the Messengers of Allah warn their nations of two punishments. First, of the punishment which comes in the world as a necessary result of denying the Messenger. Second, of the punishment with which they must inevitably deal on the Day of Resurrection, the Day of Decision. Here, the context is a proof pointing toward the worldly punishment. It was expressed as a 'horrific day' because the punishment that comes as a result of denying the Messenger is not in the nature of reminder and warning; rather, that punishment cuts the roots of that nation, after which its mark as a nation is wiped out from the face of the earth. In Surah Al-Shu'ara, it is narrated from the tongue of Prophet Hud: "Innee akhafu 'alaykum 'adhaba yawmin 'adheem" (Al-Shu'ara: 135); then their nation's answer and their destruction in the world is described as: "Wa ma nahnu bimu'adh-dhibeen, fakadh-dhaboohu fa-ahlaknahum, inna fee dhalika la-ayah" (And we are not to be punished. So they denied him, and We destroyed them. Indeed in that is a sign).

Prophet Lot (peace be upon him) and his nation's questioning and answering:
We have discussed the word 'Mala'' in another place. "Inna lanaraka fee dalalim-mubeen." That is: "Because of these statements of yours, we see you in clear error. First, you insulted the religion of our ancestors by denying those deities whom our ancestors worshipped; second, the tragedy is that you are threatening us with the descent of divine punishment, whereas our state is better in every respect than yours and that of your followers."

"Qala ya qawmi laysa bee dalalatun-wa lakinnee rasoolum-mir-rabbil-'alameen." Prophet Noah said in answer: "Do not consider me a madman or deviated person; I have come as a Messenger to you from the Lord of the worlds, and whatever I am reciting to you, I am reciting completely from God, without adding anything on my own."

"Uballighukum risalati rabbee wa ansahu lakum wa a'lamu minallahi ma la ta'lamoon." This is both the message of God and the ultimate goodwill from me toward you: despite your obstinacy, denial, and hostility, I am pursuing you and bearing everything at your hands. No motive other than sympathy and goodwill is involved in all this effort of mine. It is only this fear and grief: lest you fall into the final seizure of God. You see only the outward appearance; hence, the warning of punishment seems strange to you, but I know from God what you do not know. If you knew, along with the shine of your outward appearance, those diseases which you have hidden under your white clothes, you would realize how true a word I am saying.

"Awa 'ajibtum an ja'akum dhikrum-mir-rabbikum 'ala rajulim-minkum..." That is: "Is this preventing you from accepting my word: that Allah has sent down a reminder to a man from among you for you, so that he may warn you of the danger before you, so that you may fear Allah and become deserving of His mercy?" Here, by raising the question, the speech is left without giving an answer, because the style is of expressing regret and sorrow. This style of expression implies: "If you had reflected, without arrogance..."
From "liyundhirakum wa litattaqoo wa la'allakum turhamoon", it is known that the actual purpose of the Messenger's warning is to produce fear and Taqwa in people, and it is this fear and Taqwa which makes people deserving of divine mercy in the world and the Hereafter. On the contrary, those who wait for the sign of punishment and the observation of angels are in fact inviting divine punishment upon themselves.

"Fakadh-dhaboohu fa-anjaynahu walladhina ma'ahu fil-fulki wa aghraqna-lladhina kadh-dhaboo bi-ayatina, innahum kanoo qawman 'ameen."
That is, eventually that matter occurred of which Prophet Noah had warned. When the nation denied and, despite the long struggle of Prophet Noah, did not reform its attitude, Allah Almighty drowned all except those who took refuge in the ark with Prophet Noah. We will discuss the nature of this flood in the place where the Qur'an has described its details. Here, remember only this: only those were drowned who persisted in denying the Prophet and Allah's verses. The sunnah of Allah is that the punishment which comes as a result of denying the Prophet comes only upon those upon whom the Prophet completed his proof. Prophet Noah's nation lived in the region of the Tigris and Euphrates. Was there any other human population in that era outside this region? This question belongs to a period of history which is based on estimates rather than facts. We do not wish to discuss this question here. However, this is based on the unalterable reality of divine sunnah: this punishment came only upon those who denied Prophet Noah.
The difference between punishment ('Adhab) and trial (Ibtila): It should be remembered here that there is a difference between punishment and trial. One form is that Allah Almighty sends some calamity—famine, epidemic, earthquake, flood, etc.—upon a nation so that the negligent may wake up from their sleep of negligence. When such trial-based calamities descend, it is not necessary that their harm remain limited only to the criminals; rather, both good and bad fall into their grip. The second form is that Allah Almighty, after completion of proof, sends a decisive punishment upon a nation as a recompense for its deeds. In this case, those who reform or follow the reformers are saved from the grip of the punishment.
The dealing of God with the blind of intellect and heart: "innahum kanoo qawman 'ameen." 'Ameen' is the plural of 'Amee'. This word is used for both the physically blind and the blind of intellect and heart. Here, it is used for the blind of intellect and heart. This explains the reason why Allah Almighty drowned this nation: "because they were blind of intellect and heart." The Messenger of Allah is the final source of completion of proof. After this source is brought to bear, no other thing remains in the divine law whose appearance is necessary for completion of proof; hence, those who do not open their eyes even after the Messenger's warning destroy their own existence. Why should nature keep such people whose eyes of heart have become completely shut?

"Wa ila 'Adin akhahum Hooda, qala ya qawmi'budullaha ma lakum-min ilahin ghayruh, afala tattaqoon."
The story of the nation of 'Ad and the dawah of Prophet Hud: The 'Ad are among the ancient nations of Arabia. They belong to the Semitic race. Their dwelling was the region of Ahqaf in Southern Arabia. From the words of the Qur'an "wa ja'alakum khulafa'a mim-ba'di qawmi Nooh", it is inferred that after the flood of Noah, some of the survivors migrated toward Southern Arabia and gradually gained great power and authority there. In Arab literature, they are proverbial for both their antiquity and their majesty and force. Among them, Allah Almighty sent Prophet Hud; through the word "akhahum" (their brother), Allah Almighty pointed to the favor which He pointed to in other places with the words "rasoolam-minhum" (a messenger from among themselves). The Messenger being from among the nation itself is a great favor of Allah from the viewpoint of completion of proof. We have pointed to this above, and it has been clarified in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. Although, like every favor, nations always showed ungratefulness to this favor too.
"afala tattaqoon"—that is, do you not fear the wrath of God and His punishment? Exactly in this place, Prophet Noah said: "Innee akhafu 'alaykum 'adhaba yawmin 'adheem".

"Qalal-mala'ulladhina kafaroo min qawmihi inna lanaraka fee safahatiw-wa inna lanadhunnuka minal-kadhibeen." That is: "This warning of punishment you give us is merely your foolishness. Is this progress of ours a sign of our deserving punishment, or of our being favored and on the straight path! 'Wa inna lanadhunnuka minal-kadhibeen'—that is, this claim of yours that you are sent by God and God has sent you as a prophet to us is merely your empty claim, and we consider you completely a liar."

"Uballighukum risalati rabbee wa ana lakum nasihun ameen." Along with 'nasih' (advisor), the attribute 'ameen' (trustworthy) is brought here in the same way as in Surah Al-Shu'ara: "Innee lakum rasoolun ameen". Trustworthiness is the opposite of betrayal. A prominent quality of a Messenger is that he is trustworthy. That is, whatever message is handed over to him, he conveys it with complete trustworthiness, without changing a single letter.

"Wadh-kuroo idh ja'alakum khulafa'a mim-ba'di qawmi Nooh..."—The granting of authority to 'Ad after the nation of Noah does not mean that they were granted authority in the exact same region where the nation of Noah had it. We have mentioned above that the nation of Noah was in the north, and the region of 'Ad was the Southern region of Arabia. This authority (Khilafah) means only that just as the nation of Noah was granted authority and power, so were you granted after them. In this reminder, Prophet Hud reminded the nation of Allah's favor and also warned them. The favor is obvious: the authority which the nation of Noah possessed was inherited by them; and the warning is: when you have been made the heirs of the authority of the nation of Noah, remember also their story and their end. If you adopt their way, there is no reason that you would escape the end they faced. God's law is one for all.
"wa zadakum fil-khalqi bastah." 'Khalq' here means structure/stature. For example: "wa la-amurannahum falyughayyirunna khalqallah" (Al-Nisa: 11) (And I will command them so they will alter the structure created by God). By 'structure', it is obvious that both inner and outer structure are meant, because under the influence of Satan, the polytheists distorted their nature and also cut the ears of cattle for the pleasure of their idols. 'Bastah' means expansion, vastness, and spread. He granted superiority in both physical and intellectual aspects. From Arab traditions, it is also known that the nation of 'Ad was prominent in physical health and had a great reputation in intellectual achievements. In Surah Al-Baqarah, it is said about Talut: "wa zadahu bastatan fil-'ilmi wal-jism", and the same thing is expressed here as "zadakum fil-khalqi bastah".
