"Fadh-kuroo ala'allahi la'allakum tuflihoon." 'Ala'' is the plural of 'ila', 'alay', 'aly'. Generally, lexicographers have taken it to mean blessing, but this is incorrect. Its meaning is wonder, majesty, achievement, and marvel. Our teacher, Mawlana Farahi (may Allah have mercy on him), has clarified this word in his book 'Mufradat-ul-Qur'an', quoting ten couplets of famous pre-Islamic poets from which the same meaning is derived.
This statement of Prophet Hud also contains aspects of both favor and warning. The meaning is: "Appreciate the physical and intellectual powers and capacities God has blessed you with, remain grateful for them, do not fall into trial because of them. Remember God's marvels, majesty, and power. This alone is the path of success. If you show arrogance, remember, God's seizure is also highly severe."

"Qaloo a-ji'tana lina'budallaha wahdahu wa nadhara ma kana ya'budu aba'una fa'tina bima ta'iduna in kunta minas-sadiqeen."
In this sentence, the nation's anger and sarcasm are both inherent. The meaning is: "Have you come as a claimant of messengership so that we, under your empty threat, abandon those deities whom our ancestors worshipped?" This also implies: "It was expected of you that you would increase the respect of the ancestral religion and the nation, but you rose as an enemy of all."
"fa'tina bima ta'iduna in kunta minas-sadiqeen"—so hear: we are not going to abandon the religion of our elders out of fear of your warnings of punishment. If we are to face punishment for this crime, bring that punishment if you are truthful in your claim.

"Qala qad waqa'a 'alaykum-mir-rabbikum rijsun-wa ghadhab."
"Qad waqa'a" is on the style of "ata amrullahi", meaning: now this thing has become necessary, obligatory, you have become deserving of it, only the waiting remains. 'Rijs' means dirt and impurity, and here it refers to the dirt and impurity of disbelief, Shirk, deeds, and beliefs. There is a necessary connection between 'rijs' and 'ghadhab' (wrath). The meaning is: "You are demanding punishment, so now there is no delay; consider it arrived, because you have loaded upon yourselves a great heap of that impurity and dirt from which God's wrath burns. After gathering such a great heap of 'rijs', do not consider God's thunderbolt of punishment far away."
"a-tujadiloonanee fee asma'in sammaytumooha antum wa aba'ukum-ma nazzalallahu biha min sultan." This is the answer to the nation's claim: "How can we abandon those deities whom we have been worshipping since the time of our ancestors?" He said: "This argument of yours is completely baseless. You have indeed given some names to the things you worship, but no named entity (Mussamma) exists for these names. No proof exists within intellect or nature for their being partners of God, nor has God informed anywhere through His prophets and messengers that He has made them His partners. So what is the basis on which you have made them your deities and are fighting with me in their support? As far as God is concerned, you believe in Him; He is not a new thing, proof for Him is present with you too; but what is the proof for these imaginary idols of yours which you can present?"
"fantadhiroo innee ma'akum-minal-muntadhireen." The meaning is: "Now if you are to believe only after seeing the punishment, wait for the punishment; I too am waiting for it with you. As far as its causes are concerned, you have provided all of them; so its coming is a definitive matter. But bringing punishment is not my job, it is God's job; you wait, I too am waiting."

"Fa-anjaynahu walladhina ma'ahu birahmatim-minna wa qata'na dabiralladhina kadh-dhaboo bi-ayatina wa ma kanoo mu'mineen." This is the description of that sunnah of Allah which is inevitably fulfilled after the completion of proof through the Messenger upon a nation: Allah Almighty, through His special grace, takes the Messenger and his companions under His protection and removes them from the region of punishment, and cuts the roots of all those who denied the Messenger. This is a decisive punishment which descends when the addressed nation, through its obstinacy, proves that no capacity of faith remains within it. "Wa ma kanoo mu'mineen" points to this reality.

"Wa ila Thamooda akhahum Salihan..." The Thamood are among the remnants of 'Ad. They are also called 'Ad the Second. Their dwelling was in the north-west of Arabia. It is possible that at the time of the destruction of 'Ad, those who remained safe from the punishment migrated from the south to the north-west and settled in Hijr. The descriptions of 'Ad and Thamood in both the Qur'an and Arab traditions are almost identical. Some poets mention these two nations in such a manner as if there is no difference between them at all. The leaders of both, Qayl and Qadar, through whose hands destruction came upon these nations, are proverbial in Arabic literature, and it appears they were cast in the exact same mold.
"qad ja'atkum bayyinatum-mir-rabbikum, hadhihi naqatullahi lakum ayatan fadharooha ta'kul fee ardhillahi wa la tamassooha bisoo'in faya'khudhakum 'adhabun aleem." This is the answer to the nation's demand for punishment. Prophet Salih repeatedly warned the nation of God's punishment: "Do not think that this comfort in which you are indulging, these gardens and springs you are enjoying, these fields and crops you are harvesting, these buildings and progress in which you are lost—these days and nights will remain forever. Save yourselves from God's seizure, accept my word, and do not cast yourselves into the pit of destruction by following your uncontrolled and absolute leaders who are creating corruption in God's land." The nation answered: "You appear to us to be a bewitched and eccentric person. How can we believe these empty words of yours when you are a human like us? We will believe your word only when you bring some sign of that punishment with which you are warning us." In response to this demand of theirs, Prophet Salih nominated a she-camel: "This is Allah's she-camel. This is a sign of divine punishment for you; if you cause any harm to it, know that the punishment will descend upon you. Consider it the barrier of the punishment; if you break this barrier, nothing will save you from the flood of divine wrath."
Attributing this she-camel to Allah was to point to its specification for Allah, i.e., it was dedicated and nominated for Him. This specification and nomination was of the same nature as the specification of the animals of Hady and Qala'id (sacrificial animals) in our religion. Just as in Islam, attacking Hady and Qala'id is a major crime, Prophet Salih directed regarding this she-camel that no one should interfere with it: "This is the barrier of safety; if you cause any harm to it, the punishment of which I am warning you will break upon you."
The wisdom of nominating the she-camel: Prophet Salih nominated this she-camel as a sensor (feeler) to see: if they killed it despite this emphasis and warning, this would be a definitive proof that these wrongdoers would now go to the extent of killing him too (God forbid) by considering him a liar. Under the sunnah of Allah, this stage of a nation's audacity is the one when the Prophet is commanded to migrate, and punishment descends upon the nation.
The words of the Qur'an nowhere indicate that this she-camel was a sign in respect of any wonder in its physical creation; rather, "wa la tamassooha bisoo'in faya'khudhakum 'adhabun aleem" clearly shows that it was a sign in respect of the consequence and result of being harmed. As far as knowledge goes, the she-camel emerging from a mountain is not in any authentic Hadith either. For this reason, there is no need to give much importance to those interpretative traditions which state that this she-camel was born from a mountain. The word 'Ayah' means sign and mark. This she-camel was nominated as a sign of punishment in response to the nation's demand for punishment; hence, it is stated in the Qur'an that when the leader of Thamood hamstrung it, the divine punishment descended on the third day. This event is narrated in various Surahs throughout the Qur'an. We present an example for ease of understanding:
"Kadh-dhabat Thamoodul-mursaleen (141) idh qala lahum akhuhum Salihun ala tattaqoon (142) innee lakum rasoolun ameen (143) fattaqullaha wa atee'oon (144) wa ma as'alukum 'alayhi min ajrin, in ajriya illa 'ala rabbil-'alameen (145) a-tutrakoona fee ma hahuna amineen (146) fee jannatin-wa 'uyoon (147) wa zuroo'in-wa nakhlin tal'uha hadeem (148) wa tanhitoona minal-jibali buyootan fariheen (149) fattaqullaha wa atee'oon (150) wa la tutee'oo amral-musrifeen (151) alladhina yufsidoona fil-ardi wa la yuslihoon (152) qaloo innama anta minal-musah-hareen (153) ma anta illa basharum-mithluna fa'ti bi-ayatin in kunta minas-sadiqeen (154) qala hadhihi naqatul-laha shirbuw-wa lakum shirbu yawmim-ma'loom (155) wa la tamassooha bisoo'in faya'khudhakum 'adhabu yawmin 'adheem (156) fa'aqarooha fa-asbahoo nadimeen (157) fa-akhadhahumul-'adhab, inna fee dhalika la-ayah, wa ma kana aktharuhum-mu'mineen (158)" (Thamood denied the Messengers, when their brother Salih said to them, 'Will you not fear Allah? I am a trustworthy Messenger to you; so fear Allah and obey me. And I do not ask you any reward for it; my reward is only with the Lord of the worlds. Will you be left secure in what is here? In gardens and springs, and fields and date-palms whose fruit is heavy? And you carve houses out of the mountains happily? So fear Allah and obey me, and do not obey the command of the extravagant who create corruption in the land and do not reform.' They answered, 'You are only of the bewitched; you are only a human like us; so bring some sign if you are truthful.' He said, 'This is a she-camel. For it is a share of water, and for you is a share of water on an appointed day. And do not touch it with harm, lest the punishment of a horrific day seize you.' But they hamstrung it, and then they became regretful. So the punishment seized them. Indeed in that is a sign, and the majority of them were not believers) (Al-Shu'ara: 141-158).
The nomination of a sign of punishment instead of the punishment itself in response to the demand for punishment was a proof of Allah's mercy and compassion. He is slow in wrath and quick in mercy; hence, He preferred to give them further respite to see if they would take warning; but instead of taking warning, they took the final step of audacity and hamstrung the she-camel. After this, although no room remained, it is clear from other places in the Qur'an that after crossing the limits, Allah Almighty gave them three days of respite to see if they would repent.
Prophet Salih's direction regarding the she-camel—that it should roam freely in the entire region, no one should interfere with it, and its day of drinking water from the spring should be completely separate, and on that day others should not bring their animals to the watering place—was for testing the nation to bring out the malice and hostility hidden within them, so that nothing remained hidden.

"Wadh-kuroo idh ja'alakum khulafa'a mim-ba'di 'Adiw-wa bawwa'akum fil-ardh..." We have mentioned above that these people were among the remnants of 'Ad, and their region was the region of Hijr in the north-west of Arabia. These people achieved great authority and progress there. Many of their architectural remnants are present today. The caravans of Quraish passed through their dwellings on their journey to Syria; hence, their story was both heard and seen by them. The purpose of recalling the inheritance of 'Ad's authority by Prophet Salih was, as we pointed out, to warn them: "Do not forget God's authority in the intoxication of your authority; the dealing He made with 'Ad is the one He will make with you; He weighs with a single scale for all."
"tattakhidhoona min suhooliha qusooraw-wa tanhitoonal-jibala buyoota." 'Suhl' (plains) and 'jabal' (mountains) are opposite words; 'suhl' refers to plains in contrast to mountainous regions. These people made great progress in civilization and architecture in their era. In plains, they built grand palaces, and in mountainous regions, they carved houses out of mountains. Some architectural remnants of their era are present today, bearing witness to their perfection in the art of architecture. Prophet Salih lamented all these architectural ambitions of theirs during their peak, which shows that building sky-high and grand buildings is not a sign of a nation's rise, but of the corruption of civilization and the fall of the nation. These same grand buildings eventually become the tombs and graves of its rise, and one day crows make their nests in them. Prophet Salih reminded them: "O people, remember God's marvels and His great power, and do not rise as mischief-makers in God's land." "Fadh-kuroo ala'allahi wa la ta'thaw fil-ardi mufsideen."

"Qalal-mala'ulladhina-stakbaroo min qawmihi lilladhina-stud'ifoo liman amana..." 'Mustad'af' means oppressed, suppressed, having no influence, weak. In accepting the prophets' dawah, the poor and weak always took the lead, because they are free from the veil of arrogance; arrogance is the greatest obstacle in accepting truth. We have clarified this in the story of Iblis.
"a-ta'lamoona anna Saliham-mursalum-mir-rabbih, qaloo inna bima ursila bihi mu'minoon." This question of the arrogant to the believers is in the nature of disapproval. The meaning is: "If you people have believed this person to be a Messenger of God, you are highly foolish and simpletons." The believers' answer—"Indeed, we believe in the message he has been sent with"—is one step ahead of the actual answer to the question. They did not suffice only on acknowledging the messengership of Prophet Salih, but also expressed their perfect faith in the correctness of his actual dawah and their firm resolve on this faith. Obviously, the arrogant had a grudge not against Prophet Salih himself, but against his dawah and message. Thus, the believers struck directly at the hidden malice within them without compromise, and this was like a stone in answer to a brick, proving that those who were suppressed or kept weak are no longer going to be suppressed by anyone in the matter of this truth.
From this, it is also deduced that although those who believe in the Messengers are initially poor and weak, what appeals to them is the strength and proof of the Prophet's dawah, and not wonders and miracles; on the contrary, those who claim to be great, intelligent, and wise remain caught in the circle of signs and miracles until the end, until their eyes open when God's punishment breaks their backs.

"Qalalladhina-stakbaroo inna billadhee amantum bihi kafiroon." This is the expression of the final irritation from the arrogant: "If you have believed in Salih, believe; we are by no means going to believe in this person; we flatly deny him."

"Fa'aqaroon-naqata wa 'ataw 'an amri rabbihim wa qaloo ya Salihu'tina bima ta'iduna in kunta minal-mursaleen." 'Uqr' means to cut the hamstrings of a camel. When 'an' follows 'utoo', it comprises the meaning of both rebellion and disobedience. This was the final step of rebellion from the rebels of the nation. They hamstrung the she-camel and challenged Prophet Salih: "Look, we hamstrung the she-camel; you warned us that if we caused harm to it, punishment would descend upon us; so if you are sent by God, bring the punishment."
Remember here what we pointed out above: this was the final and definitive declaration of the nation's denial of Prophet Salih. After this, if they had received further respite, their attack would have been upon Prophet Salih himself; hence, the punishment descended on the third day. Although the crime of killing the she-camel was committed, as clarified in Surah Al-Shams, by the rebel leader of Thamood, all rebels were pleased with his act; so Allah Almighty attributed it to all of them.

"Fa-akhadhathumur-rajfatu fa-asbahoo fee darihim jathimeen." 'Rajfah' means intense motion, shaking, and trembling. This is the expression of the punishment that came upon the nation of Thamood. The Qur'an has expressed this punishment in another place as 'Sayhah' (shout/blast); in some places as 'Sa'iqah' (thunderbolt) ("mithla sa'iqati 'Adiw-wa Thamood"); and in Surah Al-Haqqah as 'Taghiyah' (the overwhelming). Mawlana Farahi (may Allah have mercy on him) has discussed the nature of the punishment of both 'Ad and Thamood in the commentary of Surah Al-Dhariyat. He presents the summary of the discussion at the end in these words:
"From this detail, it is known that Allah Almighty sent upon them the punishment of freezing clouds, fierce winds, and horrific thunderclaps. Since the actual destruction was caused mostly by the actions of the wind, if the method of arguing from effect to cause is adopted, this also follows: Allah Almighty sent upon Thamood freezing clouds containing a horrific thunderclap and a deafening shout, just as He sent upon 'Ad clouds of thunder and lightning."
In any case, the word 'rajfah' does not necessarily mean an earthquake. 'Rajfah' is a general expression of punishment. It appears that the fierce northern wind, called 'Sarsar', blew and became so severe that it shook everything. The intensity of the thunderclap was so overwhelming that people fell on their knees on the ground and perished in that state. The word 'dar' (house) here is in the meaning of 'diyar' (dwellings). Thus, in Surah Hud verse 65, 'dar' was used, and then in the mention of the same Thamood in verse 67, 'diyar' was used. This is the Qur'an itself explaining the word.

"Fatawalla 'anhum wa qala ya qawmi laqad ablaghtukum risalata rabbee wa nasahtu lakum wa lakil-la tuhibboonan-nasireen."
This is the final address of Prophet Salih to the nation. Obviously, he said this before the punishment descended, at the time when the nation hamstrung the she-camel and broke the barrier of the punishment. Saying this parting sentence, he migrated with his companions, because the sunnah of Allah regarding the Messengers is that before the punishment descends, by Allah's command, they migrate from the region of punishment. In another place in the Qur'an, it is clarified that after the killing of the she-camel, the nation received only three days of respite. Within this three-day respite, along with other wisdoms, this wisdom was also present: during this period, Prophet Salih and his companions would go so far away from the region that no blast of the punishment would touch them.
Here, the question arises: why was this matter put after the mention of the punishment in the order of description? The answer is that this preceding and succeeding occurred in the order due to the requirement of eloquence. To highlight the commission of the crime and the immediate appearance of its result, the punishment was connected here with the killing of the she-camel, and the mention of Prophet Salih's migration was put afterward. It was as if as soon as they caused harm to the she-camel and challenged God, the punishment descended. This immediacy of the punishment would not have been highlighted well if some other matter had come in between. There are many examples of this style of immediacy in the Qur'an. Ahead, in the story of the nation of Shu'ayb, there is an example of it. In Surah Hud, in the story of Prophet Noah, in verses 43-45, a highly clear and eloquent example of this style is present.
From "ya qawmi laqad ablaghtukum...", another reality is also clarified: the words "ya qawm, ya qawm" (O my nation) appearing repeatedly in the address of these prophets is not merely some form of comforting; rather, just as every person has a right upon his own existence, his family, and his household, he has a right upon his own nation and his country, the requirement of which is that he perform its goodwill with complete sincerity, devotion, and sacrifice. Without this, no one is absolved of the right of his nation. Whoever fulfills this right is successful with Allah; no questioning will be made of him as to why the nation went on the path of destruction and did not adopt the path of guidance. But those who did not fulfill this right will surely be questioned about this right with Allah.
"Wa lakil-la tuhibboonan-nasireen" is not merely a statement of regret, but a statement of the reality. A stage in the moral decline of a nation comes when all scales are turned upside down and all values are so changed that saying a word of goodwill becomes a danger to life. Those who break the barrier of divine punishment become the leaders and heroes of the nation. This limit of the moral decline of a nation is the one where the nation inevitably faces destruction. The reason is obvious: when a patient's state becomes such that he turns hostile to healers and doctors, and likes only the advice of those who wish to increase his disease, what remains for him except death?

"Wa Lootan idh qala liqawmihi..."
The story of the nation of Lot and Prophet Lot (peace be upon him): Prophet Lot is the nephew of Prophet Abraham (peace be upon him). The nation to which he was sent as a Messenger dwelt in the southern region of Syria, around the Jordan River. In his mention, the style of expression is slightly different from the mention of Prophet Hud and Prophet Salih. Those two gentlemen were attributed to their nations with the words "akhahum Hooda" and "akhahum Salihan". From this, it is known that in respect of dawah, these people were their nation, but in respect of lineage and tribe, Prophet Lot was separate from them. The Qur'an highlighted this minor difference through its style of expression. The general sunnah of Allah regarding the Messengers is that they are from among the nation itself to which they are sent. The wisdom of this is that the nation is well familiar with their past, present, morals, character, and language, so that strangeness does not cause aversion. This thing, just as it can be achieved through lineage, can also be achieved through a long stay within a nation. Since Prophet Lot had settled among them—rather, the context bears witness that he married among them—he was like a member of the nation to them. His training, as is known from the Torah, was done by Prophet Abraham. Because of these qualities of his, Allah Almighty sent him as a Messenger to this nation. This is exactly the situation we see in the case of Prophet Moses: although Prophet Moses was not from the nation of Pharaoh, he lived among them for a long period; hence, Allah Almighty sent him as a Messenger to the nation of Pharaoh.
"a-ta'toonaf-fahishata ma sabaqakum biha min ahadim-minal-'alameen." 'Fahishah' refers to open indecency, and the question here is in the meaning of expressing hatred and disgust. The name of this indecency is not mentioned here, which is a context that this indecency was so common in the society of the time that everyone understood what was meant without naming it.
"Ma sabaqakum biha" is not an attribute of 'fahishah'. If it were an attribute, according to the rule of language, 'fahishah' would have been indefinite. In my view, this is a separate sentence. Prophet Lot expressed disgust at this evil from two different aspects here. First, he said: "Do you commit such open immodesty, the immodesty of which is obvious to every sound intellect?" Then he said: "This heinous act was not committed by any nation before you."
From "min ahadim-minal-'alameen", it does not follow that this heinous act was never committed by a single individual before the nation of Lot. We have clarified in another place that the word 'ahad' also comes in the plural meaning. For example: "la nufarriqu bayna ahadim-mir-rusulih", or "lastun-na ka-ahadim-minan-nisa'". For this reason, its meaning can also be: "No ill-fated society existed before you which made this filth its bedding in the same way you have."
The gravity of the act of the nation of Lot: Here, the question arises: the dawah of all the Messengers mentioned above began with monotheism, but Prophet Lot, instead of beginning with the dawah of monotheism, first made this immodesty of the nation the subject of discussion. Was the reason for this that the evil of Shirk was not present in the nation of Prophet Lot? If it was present, why did Prophet Lot ignore it? In our view, the answer is that there is a difference between evil and evil. One evil, however grave it may be, is among human weaknesses and is found or can be found in humans; another evil is such that its heinousness is so obvious that its being found as a habit in a human and as a fashion in a society is impossible without that human's and that society's nature being completely distorted. Where one has to deal with such people, the primary thing worthy of attention is that very evil, and all other matters, however important, become secondary. If you go to a person for his reform and see him eating filth, will you preach faith and Islam to him, or will his shocking state attract your attention first? Prophet Lot faced this very situation. The evil of Shirk and disbelief was present in his nation, and all other evils which are concomitants of Shirk were also present; but those whose nature had become so inverted that men made men the object of their lust—they first required to be taken out of this swamp of filth, and the stage of discussing other matters could only come after that.
From this, estimate what is the state of the gravity of the act of the nation of Lot in the eyes of the Qur'an, and then remember that event: the British Parliament passed a law some time ago making this accursed act completely permissible for its nation. There is no limit to God's patience. He gives respite, and He gives long respite; but the British Parliament, by passing this law, opened its gates wide for God's punishment. Now see how long this period of respite remains.

"Innakum lata'toonar-rijala shahwatam-min doonin-nisa', bal antum qawmum-musrifoon." This is the third aspect of expressing disgust: "You have completely inverted nature." The emphasis in the sentence highlights the intensity of disgust and wonder. The word 'rijal' (men) here also carries significance, because 'rijal' refers to mature men. The use of this word highlights both the heinousness of this act and the loss of honor (Dayoothiyyah) which inevitably arises in a nation when this disease becomes common. When it becomes common, the distinction of age is completely lost; then all kinds of dishonorable people spread in the nation, and this curse becomes a habit and trade for them. The words "min doonid-nisa'" (besides women) show that inversion of nature which is the necessary result of this corruption: the entire creative power is wasted on a completely wrong target. Plain fields are irrigated, and crops dry up, the result of which is the destruction of lineage and the blackening of the face in the Hereafter. "Bal antum qawmum-musrifoon" is an expression of rebellion against nature, and the end of rebellion against nature is obvious.

"Wa ma kana jawaba qawmihi illa an qaloo akhrijoohum-min qaryatikum, innahum unasuy-yatatahharoon." It is clear from the style of expression that every sentence of Prophet Lot (peace be upon him) was eye-opening and nature-shaking; but on those whose minds were corrupted and whose nature was distorted, what effect could this preaching have? When they spoke after hearing all this, they said: "Expel them from the town, they act very pure!" This is the final limit of the corruption of a society. A society declines to the extent that a time comes when all cut their noses, and being noseless becomes the requirement of civilization and fashion. At that time, not having a nose on the face is not considered a defect, but having one is considered a wonder; and such people, if the wrongdoers have their way, are expelled from the community. From the word "yatatahharoon" (they purify themselves), it is deduced that they considered themselves polluted in filth and Prophet Lot and his companions pure, but this purity of theirs, instead of being appreciated, was heavy on their hearts: 'Why do they remain safe from it? We are dipping in this cesspool; why do they not enter it?'

"Fa-anjaynahu wa ahlahu illam-ra'atahu, kanat minal-ghabireen." From "innahum unasuy-yatatahharoon" above, it is inferred that some people had believed in Prophet Lot (peace be upon him), but from this verse, it is known that none except his family became believers in him. If some others had believed, they would have been mentioned among the saved. Our view is that a small group, as shown by the word 'unas', believed in him, but these people were all among his dependents; hence, the Qur'an expressed them with the word 'Ahl' (family).
From this 'Ahl', the wife of Prophet Lot was excluded. The reason was obviously the corruption of her nature. This corruption of nature must have been strengthened by the fact that she belonged, as we pointed out, to this very wicked nation. Family and national prejudice has often been an obstacle in the path of truth. Only those have succeeded in breaking this chain whose devotion to God was so intense that it overcame every opposing emotion. The Qur'an mentioned this case specifically to highlight the reality that the path of salvation opens only for those who seek it themselves and are ready to sacrifice other desires which are obstacles in its path. If this love for truth and sacrifice is not present in someone, being the wife of a Prophet, the son of a Prophet, the father or uncle of a Prophet does not benefit him at all. The end of the wife of Prophet Lot, the son of Prophet Noah, the father of Prophet Abraham, and the uncle of the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is an undeniable testimony to this reality.

"Wa amtarna 'alayhim-matara, fandhur kayfa kana 'aqibatul-mureemin."
The punishment of the nation of Lot: By 'rain' (Matar) here is not meant the rain of water, but the rain of pebbles and stones which rises from deserts, and under whose sand, pebbles, and stones caravans and towns are buried. In Arabic, this is called 'Hasib', i.e., a wind carrying pebbles and stones. Mawlana Farahi has described the nature of this punishment in the commentary of Surah Al-Dhariyat as follows:
"Upon the nation of Lot, Allah Almighty sent a wind which became severe and eventually turned into a 'Hasib' (wind carrying pebbles and stones). From this, first, the rain of pebbles and stones fell upon them, then it became so severe that their houses were inverted. Thus, pointing to them, He said: 'Faminhum-man arsalna 'alayhi hasiba' (Of them were some upon whom We sent a wind carrying pebbles and stones) (Al-Ankabut: 40). He also said: 'Faja'alna 'aleeha safeelaha wa amtarna 'alayhim hijaratam-min sijjeelim-mandood' (So We made it upside down and rained upon them stones of layered clay) (Al-Hijr: 74). That is, such a fierce wind blew that their houses became level with the ground, and pebbles and sand covered them from above. As He said: 'Wal-mu'tafikata ahwa, faghashaha ma ghasha' (And the inverted cities He overthrew, and covered them by that which covered) (Al-Najm: 54)."
Ahead, Mawlana Farahi analyzed the translations of the Torah and clarified how through translation errors, they changed the state of the event; then he presents the summary of the discussion in these words:
"From this, it is known that upon the nation of Lot, Allah Almighty sent the punishment of a wind carrying pebbles and stones which covered them and their houses; and if the statement of the Torah is also combined with it, it would be added that the punishment of lightning and thunderclap also came upon them."

"Wa ila Madyana akhahum Shu'ayba..."
The nation of Madyan and the story of Prophet Shu'ayb (peace be upon him): The dwelling of the nation of Madyan was on the coast of the Red Sea, in the south-east of Mount Tur. Their Prophet was Prophet Shu'ayb. He is the same Prophet Shu'ayb who married one of his daughters to Prophet Moses, and Prophet Moses stayed with him for a long period and herded his sheep. This entire story is narrated in Surah Al-Qasas, and is a highly pure and effective story of the pure servants of Allah.
The people of Madyan, being on an important commercial route, made great progress in trade, and then, along with doctrinal errors, those evils also arose in them which arise in trading nations. Ahead, the dawah of Prophet Shu'ayb mentions these evils of theirs.
"qala ya qawmi'budullaha ma lakum-min ilahin ghayruh, qad ja'atkum bayyinatum-mir-rabbikum." The 'Bayyinah' (clear proof) mentioned here is not detailed in the Qur'an or the Torah. It is possible that it refers to some physical miracle which, according to the general sunnah of Allah, was granted to him as to other Messengers, but the Qur'an only pointed to it, without deemed it necessary to clarify its nature; and it is also possible that it refers to his own person and his dawah, because the Messenger himself is a perfect proof and perfect argument in his person. He clarifies the truth from every aspect in such a manner that no room remains for anyone except the obstinate and prejudiced to turn away. Miracles are not among the conditions of prophethood, but among the requirements and concomitants. If a nation demands, Allah Almighty, if He wills, causes a miracle to appear through the Prophet as a completion of proof; the Prophet's actual dawah is based on those clear proofs which are deposited within intellect and nature, and his life is the perfect manifestation of these proofs. He is a walking light of God in his nation. Every word from his tongue, his every gesture, and his every deed is such a testimony to his truth, wisdom, and his being an ambassador of God that his denial is made only by those who wish to deny despite the testimony of their own hearts.
"fa-awful-kayla wal-meezana wa la tabkhasun-nasa ash-ya'ahum wa la tufsidoo fil-ardi ba'da islahiha."
Trustworthiness and honesty in dealings: Since commercial transactions and trade involve measuring or weighing of things, because of this dominant aspect, weight and measure alone were mentioned. The actual purpose is the teaching of trustworthiness and honesty in dealings. Shortness in weight and measure is not an isolated evil; rather, it is a sign of the gathering of many evils in a nation. It is in fact a sign of the corruption of the conception of justice and the death of the belief in God establishing justice, the corruption of which inevitably spreads to every aspect of life; but like a cancerous tumor, it appears in one person from one aspect, and in another from another aspect. Since the people of Madyan were traders, it appeared in them in the form of betrayal in weight and measure, but it was spreading in their entire system of civilization. For this reason, the Qur'an, along with emphasizing weight and measure, also clarified from the negative aspect: "do not create corruption in the land," meaning this shortness in weight and measure is equivalent to ruining the entire system of economy and society. If this way of yours does not change, eventually no brick of the building of civilization will remain in its place, because God has established the heavens and the earth upon a balance (Mizan). If this balance is ruined for even a single moment, the heavens and the earth will be ruined. This is an indication that the servants can keep the system of the country established only as long as they keep weight and justice established; if they ruin weight and justice, then every joint of the entire system will shake. In Surah Al-Rahman, this reality is pointed to as: "Was-sama'a rafa'aha wa wada'al-mizan, alla tatghaw fil-mizan, wa aqeemul-wazna bil-qisti wa la tukhsirul-mizan" (And the heaven He raised and imposed the balance, that you not transgress within the balance, and establish weight in justice and do not make the balance deficient) (Al-Rahman: 7-9).
A cause of the corruption of civilization: The condition "ba'da islahiha" contains intense force, the clarification of which has passed. Here, after all the above discussion, its meaning became clearer. Above, you saw that nations, because of their rebellion, repeatedly ruined the system of truth and justice, and the hand of nature corrected it each time. From the story of Prophet Noah (peace be upon him) to the story of Prophet Shu'ayb (peace be upon him), the history of every nation and every Messenger is a witness to this reality. The people of Madyan too had gained authority after destroying a corrupt authority. They were not people unfamiliar with religion and shariah; rather, they claimed to be related to the family of Prophet Abraham (peace be upon him) and to carry the teachings of past prophets; but like other nations, they had cast these teachings into the mold of their desires. Thus, on this basis, Prophet Shu'ayb addressed them: "dhalikum khayrul-lakum in kuntum-mu'mineen." If you claim faith, the truth of the claim requires that you abandon this way of corruption and adopt that way of reform to which I am inviting. This way alone is the requirement of the faith you claim.

"Wa la taq'udoo bikulli siratint-too'idoona wa tasuddoona 'an sabeelillahi man amana bihi wa tabghoonaha 'iwaja..." This points to those activities of the rebels of the nation which they adopted to harass, frighten, and turn away the companions of Prophet Shu'ayb from the path of faith. It appears that bullies sat at every corner, and wherever a Muslim was found, they turned hostile to him, threatening him so that he would leave the support of Prophet Shu'ayb and the path of faith to return to their way. We have clarified "tabghoonaha 'iwaja" in another place. The path to reach God is monotheism. Creating crookedness in this path means turning it toward the paths of Shirk and putting people onto the paths of deviation from monotheism.
"wadh-kuroo idh kuntum qaleelan fakath-tharakum, wandhuroo kayfa kana 'aqibatul-mufsideen." In the first sentence is the invitation to gratitude, and in the second sentence is the indication of the end of ungratefulness. The meaning is: "You had settled in this region as a small family. Then God made you a powerful nation. Along with your numbers, He also increased your resources of livelihood and civilization until you reached the status you possess today. All this is God's gift, and its natural requirement is to be grateful to God and adopt that way of life which God prefers. But you, contrary to this, adopted ungratefulness and rebellion, and are creating corruption in God's land after its reform; so do not forget the end of the mischief-makers. To your right and left are the remnants of the destruction of Thamood and the nation of Lot. Take warning from their end."

"Wa in kana ta'ifatum-minkum amanoo billadhee ursiltu bihi wa ta'ifatul-lam yu'minoo fasbiroo hatta yahkumallahu baynana, wa huwa khayrul-hakimeen." This is the removal of a doubt regarding the end of the mischief-makers of which they were warned above. The meaning is: "The end of the mischief-makers is definitive and certain; do not think from the respite you are receiving that my word is false. There is delay in God's house, but not injustice. The reason Allah has given you respite until now is that a group from among you has believed in my word. Allah wishes that those among you who have some capacity also separate. So for their sake, you are receiving this respite. Wait until this respite, after which God will decide between us and you, and He is the best of judges."
The law of respite for the mischief-makers: Prophet Shu'ayb pointed here to a divine sunnah. Its detail is: one form is that the dominant majority of the nation denies the Messenger's dawah and persists in this denial. In this case, it becomes clear quickly that this nation is completely devoid of the capacity of faith; hence, as a necessary result, the punishment also comes quickly upon it. The second form is that a group from within the nation believes, and another group opposes it. This form is a context that some capacity is present in this nation; perhaps if it is further refined, more butter will emerge. Since Allah Almighty is highly merciful to His servants and slow in wrath, He gives respite to the nation in such a case until the Prophet and the righteous sift their nation well in their sieve. After this process is completed, Allah Almighty decides the matter of the nation. From this verse, this indication also emerges: the nation receives this respite through the blessing of those righteous who believe in the Messenger's dawah from within it. 'Fasbiroo' (so be patient) here is in the meaning of waiting, and in 'khayrul-hakimeen', there is an indication that His decision will be based entirely on truth and justice, and exactly at the time when it should be.

"Qalal-mala'ulladhina..." This is the final threat from the arrogant of the nation to Prophet Shu'ayb and his companions: "Either you return to our creed, or we will expel you and your companions from our town." Upon this, Prophet Shu'ayb (peace be upon him) said: "a-walaw kunna kariheen" (Even if we are unwilling?), meaning: "Will you force us to return to your creed contrary to our desire and will?" From this statement of Prophet Shu'ayb (peace be upon him), it is clearly apparent that he completely ignored the threat of expulsion from the country, but took notice of their second threat and answered it in very clear and definitive words, the details of which are in the succeeding verse.

"Qad-iftarayna 'alallahi kadhiban in 'udna fee millatikum ba'da idh najjanallahu minha..." This is a highly decisive answer. In it, there is both the expression of unshakeable resolve on faith and the declaration of absolute disgust at the creed of disbelief. Prophet Shu'ayb first declared that creed as a fabrication against Allah, i.e., a heap of lies which has been attributed to God completely without authority. Second, he said: "When Allah, through His grace and mercy, saved us from this curse, what is the question of our falling into it again?" It should be kept in mind that Prophet Shu'ayb is giving this answer also on behalf of those companions of his who, before believing in Prophet Shu'ayb, were involved in the general deviation of the nation; hence, he used the words "after Allah saved us from it"; as far as the prophets are concerned, they are on the guidance of nature even before their sending, and their daman is never stained with Shirk.
"wa ma yakoonu lana an na'ooda feeha illa ay-yasha'allahu rabbuna." In the first part of the sentence is the expression of resolve, and in the second is the surrender to God's will (Tafweedh), and this resolve and surrender is the reality of monotheism. The meaning is: "As far as we are concerned, hear clearly: now there is no possibility of returning to your creed. But the will of Allah is dominant over everything. Emerging successful in His trials depends on the success He blesses; only He knows what is destined for whom and what the end of whom is to be. 'Wasi'a rabbuna kulla shay'in 'ilma'—so our trust is only in Allah. He alone guided us to this path, and from Him alone is the hope that He will bring us to the destination. 'Alallahi tawakkalna'—so our supplication to Him alone is: O our Lord, decide between us and our nation in truth, and You are the best of deciders."
"Rabbanaf-tah baynana wa bayna qawmina bil-haqqi wa anta khayrul-fatiheen." This exact same thing was stated by Prophet Abraham (peace be upon him), which passed in Surah Al-An'am: "Qala a-tuhajjoonanee fillahi wa qad hadan, wa la akhafu ma tushrikoona bihi illa ay-yasha'a rabbee shay'a, wasi'a rabbee kulla shay'in 'ilma" (He said, 'Do you argue with me concerning Allah while He has guided me? And I do not fear what you associate with Him unless my Lord should will something. My Lord encompasses all things in knowledge').

"Wa qalal-mala'ulladhina kafaroo min qawmihi la'init-taba'tum Shu'ayban innakum idhal-lakhasiroon." After the aforementioned decisive answer of Prophet Shu'ayb, although no room remained for these rebels to say anything, in passing, they gave a final threat to those poor Muslims who had believed in Prophet Shu'ayb: "If you people do not leave the support of this person, remember that you will be in great loss." They did not clarify this loss, because in its vagueness, everything is hidden, and the use of this word also displays the show of sympathy. The meaning is: "We have explained the good and bad to you, but this matter of your welfare is not entering your understanding; so bear its consequences yourselves."

The punishment of the people of Shu‘ayb: "So the earthquake seized them, and they lay lifeless in their dwellings." This very verse has already passed above in the story of the people of Ṣāliḥ. There we mentioned that it is an expression for a general punishment. As for the question of the exact nature of this punishment, this verse itself does not clarify it. The punishment is described in Surah Hūd, verse 94, with the word "ṣayḥah" (the blast/shout), which carries the sense of a roar or thunder-clap. Then in Surah ash-Shu‘arā’, verse 189, it is described as the punishment of the Day of the Shade, which indicates that from a distance it appeared like a pillar of dust or smoke or in the shape of a mountain. This clue, as we have already explained in the story of the people of Lot, indicates the punishment of ḥāsib. In the punishment of ḥāsib, the earthquake, the cry, and the shade all come together. Its details have already been mentioned above. Another indication from the قرآن suggests that they too were struck by the same punishment that befell the people of Lot. In Surah Hūd, the warning quoted from the tongue of Prophet Shu‘ayb to his people is: 'O my people, let not my opposition lead you to a punishment like that which befell the people of Noah, or the people of Hūd, or the people of Ṣāliḥ; and the people of Lot are not far from you.' Reflect carefully on the final phrase of this verse and it suggests that these people too fell under the same ḥāsib that struck their neighboring predecessor nation, the people of Lot.

"Those who denied Shu‘ayb were as though they had never lived there; those who denied Shu‘ayb, they were the losers." This states the result of that punishment, and both phrases allude to the two threats given by the unbelievers of Shu‘ayb to Prophet Shu‘ayb and his companions. They threatened to expel them from their town. Referring to that, it is said that they themselves were obliterated from that land as if no oil had ever remained in those lamp-wicks. They also threatened the companions of Prophet Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) that if they did not abandon following that man, they would be ruined badly. Referring to that, it is said that those who rejected Shu‘ayb were the ones who suffered loss.

"So he turned away from them and said, O my people, I certainly delivered to you the messages of my Lord." This very verse has already passed above in the story of Ṣāliḥ with slight differences in wording. See verse 79. There we explained its context in detail. Prophet Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) actually said this when, after receiving the command to migrate from God, he announced his disavowal and migrated. But to show the suddenness of the punishment, it was mentioned after the punishment. The Qur'an contains countless examples of this elegant style, which have already passed and will continue to appear ahead. 
As for 'How then should I grieve over a disbelieving people,' it does not negate sorrow altogether, but rather sympathetic sorrow. The meaning is: I delivered to you the message of God and devoted my days and nights to your well-being. Even after all that, if you are bent on destroying yourselves, then what place is left for me to pity your fate?

Theme of verses 94 to 102: The purpose of narrating all the previous stories was to inform Quraysh that the same criterion by which those nations were tested is now before you, and if you deny your Messenger, you too will see the same end they saw. Therefore, the coming verses lay before their eyes the principles and rules that emerge from the above historical record, so that Quraysh may take any step while being fully aware of its consequences.
Tafseer of verses 94–95: 'We did not send a prophet to any town but We seized its people with hardship and adversity so that they might humble themselves. Then We replaced evil with good until they multiplied and said, "Our fathers too were afflicted by hardship and ease." Then We seized them suddenly while they were unaware.'
Meaning of ba'sā' and ḍarrā': The analysis of ba'sā' and ḍarrā' has already been given under An‘ām 6:42. When these two words are used in opposition to one another, the first refers to financial calamities, such as famine, inflation, recession, and the like, while the second refers to bodily calamities, such as diseases and epidemics. But when ḍarrā' is used with سراء (ease/prosperity), then both words encompass every kind of hardship and prosperity. The phrase 'an‘a al-shay' kathura wa ṭāla' means a thing grew and flourished; 'an‘at al-arḍ, ghatāhā al-nabāt' means the earth became covered with vegetation.
The echo of the call to repentance across horizons: This describes a divine practice that necessarily appears with the sending of prophets and was manifested in all the times of the prophets whose stories have been narrated above. That practice is that when a prophet begins calling to repentance, forgiveness, reward, and punishment, its supporting signs begin to appear in this universe as well. On one side, the Messenger warns people of the consequences of heedlessness, forgetting God, corruption on earth, and punishment in this world and the next. On the other side, God Almighty also subjects people to tests of flood, famine, plague, and storm so that people may also see with their own eyes—if they have eyes to learn lessons—that God can seize them whenever and wherever He wills, and then no one besides God can save them. Thus, the call is supported by events, and the testimony of observation joins the testimony of reason and nature. What the prophet says is as though heaven and earth themselves display its scenes on their stage so that those who still retain some responsiveness may bow before God and repent and reform.
Another turn of trial: Those who have eyes to see and hearts to receive effect benefit from such trials and turn to God. They seem to hear the echo of the prophet’s call from all elements of the cosmos, and not only hear it with their ears but also see it with their eyes. But those whose hearts have become stone and whose ears have become deaf, for them this divine practice takes on a different form. God grants them respite. Hardship is replaced by prosperity, signs of growth and abundance become evident in every area of worldly means and resources, and the days of trial and suffering are forgotten. Then rebellious people begin to boast and ridicule the prophet and his companions, saying—God forbid—these narrow-minded people used to consider the previous famine or flood or calamity as a divine warning about the فساد of our conduct and beliefs, and they kept warning and discouraging us in all kinds of ways. Yet what do such matters have to do with faith and morality? Such fluctuations occur in the life of nations. Such days have not only passed over us; they passed over our fathers and forefathers too, who were good and virtuous people. These are merely accidents of time: sometimes difficulty, sometimes ease; sometimes a good harvest, sometimes ruin; sometimes flood, sometimes famine. To link these things with deeds and morals is nothing but folly and superstition.
As the purpose of disasters and afflictions was stated to be to shake and awaken people—'so that they might humble themselves'—similarly, the purpose of respite and gradual increase is that those who did not awaken from these warnings may become intoxicated and sink into deep sleep, so that God’s punishment seizes them while they do not know when or from where it came.
Warnings common to both the righteous and the wicked: One thing to remember here is that the trials and calamities that come to produce humility before God, or in Qur'anic terms tawaddu‘/taḍarru‘, are general and common. Both the righteous and the wicked are tested by them. This becomes a source of confusion and misguidance for the ignorant and the rebellious. They think that since famine or plague made no distinction between faith and disbelief, character and character, and struck us just as it struck our critics and advisers, how can we accept that it has any connection to disbelief and faith, or beliefs and deeds? They think this would only make sense if the prophet and his companions had been exempted from famine or calamity in such a way that they enjoyed easy food and milk while we were chewing dry leather. But since that did not happen and both we and they were afflicted in our own ways, how can it be said that these afflictions have anything to do with human goodness or wickedness? In truth, this is the misconception that blinds today’s intoxicated people just as it blinded the opponents of the prophets. They see a cyclone rise and sweep away both good and bad indiscriminately. An earthquake comes and levels temples, mosques, and churches alike. Famine comes and includes both the praying and the non-praying, the sinful and the believer. Such things become a trial for them. First, they do not draw any proper lesson from them; and even if, temporarily, their hearts become somewhat softened, the effect does not last. Very soon desires drag them back into the same heedlessness and intoxication they had previously lived in, and their satanic guide teaches them again that old pagan philosophy just mentioned: 'Our fathers too were touched by hardship and prosperity'—cold and hot days always come upon nations. To remain safe from this satanic misconception, one must always keep in mind this reality: in warning calamities, although outwardly there is no distinction between the good and the evil, since their purpose is not to punish but to produce humility, the results are very different. If believers receive a share in such calamities, their humility increases, which compensates for shortcomings and raises their ranks. In contrast, those in whom such events do not produce humility have the proof of God completed against them, and then when the decisive punishment comes, it annihilates them. Those who remain are only those tasked with reforming evil.

"If only the people of the towns had believed and had taqwa, We would have opened for them blessings from the heaven and the earth; but they denied, so We seized them for what they used to earn." The people of the towns refers to the same nations whose stories of denial were narrated above. It is said that if these people had believed and adopted the path of God-consciousness, God would not have dealt with them by giving respite and then seizing them in punishment; rather, He would have opened for them the gates of His endless blessings from both heaven and earth.
Two important realities: Here two realities have become clear. First, the abundance of wealth and resources that a nation gets as respite is not God’s mercy and blessing, but rather it is like the final strengthening of a patient before the last gasp that ends his life. Just as a candle flares up once before going out, then goes out, so such a nation flares up one last time and is extinguished. Second, the life of faith and taqwa is, as it guarantees God’s mercy in the Hereafter, likewise in this world if any nation adopts it, it becomes the key to all blessings and success from heaven and earth. The ignorant are those who think that piety and God-consciousness stand in the way of worldly success.

Tafseer of verses 97–99: 'Did the people of the towns feel secure that Our punishment would come to them by night while they were asleep? Or did the people of the towns feel secure that Our punishment would come to them in the morning while they were playing? Did they feel secure against God’s plan? No one feels secure from God’s plan except the losers.'
God’s seizing is unpreventable: The reference to the people of the towns points to the same nations mentioned above. Referring to them, Quraysh are made to reflect: can they put any barrier to protect themselves against God’s punishment? Our punishment came to them in the darkness of night while they were asleep, and it came openly in the day while they were busy with their amusements and activities, but neither at night nor in the day could they save themselves from it. God’s plan is unpreventable. No one has the power to protect themselves from it. Therefore, it is not lawful for anyone to feel secure from it. Only those feel secure from it whose destruction has already approached and who are destined to fail. 'Makr'—as we have explained elsewhere—means secret planning. Secret planning means that God seizes from where no one even suspects they will be seized. The respite after warning described above is an example of this divine secret strategy. A nation thinks it has now gained the upper hand, but in reality it is digging the pit of its own destruction.

"Did it not guide those who inherit the earth after its former people that if We willed, We could afflict them for their sins, and seal their hearts so they would not hear?" The phrase 'those who inherit the earth' refers here to Quraysh. It says: why do those who have inherited rule and power in this land after the nations mentioned above not learn from their forefathers’ history? Why do they not understand that the same scales and weights by which God judged those earlier nations will also judge them? Why do they not think that just as We destroyed them for their crimes, so too, whenever We will, We can destroy these people as well. The phrase 'We seal their hearts so they would not hear' occurs in the same context as in Surah al-An‘ām where it is said: 'Say: Have you considered—if Allah were to take away your hearing and your sight and seal your hearts, who besides Allah could restore them to you?' Let it be noted that the sealing of the hearts is the prelude to God’s punishment. Before divine punishment descends upon a nation, a seal is placed upon its hearts. We have explained God’s law on this matter at many places. That is the threat given here to Quraysh: until now We have not sealed their hearts, but with the stubbornness and obstinacy they continue to exhibit, the time is not far when We will seal their hearts and they will become completely unable to hear or understand. This would be the prelude to Our punishment, after which Our decisive punishment will surely come down.

"Those towns—we relate to you some of their news. Their messengers came to them with clear proofs, but they were not such as to believe in what they had previously denied. Thus does Allah seal the hearts of the disbelievers." When does the sealing of hearts occur? On what kind of people does it occur? And what is the final result? This verse answers these questions. It says that the stories of the nations We have narrated to you should serve as lessons. Their messengers came to them with clear signs from God, but they did not believe in them. The reason was that they had already been denying God’s signs and verses. It is human nature that when a person becomes habituated to turning away from God’s general signs, little by little his heart becomes so hardened that even when special signs appear through the messengers, they no longer affect him. This is how the hearts are sealed in Our way. The heart’s job is to learn lessons, contemplate, and reason. If a group becomes such blind and deaf followers of their desires that even after reminders and warnings they do not open the eyes of the heart, then such people’s hearts are eventually sealed completely. Note that 'bimā kadhdhabū' is not the object of 'yu’minū'; rather, the particle 'bi' indicates the cause.

"We did not find most of them true to any covenant, and indeed we found most of them defiantly disobedient." This states the result and effect of the sealing of hearts: when God seals a group’s hearts, they become so stubborn that no matter what signs you show them, they do not budge. They will swear repeatedly that if such and such a miracle were shown, they would believe, but if that miracle is shown, they demand yet another miracle and go back on their previous pledge. The word 'fāsiq' here means one who breaks covenants. The same theme is stated more clearly in Surah al-An‘ām: 'They swore by Allah their strongest oaths that if a sign came to them they would surely believe in it. Say: Signs are only with Allah. And what will make you realize that even if it comes, they will not believe? We will turn their hearts and their eyes just as they did not believe in it the first time, and We will leave them in their rebellion, wandering blindly' (6:109–110). The same reality is also stated in the story of Moses and Pharaoh: 'When the punishment fell upon them, they said: O Moses, pray to your Lord for us according to the promise He has made with you. If you remove this punishment from us, we will surely believe you and let the Children of Israel go with you. But when We removed the punishment for a term they were to reach, they broke their word at once.'

Theme of verses 103 to 171: Next, the story of Moses and Pharaoh, and along with it the complete history of the religious and political rise and decline of the Children of Israel, is presented in brief but highly comprehensive form. From a historical perspective, this is the completion and conclusion of the stories narrated above. The earlier stories ended with the story of Shu‘ayb. In historical sequence, after that begins the history of Moses’ mission, when he was sent as a messenger to Pharaoh and his people. Through Moses, God’s proof was completed against Pharaoh and his people, and in consequence of their denial, Pharaoh and his people met the same end that had been narrated earlier from the people of Noah to the people of Shu‘ayb. On the other hand, the Children of Israel emerged as a distinct nation, and gradually God granted them the office of religious leadership and also political power and prestige. But they did not value that office and power, and little by little fell into even more serious evils than those of earlier nations. After the mission of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), just as the fate of Quraysh came under the divine scale of justice, so too the fate of the Children of Israel was now put to the test. The time of decision had come for them: if they did not believe in this unlettered Prophet, in accordance with the covenant bound with God—on whom testimony had been taken through the Torah, all previous scriptures, and the prophets—then they too would suffer the same fate as earlier nations. In this light, recite the coming verses:
'Then after them We sent Moses with Our signs to Pharaoh and his chiefs, but they ظلموا with them. So see how was the end of the corrupters.'
The pronoun in 'min ba‘dihim' refers to those messengers and their nations mentioned from verse 59 to verse 92 above. 
By 'āyātinā' are meant both the miracles shown by Moses to Pharaoh and his people, and also the natural and rational proofs of tawḥīd, Resurrection, and Prophethood that Moses and Aaron presented very clearly before Pharaoh and his courtiers. These proofs are mentioned throughout the Qur'an. God willing, we will shed light on them in the commentary of Surah Ṭā Hā.
The call of Moses was in harmony with the call of other prophets: The دعوت of the prophets is fundamentally based on the clear proofs of reason and nature. Sensory miracles are not among the essential requirements of prophethood, but rather among its incidental accompaniments. To complete the proof against obstinate and stubborn people, God grants prophets such miracles too. Later, at the appropriate place, we will clarify that since Moses (peace be upon him) was sent as a messenger to Pharaoh and his people, his responsibility was not limited merely to liberating the Children of Israel from Pharaoh’s grip; he was also responsible for inviting Pharaoh to faith and submission. The Qur'an establishes that he fulfilled this duty, although Pharaoh and his people did not believe. Only a few believed, and they also emigrated with Moses. For now, suffice with this brief indication. Details will come in later surahs. In the Torah, because of the particular mindset of its compilers, the story of Moses became the story of a nationalist leader. Whereas Moses was a noble prophet and messenger. How could his mission and method possibly be different from those of other prophets and messengers?
As for 'fa-ẓalamū bihā', wherever the preposition 'bi' comes with 'ẓalamū' in the Qur'an, it carries the meaning of 'kafarū' and 'jahadū' etc., meaning they wronged their own souls and denied God’s signs. This style of Arabic has been explained elsewhere. In translation, we have unpacked the implied meaning.
The wisdom in detailing the story of Moses and Pharaoh: The stories of the Arab nations narrated above are now being completed with the story of Moses, the people of Pharaoh, and the Children of Israel. This story, as you have seen, is narrated with relative detail. One reason is that this was not some forgotten tale of the past; its historical record, though altered and distorted, existed in the Torah, and the Children of Israel—who claimed to be its custodians—were also present before the مخاطبين. Another reason is that it was the Children of Israel from whom God took back the trust of His religion and law, transferring that trust to this ummah. Therefore every part of this story carries a lesson for this ummah and for the Children of Israel.
The phrase 'so see how was the end of the corrupters' points to the real purpose of narrating this story, so that the reader’s attention does not drift away from the main objective. As explained while discussing the central theme of this surah, the Quraysh are being informed—through the lens of history—that the advent of a messenger among them has now placed them under the divine scale of justice. If they deny this messenger, they will be counted among the corrupters on God’s earth, and their end will be the same as that of the earlier corrupters.

"It is incumbent upon me to say nothing about Allah except the truth. I have come to you with clear proof from your Lord, so let the Children of Israel go with me." Meaning of 'ḥaqīqun ‘alā': Ḥaqīq is on the pattern of fa‘īl from ḥaqqa and ḥuqqa, and carries a passive sense. It means worthy, fit, and deserving. For example, one says: huwa ḥaqīqun bih, meaning he is fit and deserving of it. If '‘alā' comes after it, as here, then—as the author of Aqrab al-Mawārid has stated explicitly—its meaning becomes 'ḥarīṣ' (keen, eager). In my view, the addition of '‘alā' to its original sense introduces an additional nuance, and this is under the principle of taḍmīn mentioned earlier in the phrase 'ẓalamū bihā'. In light of this explanation, I translate 'ḥaqīqun ‘alā an lā aqūla ‘alā Allāh illā al-ḥaqq' as: 'I am fit and eager not to attribute to God anything except what is true.' It is obvious that the one who is God’s messenger and envoy is the one most fit to translate God accurately and not attribute invented things to Him, because his knowledge is not based on conjecture or opinion but on direct divine revelation and speech, and because of the responsibilities of his office he is also very keen that nothing contrary to truth should come from his tongue, since the accountability he fears—or may fear—is unlike that of anyone else.
'Qad ji'tukum bibayyinatim min rabbikum': The clear proof here refers to the staff and the white hand miracle with which God armed him and sent him to Pharaoh.
A special matter regarding miracles with Moses: The Torah indicates that Moses, being fully aware of Pharaoh’s tyranny and rebellion, was greatly anxious when commanded to go to Pharaoh by God. He said that Pharaoh and his courtiers would not listen to him unless he was granted a clear sign capable of intimidating them. Thus, because of this special situation, God granted Moses these miracles from the very beginning, and because Moses knew the mindset of Pharaoh and his entourage, he displayed those miracles in the first meeting so that something might strike the arrogance of the Pharaohs and incline them to listen.
As for 'Send the Children of Israel with me,' two questions arise. First, did Moses not present any argument to Pharaoh and his courtiers, but simply place this demand before them without introduction? Second, where did Moses want to take the Children of Israel? The answer to the first question is that the Qur'an narrates the stories of prophets and nations in different surahs, each in fragments according to the central theme and purpose of the surah. In every surah, only that part of the story is highlighted which serves to illuminate the surah’s subject; the rest appears in other surahs where appropriate. Thus, in other surahs the call to fear God and remember Him—according to the general practice of the prophets—is also mentioned, as is the dialogue concerning tawḥīd and the Hereafter between Moses and Pharaoh. God willing, these matters will be discussed in Surah Ṭā Hā or another suitable place.
As for the second question, the Qur'an gives no clarification about where Moses intended to take the Children of Israel. Studying the Book of Exodus in the Torah shows that Moses initially presented the request to Pharaoh in the form of allowing the Children of Israel to go with him for worship. Pharaoh flatly refused, and in anger he further intensified the forced labor and hardship imposed on the Children of Israel, saying that they had become lazy and idle and were seeking excuses for worship and such things. Then, when he was exhausted by Moses’ miracles and softened somewhat, he asked where they intended to go for worship, and why could they not worship in this very city? Moses replied that they would go out into the wilderness for three days’ journey for this worship, because they could not perform it here, since if they were to offer the sacrifice they wished to offer here, the Egyptians would stone them. This dispute continued for a long time between Moses and Pharaoh. Ultimately, worn down by the calamities that emerged through Moses’ miracles, the courtiers compelled Pharaoh to let the Children of Israel go wherever they wished, or else Egypt would be destroyed by Moses. Pharaoh was forced to allow them to leave, but when Moses went out with all his people—men, children, livestock, and all provisions and belongings—he realized he had made a mistake in granting permission. So he pursued them with his entire army to force their return, but that pursuit ended in Pharaoh and his troops drowning in the sea.
This shows that Moses did not reveal his full plan to Pharaoh and his courtiers. He only indicated that he wished to go into the wilderness for three days to worship God and offer sacrifice, and specifically wished to offer cow sacrifice, from which the Egyptians had deprived the Children of Israel, just as Hindus in India have deprived Muslims. Moses wanted to take the Children of Israel away from the slave-like Egyptian environment, organize them, and revive within them the religious traditions that had become entirely dead under Egypt’s subjugation. It is our conjecture that for this purpose he intended to go to that region of Sinai where he saw the divine manifestation upon his return from Midian, and where he later received the tablets of the Ten Commandments during this migration journey. The Torah indicates that several years were spent in persuading Pharaoh to accept this request. During that period, through his many miracles and his calling arguments, Moses completed the proof of God against Pharaoh and the Egyptians on the one hand, and on the other hand prepared the Children of Israel through various trials so that they could once again become bearers of divine law. Thus, at last, according to the established practice of messengers, the time came for them to migrate. So they migrated, and with that migration the path of salvation and success opened for them and their believing companions, while their enemies were seized by divine punishment. As we have clarified at various places regarding migration, this is the divine practice, and just as it manifested in the cases of all messengers, so too it manifested in the case of Moses.
There is no place here for more detail. Those who seek further details should read the article we wrote on this very topic in our collected essays (see our book 'Naqdiyāt'). There we showed that Moses’ struggle was in every respect exactly in accordance with the known method of prophets and messengers. Those who have tried—God forbid—to portray him as a nationalist leader have misunderstood the meaning of 'arsil ma‘ī banī Isrā’īl'.

Tafseer of verses 106–108: 'He said: If you have brought a sign, then bring it if you are truthful. So he cast his staff, and lo! it was a manifest serpent. And he drew out his hand, and lo! it was white to the beholders.' 
For Pharaoh, it was extremely strange that a man—one from among his own subjects—should come to him claiming to be God’s messenger. He himself, as we have stated elsewhere, had made himself the embodiment of the greatest deity, the sun, and the supreme lord of the people. Therefore, as soon as he heard it, he said: if you are truthful in your claim that you have come as God’s messenger, then show some sign of your messengership. Moses showed him the miracles of the staff and the white hand in response.
The divine practice regarding miracles: The divine practice regarding miracles appears to be that they are granted in consideration of the people’s tastes and inclinations so that they can become decisive proofs against them. In Egypt, historical records show that magic and illusion were highly influential and magicians held a high social status. Therefore, God granted Moses such miracles by which the magic of the magicians could be invalidated. Among the Arabs, by contrast, eloquence and rhetorical excellence held the highest esteem, and the influence of orators and poets dominated society. Therefore, our Prophet was granted the miracle of the Qur'an, whose eloquence humbled and bewildered all eloquent and rhetorically skilled people. Here the word 'thu‘bān' is described as 'mubīn'—clear, manifest—which indicates that the staff truly became a serpent, an open and unmistakable serpent, leaving no room for doubt. It was not merely that something began to slither or that a head and tail appeared; rather it was a serpent in essence, with all its properties and qualities. Likewise, in 'bayḍā’ lil-nāẓirīn', the word 'nاظرين' indicates that the brightness appearing in the hand was not a mere optical illusion, but, to those who observed with reflection and contemplation, its radiance was fully genuine and real. Note that 'naẓar' in Arabic originally means to look with consideration and reflection.
Distinction between miracle and magic: Here it should be noted that the distinction between miracle and magic cannot be established by a strict logical definition. Their real difference becomes apparent from two things. First, by mutual comparison: just as if raw clay and a refined gemstone are placed side by side, the difference becomes obvious, similarly when one compares miracle and magic, the majesty, grandeur, manner of appearance, overpowering force over falsehood, and triumphant dominance of the miracle proclaim that it is not a toy made of potters’ clay, but something belonging to a different realm—
'Jauhar-e-jām jam az kān-e jahān-e dīgar ast'—its essence comes from another world. Second, by the character and conduct of the one presenting it. Those who display magic and tricks have always been among the social riffraff and lowly, whose humiliation, depravity, and moral baseness have always been proverbial. In contrast, miracles have appeared through those regarded as the finest of humanity, from whom the world has learned lessons of knowledge, action, wisdom, and insight, whose every period of life and every word and deed has become a model and paradigm, and who have always been found entirely genuine even in the hardest tests of life. For now, suffice these indications; details will come at appropriate places later.

"The chiefs of Pharaoh’s people said: This is surely a skilled magician. He wants to drive you out of your land. So what do you command?" This was the consultation that Pharaoh’s courtiers held among themselves after seeing Moses’ miracle, and then they presented their agreed opinion to Pharaoh. They concluded that this man was not just any magician but a highly skilled one, and that his goal was not merely what he outwardly claimed; rather, by organizing the Children of Israel, he wanted to expel them from their land.
A political stunt by Pharaoh and his courtiers: It should be noted here that it is not necessary that they genuinely held in their hearts what they said with their tongues. They knew Moses and Aaron very well. Considering their past, present, character, and conduct, they could easily have concluded that Moses could neither be a magician nor have any aim besides reform. But people in power have always had the habit that whenever a reformative call rises against them, they inevitably try to give it some dangerous political meaning in order to turn the masses against it. Pharaoh’s courtiers did exactly that. They smeared this pure reformative call and struggle of Moses by alleging that it was actually a conspiracy to expel the aristocracy from the land.
This stunt was effective in light of the time’s conditions. If one studies the history of the period in the Torah, it becomes clear that at that time Pharaoh and his elites feared above all that the Children of Israel had become too numerous, and that if they gained strength, one day they might drive them out of the land. The following passage from Exodus is worth noting: 'And the children of Israel were fruitful, and increased abundantly, and multiplied, and waxed exceeding mighty; and the land was filled with them. Now there arose up a new king over Egypt, which knew not Joseph. And he said unto his people, Behold, the people of the children of Israel are more and mightier than we: come on, let us deal wisely with them; lest they multiply, and it come to pass, that, when there falleth out any war, they join also unto our enemies, and fight against us, and so get them up out of the land. Therefore they did set over them taskmasters to afflict them with their burdens... And the Egyptians made the children of Israel to serve with rigour... And Pharaoh charged all his people, saying, Every son that is born ye shall cast into the river, and every daughter ye shall save alive' (Exodus 1:7–22).
We have quoted this long passage only so that one can appreciate the extent to which Egypt’s aristocracy was alarmed by the increasing number and strength of the Children of Israel. It was precisely in this context that Moses’ call arose, and in such a way that he demanded taking all the Children of Israel for a three-day journey into the wilderness to celebrate the sacrificial festival.
