In such conditions, what more effective tactic could Pharaoh’s chiefs adopt to scare their people about Moses and his call than to spread the notion that the feared danger had now arrived—that this man was organizing the Children of Israel and planning to expel you from the land—so if you want protection, gird yourselves for the confrontation.

"They said: Put him and his brother off." This was the counsel unanimously presented to Pharaoh by the courtiers: that no hasty action should be taken in the matter of Moses and Aaron. The wise course is to postpone them for now, and send messengers throughout the kingdom to gather all the expert magicians of the land so that in a public gathering they may defeat Moses and Aaron in such a way that their prestige is shattered. If they were opposed only by ordinary magicians, there is a danger that those magicians might be defeated and the result would backfire on us. 
'Arjih' is originally 'arji’hu'. The word arjā’ means to delay, to defer a matter to another time, or to keep someone waiting. The Qur'an uses its various forms. Here it appears with the hamzah dropped and the hā’ made silent. This pronunciation is in accordance with Arabic usage; the Arabs sometimes alter a word in this way to make it lighter. The author of Lisān has cited some clear examples of this.

"And the magicians came to Pharaoh. They said: Surely there will be a reward for us if we are the victors." Here, according to its usual method, the Qur'an omits the unnecessary part of the story: namely, that in accordance with this suggestion of the courtiers, Pharaoh sent messengers throughout all cities of the kingdom who collected expert magicians from everywhere and brought them to Pharaoh’s court. Since this was self-evident from the context, it was omitted, and the narrative moved directly to the next point: that as soon as the magicians arrived, they expressed—in the usual sycophantic manner of professionals—their hope and desire that if they won the contest, they would receive a great reward from the authorities. Pharaoh encouraged them, saying that not only would they receive the reward, but they would also be counted among the close favorites.
The moral lowliness of the magicians before faith: The Qur'an highlights their greed and desire especially so that it becomes clear what the moral degradation and baseness of magicians and illusionists is like. Then it goes on to show the transformation that occurred in these same magicians after they were blessed with the light of faith, to the extent that they no longer cared about wealth, let alone their own lives: 'Indeed, to our Lord we are returning.'

"They said: O Moses, either you throw first, or we shall be the ones to throw." He said: Throw. And when they threw, they bewitched the eyes of the people and terrified them, and brought forth a mighty magic.
Moses’ trust in God: Although the magicians, keeping to their professional etiquette, made the offer to Moses that he could display his art first, they also subtly expressed their desire to start. Moses gave them the first turn, which shows that he had full confidence that God’s support was with him, and that however great a magic these magicians might display, God would surely nullify it.

"They bewitched the eyes of the people and terrified them." This indicates that no matter how great magic may be, it does not alter the reality or essence of anything. It merely affects the eyes of the observers and their imaginative faculty, causing a person to see a thing in the form that the magician wants him to see.
The word 'alqā' here means presenting and displaying. Just as dice are thrown, or arrows used for lots are cast, likewise a magician throws something before the audience and performs his magic upon it. In this connection, the use of the word 'alqā' is apt.

"And We inspired Moses: Throw your staff! Then it swallowed what they had fabricated." 
The meanings of 'laqaf' are to swallow something quickly, and 'ifk' or 'yafk' mean to speak or do something false and contrary to reality. It has been noted above that magic does not change the reality or essence of anything; its entire effect is on the spectators’ sight and imaginative faculties. Therefore, what magic produces is entirely falsehood, deception, and illusion. In contrast, a miracle is entirely real. It says that when the magicians displayed their magic and their ropes appeared to slither like snakes, We inspired Moses to cast his staff, and it turned into a serpent that swallowed up all their artificial snakes and snakelets.

"Thus the truth prevailed, and what they were doing was nullified." This is exactly like the sun appearing before a waxen moon. Obviously, however many artificial moons and suns there may be, once the true sun rises, their glitter and polish disappear. So it happened here: the instant Moses’ miracle appeared, the whole enchantment of the magicians vanished. As noted above, the real distinction between miracle and magic becomes apparent in the arena of confrontation. When the two clash, truth must prevail and falsehood must be defeated.

"And they were defeated there and returned humbled." This is said not only about the magicians but also about Pharaoh and all his chiefs and supporters: they returned from the arena disgraced and humiliated. Elsewhere in the Qur'an it is mentioned that this contest took place on a specific festival day, in an open میدان, and Pharaoh had specially arranged for as many of his people as possible to gather so that he could proclaim his victory loudly—but instead such humiliation occurred that they were left unable to show their faces anywhere.

"And the magicians fell down prostrating." The magicians were so affected and overwhelmed by the overpowering nature of Moses’ miracle that they fell into prostration without hesitation. The passive form is used to express how their spirit of reverence and honor had overpowered them. Prostration as a form of respect and honor was practiced among Egyptians, Arabs, and Israelites alike, though in most cases it was limited to something like the bowing we perform in prayer.
A magician knows best the difference between magic and miracle. This makes clear that the greatest ability to distinguish between magic and miracle lies with the magician himself, provided he has the moral courage to acknowledge the truth. A magician is fully aware of the reality and extent of his own art; therefore, when confronted with a miracle, he immediately recognizes that it is beyond his craft. Thus the magicians of Pharaoh, upon seeing it, immediately admitted their helplessness and Moses’ victory and bowed their heads in reverence.

"They said: We believe in the Lord of the worlds." The magicians did not stop at honoring Moses; they openly announced their faith as well, and explicitly said that they believed in the Lord of the worlds, the Lord of Moses and Aaron. This clearly also meant that they rejected Pharaoh’s divinity. Who can imagine what effect this announcement must have had on the whole gathering, and how badly Pharaoh and his courtiers were disgraced!
A trace of love for truth existed within the magicians: It must be remembered that although these men were magicians and had acquired certain traits of that profession, as mentioned above, there still existed within them a small spark of love for truth. Surah Ṭā Hā indicates that they did not come willingly for the contest; rather, they were brought against their will. Their own words are quoted there: 'We believe in our Lord, that He may forgive us our sins and the magic to which you compelled us.' It is possible that they had already realized that Moses was not a magician and that what he had brought was not magic. For that reason, they may have wanted to avoid confronting him, but the fear of Pharaoh and his agents forced them to do so. If that is correct, it means that a hidden light of truthfulness existed within them, and the grandeur of Moses’ miracle ignited it. If even a spark of felicity exists in a person, by the grace of God it will manifest its effect.

"Pharaoh said: You believed in him before I gave you permission? This is surely a plot you all conspired in the city so that you might expel its people from it. Soon you will know." This was a very delicate moment. Pharaoh and his courtiers, indeed his whole people, had been completely unsettled. But Pharaoh was a very shrewd politician. To manage the deteriorating situation, he immediately spread the rumor that this was a collusion between those magicians and Moses—that these magicians had joined Moses in a plot to expel them from here. He alleged that they had already secretly decided among themselves that they would surrender at the critical moment and announce their faith, thereby establishing Moses’ dominance over all, and thus succeed in overthrowing the present ruling group. So he accused them of conspiracy and rebellion and announced the punishment established in the state’s law for rebels: first their hands and feet would be cut off on opposite sides, then they would be crucified publicly.
From Pharaoh’s statement 'You believed in him before I gave you permission?' it is clear that his government had no room for religious freedom—and how could it have, when the king himself claimed to be the supreme lord of the people? In such a system, anyone recognizing another lord besides him would inevitably be considered a rebel against Pharaoh.

"They said: Indeed, to our Lord we are returning. And you do not resent us except that we believed in the signs of our Lord when they came to us. Our Lord, pour upon us patience and cause us to die as Muslims." 
The miracle of faith in God: Observe the miracle of faith in God. These are the same magicians whose lowliness and meekness had only moments earlier been such that when they came before Pharaoh to display their tricks, they begged for a rich reward like jesters and imitators. Yet as soon as the light of faith entered their hearts, every corner of their inner being began to shine so brightly that it seemed as though no shadow of darkness had ever touched them. They seemed not to be flesh-and-blood humans but mountains of resolve and perseverance, angel-like embodiments of purity and sanctity. Consider how great the threat Pharaoh had made against them. Yet in reply, they said in effect: we care not. If you cut off our hands and feet and crucify us, we are going nowhere else—we are returning to our Lord. And if your entire wrath against us is for the crime that we believed in the signs of our Lord when they came to us, then do whatever you can; if this is the punishment for such a crime, then we welcome that punishment.

Transformation through faith in God: Reflect on the reason for this transformation and the reality becomes clear: if a human being is devoid of faith, nothing is more lowly; but if he is endowed with faith, nothing is more exalted.
'Our Lord, pour upon us patience and cause us to die as Muslims.' This is a prayer for the gift of patience and steadfastness in the trials ahead. The meaning is: we have already declared our faith, and Pharaoh has declared his punishments. Now our complete reliance is upon You, O Lord. Pour upon us abundant patience, and despite the completion of trials, grant us death upon faith and submission.

"The chiefs of Pharaoh’s people said: Will you leave Moses and his people to cause فساد on the earth and leave you and your gods? He said: We shall kill their sons and keep their women alive, and indeed we have overpowering control over them." 
The panic of Pharaoh’s courtiers: Moses’ success in the open contest completely unnerved Pharaoh and his courtiers. The courtiers insisted to Pharaoh that there was now no room left to give Moses and his people any more respite. If they were allowed further opportunity, they would overthrow you and your gods and incite rebellion in the land. Pharaoh reassured them that there was no reason to panic. Our authority is fully established over them. We shall keep killing their male children and keeping their females alive. The meaning is that they are under our control; if we do not want to allow them to gain strength, what can they possibly do?
The scheme of killing boys: The scheme of killing boys and sparing girls has already been mentioned above. It was adopted so that the number of the Children of Israel would not become so large that they would become a threat to the aristocracy. At first, this scheme failed because the midwives refused to cooperate, but Pharaoh was not discouraged. He then ordered the public to throw the Israelite children into the river. Here Pharaoh is alluding to that same policy: we will not allow them to become a threat. If needed, we will carry out this scheme with even greater force and severity.
A two-sided threat: It must be remembered here that although the Pharaohs feared the Israelites to this degree, they were at the same time unwilling at any cost to let them leave Egypt entirely. The reason is that all the prosperity and abundance of Egypt depended on these slaves. From carrying bricks to agriculture and construction, all work was done by their labor. The work of Pharaoh and his people was only to enjoy themselves, rule over the Israelites, and exploit them. Obviously, in such circumstances, it was impossible for the Pharaohs to let the Israelites grow strong, and equally impossible to let them depart Egypt altogether. To escape this two-sided danger, Pharaoh and his advisers adopted the policy of killing their male offspring to keep their numbers under control. When a human being exceeds his limits and starts meddling in divine limits, his reason becomes ruined in just this way.
The nature of Pharaoh’s claim to divinity: The scholars and commentators have faced great difficulty in interpreting 'leave you and your gods.' The problem is that the word 'ālihatak' seems to indicate that there were certain gods and goddesses in Egypt that Pharaoh himself worshipped. If this is accepted as true, then how is his claim to be the supreme lord to be explained? How can someone who claims to be supreme lord worship other deities?
The answer is that Pharaoh, in his own delusion, considered himself the embodiment of Egypt’s greatest deity, the sun. Thus his position was that of a god-king. In other words, he was simultaneously the king of the Egyptians and, by virtue of being the manifestation and embodiment of their highest deity, their supreme lord as well. He had made countless statues and idols of himself and installed them throughout his kingdom, and his subjects would go for their darshan and prostrate before them. Thus the king possessed both divine and royal authority over the subjects at once. Here the term 'ālihatak' refers to those statues and idols representing his person. The surviving remains of ancient Egyptian temples also confirm this.

"Moses said to his people: Seek help from Allah and be patient." In the commentary on Surah al-Baqarah, section 32, we have already discussed the importance of patience and prayer in the struggle to establish religion. In truth, these two are the means of victory in this struggle. The Qur'an everywhere emphasizes taking help through these two weapons in the face of hardship. Here, although the word Allah appears outwardly, prayer is intended by it, because the means of seeking help from Allah is prayer. Pharaoh’s resolve to suppress the Children of Israel and destroy their offspring naturally caused severe distress among the Children of Israel. To relieve their distress, Moses advised them to pray and remain patient. Trials and tribulations require great steadfastness. This cannot be achieved without God’s help. The Qur'an expresses this truth elsewhere: 'And patience is not granted except through Allah' (16:127). The means of obtaining this divine help is prayer. Note that by prayer here is meant not only the general prayer, but also that special prayer which was emphasized to the Prophet (peace be upon him) and his companions during the early difficult Makkan period. The same instruction was given to Moses and Aaron as well. We shall elaborate this further under Surah Yūnus, verse 87. 
'Indeed, the earth belongs to Allah; He causes to inherit it whom He wills from among His servants.' This is a rebuttal of Pharaoh’s arrogance expressed in the words 'We are truly the overpowering ones over them.' The meaning is that Pharaoh may continue claiming superiority and mastery with his own mouth, but the real owner of the earth is Allah. He grants its inheritance to whichever of His servants He wills, and final success belongs to those who fear Him. We have repeatedly explained the divine law concerning the messengers: Allah invariably grants them victory over their enemies, whether this victory comes during their lifetime or after their death through their followers, and whether they remain in the same land where they preached or migrate elsewhere by God’s command to another region of the earth. Moses (peace be upon him) achieved victory over his enemies during his own lifetime, indeed shortly after the events mentioned above, while his community was granted rule in the land of Palestine, and the matter was completed after his death. It should be remembered that after the punishment that befell Pharaoh and his people, the aristocratic order that ruled Egypt was completely destroyed and replaced by others of different lineage and traditions, who also had political rivalry with the previous rulers and feared that they might collude with the Children of Israel and become rulers of the land. Since this matter belongs to history and is not directly relevant to us here, we are content with a brief indication. Those seeking detail should study the history of that period.

"They said: We were harmed before you came to us." Because of their uncertainty and weak conviction, Moses’ reassurance had no effect on the Children of Israel. They said that our days were becoming harder and harder. Before your coming we were already being persecuted, and now after your coming we are still being persecuted. The point is that these are the conditions and events that have taken place or are taking place, so who can live on your promises and your empty reassurances? You are becoming a burden and trouble for us instead of mercy!
Earlier, citing the Torah, we explained that the aristocracy ruling Egypt was greatly alarmed by the rapidly increasing number of the Children of Israel and had launched a campaign to curb them by extermination. Later, when Moses rose with the call to organize and reform the Children of Israel, the anxiety of the rulers grew even more. They realized that the Children of Israel had found a powerful leader and would soon become an organized force challenging their rule. In this panic, Pharaoh not only intensified the campaign of extermination but also ordered his officials, tax collectors, and labor supervisors to make the forced labor of the Israelites harsher. The straw formerly supplied to them for making bricks was cut off, and they were commanded to gather the straw themselves, while the same quota of bricks was still required from each person every day. Thus the torment, already no light matter, became even more severe after Moses. The Children of Israel placed all this blame on Moses, saying: these are your blessings!
'Your Lord may well destroy your enemy...' To remove the discouragement of the Children of Israel, Moses reassured them again: do not despair or be afraid. God will destroy your enemies and grant you succession in the land. Here 'the land' does not mean Egypt but, as explained later in verse 137, the region of Palestine. It is said: 'And We made the oppressed people inherit the eastern and western parts of the land which We had blessed; and the good promise of your Lord was fulfilled for the Children of Israel because they were patient' (7:137). It is clear that the region indicated by 'We blessed in it' is Palestine, and the wording of the verse shows that it was by granting them rule in that land that God fulfilled the promise mentioned here on Moses’ tongue.
'And He will make you successors in the land, and see how you act.' This reminds them of the real purpose of succession on earth: when God destroys one people and elevates another, He does so only to see what attitude this people adopts after inheriting power. Will they, like the previous people, become intoxicated with power and spread corruption in the earth, or will they recognize and fulfill the right of this succession? If they too take the same path, then after a certain period God grants them respite and then destroys them, replacing them with others and testing them in turn. This cycle of examination continues constantly and will remain so as long as this world exists. Therefore no nation should fall into the misconception that once it has become God’s favored people, it will remain favored forever, no matter how much corruption appears in its beliefs and deeds.

"We seized the people of Pharaoh with years of famine and نقص from fruits so that they might take heed." 
'Sinīn' is the plural of 'sanah'. Its common meaning is year, but it also means years of famine and hardship, and it is in this sense that it is used here.
Manifestation of the divine practice: Under verses 94–95 above, we already explained that when God sends one of His messengers, He also manifests the supporting signs of the messenger’s call in the horizons and in souls through different kinds of trials, so that people’s ears may open to the messenger’s invitation, they may take warning, and they may turn back to God. Here, referring to that same divine practice, it says that on one side Moses raised his call and shook the Pharaohs with his miracles, and on the other side We subjected Pharaoh’s people to famine and reduction in produce so that the fear of God and remembrance of Him might awaken within them.

"When good came to them, they said: This is ours. But when evil struck them, they took ill omen from Moses and those with him." 
Meaning of ṭayr: Taṭayyur comes from ṭayr. Ṭayr means birds, and because among superstitious people it was common to take omens from the flying of birds, the word taṭayyur came to mean taking omens. Later, its common use became the taking of bad omens. From the same root comes the word ṭā'ir, used for something from which a good or bad omen is taken, and later by extension it came to mean one’s lot, destiny, or fortune.
The reaction of the Pharaohs to calamities: This explains how the punishment sent by God to awaken them and draw them toward Moses’ call was interpreted by them as the result of Moses’ ill omen. When conditions were favorable, they attributed it to their own good fortune and merit; but when a terrestrial or celestial disaster struck, they said it was the misfortune of Moses and his companions. They were spreading irreligion and disbelief, and because of that the earthly and heavenly deities were displeased, resulting in these calamities. It is said that their destiny and misfortune were with God, and that this arose from their own bad deeds, but most of them did not know this reality, so they searched for the source of their ill fortune in the fortune of others.

"They said: No matter what signs you bring us..." This was their final answer: that however many signs you may show us to work magic upon us, we are not going to accept your words by being impressed by such things.

So We sent upon them the flood and locusts and lice and frogs and blood as detailed signs, but they were arrogant and were a criminal people. 

The Miracles of Prophet Moses: The trials mentioned above in Ayah 130 occurred, as we indicated, under the general Divine Law (Sunnah) which necessitates Divine Mercy to support and strengthen the call of every Messenger and to create humility and remembrance within the people. However, when the people of Pharaoh were not influenced by them, Allah showed very significant miracles at the hands of Moses, a few of which are hinted at here; we present their details in the light of the Torah. 

'The Flood' (Tufan): In the Torah, the detail of this flood is given as follows: 'And the Lord said to Moses, Stretch out your hand toward heaven, so that hail may fall upon all the land of Egypt, upon man and beast and every plant of the field in the land of Egypt. And Moses lifted his staff toward heaven, and the Lord sent thunder and hail, and fire came down to the earth... and the hail was so heavy that such hail had never fallen in the land since the Egyptians became a nation... and the hail struck everything in the field throughout the land of Egypt, both man and beast...' (Exodus 9:22–25). From this, it is known that this was a storm of thunder, lightning, and hail. Rain and fierce winds often accompany it. The mention of 'fire' in it is a mistake by the translators of the Torah; it does not mean our ordinary fire, but rather that radiance/lightning which is a necessary accompaniment of such storms. 

'Locusts' (Jarad): Jarad means locusts. The detail in the Torah is as follows: 'Then the Lord said to Moses, Stretch out your hand over the land of Egypt for the locusts, that they may come upon the land of Egypt and eat every plant of the land... So Moses stretched out his staff over the land of Egypt, and the Lord brought an east wind... by morning the east wind had brought the locusts... and they settled within all the borders of Egypt... for they covered the face of the whole earth, so that the land was darkened, and they ate every plant of the land and all the fruit of the trees...' (Exodus 10:12–15). 

'Lice' (Qummal): Qummal means lice. The detail of how this affliction appeared is stated: 'Then the Lord said to Moses, Say to Aaron, Stretch out your staff and strike the dust of the earth, so that it may become lice throughout all the land of Egypt... and there were lice on man and beast. All the dust of the earth became lice throughout all the land of Egypt' (Exodus 8:16–17). 

'Frogs' (Dafadi'): Dafadi' is the plural of Difda', which means frog. The detail of this punishment is stated: 'Then the Lord said to Moses, Go to Pharaoh and say to him, Thus says the Lord, Let My people go, that they may worship Me. If you refuse to let them go, behold, I will strike all your territory with frogs... and the river shall swarm with frogs... they shall come into your house, into your bedroom, on your bed, into the houses of your servants...' (Exodus 8:1–6). 

'Blood' (Dam): Dam means blood. The form of its appearance is mentioned as: 'And the Lord said to Moses, Say to Aaron, Take your staff and stretch out your hand over the waters of Egypt... that they may become blood... and there shall be blood throughout all the land of Egypt, even in vessels of stone and wood... and the water of the river turned to blood... and the fish in the river died, and the river stank...' (Exodus 7:19–21). 

'Detailed Signs' (Ayatin Mufassalat): Meaning all these signs are mentioned in detail. The context proves that the adverb for 'detailed' is omitted here, implying their detail exists in the Torah. Indeed, the fact is that not only these, but several other miracles of this nature are described in the Torah with all minor details. Since the Quran has only hinted at them, it has given the reference of the Torah for their details. 

'But they were arrogant and were a criminal people': Meaning instead of developing humility and remembrance within themselves, their pride and arrogance increased. This is because they were criminals, and Allah does not guide the criminals. For them, the signs became a cause of the completion of proof (Itmam al-Hujjah) and hardness of heart rather than guidance.

And when the punishment fell upon them... (to the end of the verse). 'Lamma' (when) also comes in the meaning of 'Kullama' (whenever), especially in those instances where the depiction of a situation is intended. A parallel exists ahead in Ayah 189. 

The meaning is that whenever they were caught in the grip of punishment, they would start pleading and entreating Moses: 'By the virtue of the proximity and relationship you have with your Lord, pray and intercede for us so that this punishment is removed from us. If you remove it, we will certainly accept your word and let the Children of Israel go with you.' But when the punishment was removed, they would again renege on their promise. In the Torah, it is mentioned as: 'Then Pharaoh called Moses and Aaron and said, Entreat the Lord to take away the frogs from me and from my people, and I will let the people go that they may sacrifice to the Lord' (Exodus 8:28–32). 

'By the covenant He has with you' (Bima 'ahida 'indaka): In my view, the meaning is: 'Since God listens to you and upholds the sanctity of your prayer, therefore pray for us.'

'Until a term which they were to reach': The purpose is to point toward their lack of conscience and their baseness—that despite knowing their deception and lies could not remain hidden for long, they made a false covenant merely with the thought of taking advantage of it for as long as possible.

'So We took retribution from them...' (to the end of the verse). This retribution refers to the revenge for those crimes upon which they remained persistent despite the full completion of proof. Initially, they remained indifferent and heedless of Allah's signs, and when the signs appeared before them with an open challenge, they denied them, saying they were not from God but were the wonders of sorcery and magic. In punishment for this, they were drowned in the sea. The details of this drowning incident will appear ahead in the appropriate place in this book.

'And We made the people who were oppressed inherit...' (to the end of the verse). 'Yustad'afun' (oppressed/weakened) refers to those oppressions and hardships which were inflicted upon the Children of Israel by the people of Pharaoh to keep them suppressed, some of which have been hinted at above. It is stated that the same nation which was tightened in the very hard shackles of slavery and subjugation, Allah granted them elevation and bestowed upon them the rule of the land of Palestine. To describe this granting of rule, the word 'Iraath' (inheritance) is used here, which contains the meaning that Allah removed their former rulers from there and made the Israelites inherit it. Regarding the description of this land, the words 'which We had blessed' (allati barakna fiha) firstly determine that it refers specifically to the land of Palestine, because in the Quran, this land is mentioned with this attribute. Secondly, these words manifest both types of blessings of this land—spiritual and material—because in Arabic, the word 'Mubarak' carries both these meanings. 

From the words 'east and west' (mashariq and magharib), the vastness of the boundaries of this rule is being hinted at. In Arabic, sometimes the plural of a word comes to denote the vastness of its sides. While discussing the word 'A'raf' previously, we hinted at this point. 

'And the good word of your Lord was fulfilled for the Children of Israel' refers to that promise mentioned in Ayahs 128–129. The meaning is that the promise Allah made to the ancestors of the Children of Israel, and which He finally renewed with Moses, that promise was finally fulfilled. Through 'because they were patient' (bima sabaru), attention is drawn to the reality that the favors Allah bestows upon nations are, in any case, based on attributes and character. It is not that mere lineage and family make someone a favorite of God. 

'And We destroyed what Pharaoh had been producing': Meaning We also destroyed all the magnificent constructions of Pharaoh and his people and laid waste to their lush and green gardens. Two words are used here: 'ma kana yasna'' and 'ma kanu ya'rishun'. In my view, the first refers to constructions and the second to gardens. The Pharaoh mentioned here had a special taste for constructions, and the Children of Israel were mostly engaged in forced labor day and night for the sake of these constructions. 'Ma kanu ya'rishun' primarily refers to vineyards because their vines are raised on trellises, as the expression 'jannatun ma'rushat' exists in the Quran, but sometimes an expression for something is made through its dominant part, which is specific in word but general in meaning. Egypt, at least in that era, was distinguished in the production of grapes. Hints of this also emerge from certain places in Surah Yusuf. 

From this portion, the fact also emerges that destruction came upon the people of Pharaoh besides drowning, through which their cities and gardens were all laid waste. Their gardens, as passed above, had already been destroyed by hail and locusts; it seems an earthquake also occurred during this interval, by which their buildings were also demolished, and whatever had remained from previous calamities was also ruined.

And We took the Children of Israel across the sea; then they came upon a people devoted to some idols of theirs. They said, 'O Moses, make for us a god just as they have gods.' He said, 'Indeed, you are a people behaving ignorantly.' 

'And We took the Children of Israel across the sea': Regarding the eloquence in this style and the aspect of special care and favor from Allah for the Children of Israel, we hinted at it in the Tafseer of Baqarah, Ayah 50. 

The account of the Children of Israel after leaving Egypt: Up to this point, that part of the history of the Children of Israel has been described which relates to Egypt and Pharaoh. In it, the glories of Allah that were manifested, how He helped Moses and the Israelites, and how He destroyed Pharaoh and his people, has all come forward. From this perspective, this account has become the completion and supplement to the accounts that have passed earlier in this Surah. Now, that part of the history of the Israelites is being described which follows their exit from Egypt and crossing the river. From this part, the reality will become prominent as to how Allah helped the Children of Israel and guided them at every step, but the Israelites were ungrateful and stumbled at every step. 

'Then they came upon a people devoted to some idols of theirs': Here, 'people' refers to any of those nations with whom the Israelites came into contact after exiting the river. Every one of those nations was idol-worshipping. The style of 'devoted to idols' (ya'kufuna 'ala asnam) carries a sense of contempt for them. That is, they were so mindless and foolish that, leaving the Compassionate and Merciful God, they were clinging to idols carved by their own hands and were active in their servitude and worship. When the word 'Ukuf' comes with 'ala', it means to stick to something and associate oneself with it. 

The first stumble of the Children of Israel: 'They said, O Moses, make for us a god.' But the Israelites proved more foolish than them, as they were captivated by this sight and began demanding from Moses to make a deity for them just like the deities those people had. From this, one can estimate that the long slavery in Egypt had made the mental and moral level of the Israelites so low that even after seeing so many glories of Allah's beauty and majesty, they were still, as it were, in the darknesses of Egypt. Attachment to old familiar things does not leave easily. Perhaps this is the point that after the Conquest of Makkah, the Holy Prophet (PBUH) issued orders to erase all traces of polytheism at once, so that these things might not become a trial (fitnah) for the raw and weak-minded people. 

'He said, Indeed, you are a people behaving ignorantly': In response to the demand, Moses said that you are extremely mindless, superstitious, and emotional people.

Indeed, those—destroyed is that in which they are... (to the end of the verse). This thing they are doing will be entirely ruined and trampled before God because it is sheer falsehood. Think that Allah, by choosing you for His guidance and Shariah, bestowed upon you excellence and elevation over the whole world; so should I now leave Him and go out to find another deity for you? What could be greater ingratitude and thanklessness than this?

And [recall] when We saved you from the people of Pharaoh, who were afflicting you with the worst torment... (to the end of the verse). 

The Tafseer of this verse has already passed in Baqarah and also in this Surah. What is noteworthy here is that from this verse up to Ayah 147, Allah has mentioned those special arrangements He made for the Children of Israel. But the value the Israelites gave to this was that while Moses went to Tur to receive Allah's Shariah, the Israelites—despite all the advice of Moses and all the efforts of his deputy Aaron—fashioned and prepared the very idol they had demanded from Moses and for which Moses had given them the warning and rebuke mentioned above. The purpose of all this detail is to show what actions that nation, which is so proud today of its honor and sanctity, has already committed in the very presence of its Prophet and while witnessing his great miracles.

And We appointed for Moses thirty nights and completed them with ten... (to the end of the verse). 

This verse has been explained under Baqarah. This is the mention of the special arrangement Allah made to give His Shariah to Moses. He was instructed to present himself at a specific place on Mount Tur for 30 days. Moses, as discussed under Taha Ayah 84, arrived at Tur before the appointed time. Although this precedence of Moses was in the quest for Allah's pleasure, Allah took him to account for this haste and precedence, and the wisdom of the sequence necessitated that the appointed period of 30 days be increased to 40 days. We will clarify ahead at an appropriate place that the Prophets (PBUT) never commit mistakes by being overwhelmed by following desires, but if in the passion of seeking Allah's pleasure, a step of theirs ever lands slightly beyond the boundaries, Allah stops them from that as well, because they are the balance/scale of truth, and it is necessary for the balance to be correct from every aspect and 100% correct. 

The instruction of Moses to Aaron: 'And Moses said to his brother Aaron...' This refers to the arrangement Moses made for the supervision of the Children of Israel during his temporary absence. For the period of his absence, he made Aaron his deputy and instructed him: 'Do not let any corruption, disorder, innovation, or misguidance arise within the nation. If any such thing appears, keep reforming it and never follow the way of those who create disorder.' From the words 'and do not follow the way of the corrupters' (wa la tattabi' sabila al-mufsidin), it is suggested that Moses was aware of the corrupt elements within the nation and he also had apprehension from them due to their previous mischief; therefore, he instructed Aaron with special emphasis that if these elements try to raise any fitnah, do not let it ignite. It should be noted that all this arrangement is being described so that it becomes clear that whatever curse of calf-worship the Israelites adopted during this interval, they adopted it despite all this arrangement.

And when Moses arrived at Our appointed time and his Lord spoke to him, he said, 'My Lord, show [Yourself] to me that I may look at You.' [Allah] said, 'You will not see Me, but look at the mountain; if it should remain in its place, then you will see Me.' But when his Lord manifested His glory to the mountain, He made it as dust, and Moses fell unconscious. And when he awoke, he said, 'Exalted are You! I have repented to You, and I am the first of the believers.' 

'Miqat' means the appointed time. Here, 'Miqat' refers to that special time which Allah appointed to honor Moses with His address and speech. 

'He said, My Lord, show [Yourself] to me that I may look at You': After being honored with the speech, Moses felt a longing that the One whose speech had graced his hearing, His sight should also grace his vision. Thus, with extreme etiquette, he made this request: 'O Lord, show Yourself to me that I may see You.' This longing is a natural longing; therefore, Allah did not rebuke Moses for it but rather said that man cannot see the Essence of God with these terrestrial eyes; he can only see the manifestations of His attributes. 

'But look at the mountain; if it should remain in its place, then you will see Me': This observation was shown for Moses' reassurance—that even mountains and hills, which are superior in terms of being solid and firm, cannot withstand the radiance of God's Essence; so how can you, a weak-framed human, withstand it? Human endurance is limited. His eyes see light, but if this light exceeds a specific limit, the eyes become dazzled, and sometimes vision is even snatched away. His ears hear sound, but their capacity to hear is also only up to a fixed limit; if a lightning bolt's crack merely exceeds the limit, the eardrums themselves become useless. The sun is an indispensable necessity for his life, but its light and heat are life-giving for him only as long as it reaches him from an extremely long distance, through who knows how many atmospheric veils and filters. If one day it comes slightly closer to the globe and casts a glance upon it, all living beings would be burnt to ash and dust. So when human endurance is so frail against the creatures of this universe, how can it withstand the Essence of God, who is absolute Light and beyond and above all 'how' and 'wherefore'? He said, 'You cannot see My Essence, but look toward the mountain in front; I will cast My glory upon it. If it remains steady in its place, then understand that you can see Me; if it cannot remain steady, it means this thing is far beyond your endurance.' 

'But when his Lord manifested His glory to the mountain, He made it as dust': 'Dakka al-ha'it' means he demolished the wall until he leveled it with the ground. 'Dakka al-ard' means he leveled its ups and downs. The meaning would be He demolished the mountain and leveled it with the ground. 'Jabal' (mountain) here does not refer to the entire mountain or the whole mountain range, but to a specific part of it or a particular peak in front of Moses. The whole is spoken but a part is intended, as is common in every language. The meaning is that when Allah (Glorified be He) cast His glory upon the specific part of the mountain, the mountain broke into pieces and collapsed. It should be clear that this would have been merely a reflection and a fragment of the glory of the Essence, yet even a solid thing like a mountain could not withstand it, and Moses fell unconscious. 

'And when he awoke, he said, Exalted are You! I have repented to You, and I am the first of the believers': When Moses regained consciousness, he said, 'Subhanaka' (Exalted are You), You are too sublime and pure to be seen with terrestrial eyes. 'I have repented to You'—I made this bold move to desire to see You; I repent for it and seek forgiveness. 'And I am the first of the believers'—and I become the first to believe that Your Essence is beyond the observation of our eyes. 

The remedy for the Israelites' sensory-worship: Allah showed all this to Moses on this occasion to remove a severe intellectual disease of the Children of Israel. It is mentioned at multiple places in the Torah that the Israelites repeatedly demanded from Moses to see God. They would say that when God speaks to you, He should also speak to us face to face and we should see Him. All those glories of Allah they had seen until then were not sufficient to satisfy their hearts. Their hearts would not settle on an unseen God. The demand 'make for us a god just as they have gods' mentioned above is also a manifestation of this desire of theirs—that they wanted to see God in a sensory form. To remove this disease of sensory-worship, Allah clearly told and showed Moses in the very first stage that God is not a thing to be seen with eyes or touched with hands; He is only a thing to be understood with intellect and accepted with the heart. Eyes can only see the splendors of His attributes; their reach does not go beyond that. Moses explained all these things to the Israelites, but this doubt did not leave their hearts; consequently, they also came under God's wrath due to this, mention of which has passed in Baqarah and will appear ahead. 

From this place, it also becomes clear that those yogis and Sufis who have declared the observation of the Divine Essence as the level of perfection in gnosis (ma'rifah) and made it their objective have set their goal far beyond the limits of their reach, and its outcome is nothing but bewilderment and confusion. It is like a fly going out to hunt a falcon. In the Tafseer of Surah Najm, we will explain, if Allah wills, that to say nothing of others, even the most high-ranking and close Angel Gabriel has a fixed limit to his reach; he receives the lights and glories from there; if he steps a bit beyond that: 'If I fly even a hair's breadth higher, the splendor of glory would burn my wings.'

[Allah] said, 'O Moses, I have chosen you over the people with My messages and with My words... (to the end of the verse). 

The meaning is: That chosenness I have bestowed upon you over the people through My message and My speech is enough for your honor; hold fast to the teaching and guidance I have granted you and remain constantly grateful to Me. That is, fulfill its right. Value it with heart and soul yourself, and tell and teach this to others. From the style of speech, it also emerges in an extremely subtle way that 'what has been given is enough for you, be content with it and do not desire My vision.'

And We wrote for him on the tablets of everything... (to the end of the verse). 

'And We wrote for him on the tablets': Did Allah Himself write on the tablets or did Moses (PBUH) write under Allah's instruction? Both things emerge from the Torah. 'And Moses went and wrote all the words of the Lord' (Exodus 14:3–4). In another place it is: 'And Moses turned and went down from the mountain with the two tablets of the Testimony in his hand... and the tablets were the work of God, and the writing was the writing of God, engraved on the tablets' (Exodus 22:15–16). The words of the Quran are inclusive of both meanings and fundamentally there is no difference between the two. When Moses wrote under Allah's instruction, then Allah Himself wrote it. An engineer builds a bridge, but he does it under the order of the king or government and according to its plan and design. Therefore, it is said that the king or government built the bridge. All the arrangement for the preservation of the Quran was carried out by the hands of the Prophet (PBUH) and his companions, but since it was carried out under Allah's instructions, it was said: 'And indeed, We are its guardian.' 

Arrangement of 'Teaching by the Pen' for the Children of Israel: These words manifest that special care and favor which Allah bestowed in the matter of giving Shariah to the Children of Israel. The Prophets (PBUT) who came before Moses gave oral teachings to their respective nations, but for the Children of Israel, Allah made the arrangement of 'teaching by the pen.' Their Prophet did not only tell and teach them through word and deed but also recorded everything for them so that Allah's Shariah reaches them in the most preserved and safe form. But the value they gave to this arrangement—its details have passed in Baqarah and are appearing ahead. 

'Of everything an instruction and explanation for all things': The nature of the details recorded in the tablets: Meaning those tablets contained every kind of necessary instruction and all necessary details. When 'of everything' (min kulli shay') and 'for all things' (likulli shay') come in such a context, their relationship is with the objective and subject at hand. That is, at this stage, all those matters of religion and Shariah that were intended to be told were told, and all the details required regarding communal organization and formation were recorded on the tablets. Generally, it has been understood that only the famous Ten Commandments were recorded on the tablets, but this does not seem quite correct. From the references we quoted above from the Book of Exodus, it is known that the tablets were two and both were filled on both their sides. Moreover, there are many other details at the mentioned place in the Torah which relate to communal organization and formation, and their nature is such that it was necessary for the Israelites to be aware of them at this stage. Then, it is also noteworthy that the Quran has not mentioned only two tablets like the Torah, but has used the word 'Alwah' (tablets), which comes for plural, and in Arabic, plural is applied to more than two. 

'So take them with strength and order your people to take the best of them': Hold it firmly, meaning adopt it with full resolve and determination, and remain firm upon its instructions in every kind of circumstance, whether easy or hard. And also instruct your nation not to demand idols like those of other ignorant nations in imitation of them, nor adopt their ways, but rather adopt the better way which has been told in this scripture. The comparison and preference in 'the best of them' (bi-ahsaniha) is not between the various instructions of the tablets' writing—because all of them were 'best' and all were to be adopted without exception or selection—but rather this preference is in comparison to the ways of the polytheistic nations with whom the Israelites came into contact after crossing the river and by whom they were influenced to such a degree that they presented that demand before Moses mentioned above in Ayah 138. The meaning is: Tell the Children of Israel not to be captivated by the superstitions of the idol-worshipping nations, but rather adopt this pure and sublime and best way which has been told to them in these tablets. The superlative form (ahsan) has been used in the Quran in some other instances for this meaning as well, e.g., in Surah Zumar, Ayah 55; if Allah wills, we will explain its further detail at some suitable place. 

'I will show you the home of the defiantly disobedient': This is a hint toward the upcoming stages—that it is necessary for you and your nation to hold fast to these teachings and instructions also for the reason that ahead you have to pass through the regions of the disbelievers and the defiant and deal with them. In those regions of disbelief and corruption, these very things will serve as an escort and provision for the journey, and through them, you will remain dominant and victorious. Without being armed with these, there is a danger for your nation to fall into fitnah at every step.

I will turn away from My signs those who are arrogant upon the earth without right... (to the end of the verse). 

A timely warning: This is a timely, comprehensive warning and reminder as to who will value these teachings and benefit from them, and who will turn away from them and consequently face deprivation in the Hereafter. He said that those who, while living and dwelling on God's earth, consider themselves independent of God, superior to His command and order, and consider His bestowed favors as their personal right—Allah will not grant them the tawfiq (success/ability) to incline toward guidance. Within such people, the desire for good and guidance dies. For this reason, if the way of guidance is shown to them, they run away from it, and if invited toward the way of misguidance, they immediately set out upon it. This condition of theirs is the result of the fact that they spend their lives indifferent to God's signs, and even if the clearest matter comes before them, they deny it. Such people, who are deniers of Allah's signs and the meeting of the Hereafter, all their deeds will go to waste and remain fruitless. In the Hereafter, value and price belong only to that deed which was performed for God's pleasure with the Hereafter in view.

And the people of Moses made, after [his departure], from their ornaments a calf—an image having a lowing sound... (to the end of the verse). Meaning Allah made all these efforts for their guidance which were mentioned, but the value they gave to it was that after Moses' departure, they made an image of a calf and got engaged in its worship. 

'From their ornaments a calf—an image having a lowing sound': From the Torah, it is known that to make this image, people gave their gold jewelry in contribution. From those very jewels, an image of a calf was made, and it seems that the craftsmanship was such that when air passed through it, a lowing sound would emerge from it, just as calves low. 'Khuwar' in Arabic is the word for the lowing sound of an ox. 

The Quran has given all this clarification to manifest the Israelites' dullness, intellectual poverty, and also their ingratitude and thanklessness—that the peerless and matchless God who showed them those glories of His majesty and beauty mentioned above, the value they gave to Him was that they made a calf from their own jewelry, and that too not a real calf, but merely a body, a mold, a torso, from which a lowing sound emerged, and they believed regarding it that 'this is that Lord God who brought the Children of Israel out of the slavery of the Egyptians and this very one will, in his guidance, grant the Israelites the kingdom of the Promised Land'!! In this way, they fulfilled that longing which they had expressed before Moses before his departure to Tur and for which Moses had scolded them. 

The people of the Torah have attributed this whole fitnah to Aaron (PBUH), but the Quran, as is coming ahead, has strongly refuted this and held Samiri responsible for all this corruption, as will appear in Surah Taha. Samiri was very shrewd and a hypocrite and had joined Moses' group merely for his corrupt objectives. For a person who is acquainted with the idol-worship and craftsmanship of Egypt etc., it was not difficult to cast such a calf that produced a lowing sound. The worshippers and priests of temples in every era have shown such wonders and perfections to deceive the commoners that one is left amazed. The famous traveler Ibn Battuta, in his travelogue, has mentioned things far more amazing than this. Naturally, where there is idol-worship, the art of idol-making also progresses a lot. Regarding the construction of Egypt's Aswan Dam, the ancient and great temple of Abu Simbel which was moved from its place under international arrangement—I had the opportunity to study its details in an English magazine. I was amazed to read that the statues of the King (Pharaoh) and the Queen were installed in the temple at such an angle that on the date of the King's birth each year, the first rays of the sun would fall on the King's forehead. Now consider, what could be greater than this to establish the seal of divinity for a king who is considered an incarnation of the Sun God in the hearts of the common cattle? In short, for Samiri, it was not difficult to make such a calf with the help of his companions which produced a sound like a calf; but he did not stop at that, rather, to deceive the commoners, he also staged a drama of a vision and a special miracle by which his influence was well established over the people. A hint is enough here; the detail will come, if Allah wills, in the Tafseer of Surah Taha. 

'Did they not see that it could neither speak to them nor guide them to a way?': This is a parenthetical sentence in the middle of the speech. The meaning is: They did not think even a little that a thing which can neither speak nor guide, for what ailment is it a remedy that it should be accepted as a deity and worshipped? A deity is not a toy, but rather the greatest necessity of life is attached to it—that it guides. If from this perspective the existence of a thing is meaningless, then why accept it as a deity and why worship it? 

'They took it [for worship], and they were wrongdoers': This is part of the original point. That is, they made the calf a deity and thus became those who inflict wrong upon their own souls.

And when they regretted... (to the end of the verse). 

'Suqita fi aydihim' is an idiom of the Arabic language, the meaning of which has generally been given as being regretful and ashamed, but since becoming alert to a mistake is also a necessary consequence of regret and shame, if its translation is made as 'becoming alert/aware', it would not be wrong in my view. What is the origin of this idiom? Linguists differ on this, and this difference is a natural result of the fact that researching the origin of every idiom is a very difficult task. I sometimes think that some thing being 'dropped into the hands' is like it coming in front; in such a condition, even a dull-witted person becomes alert to it because 'one does not need a mirror to see the bracelet on the hand'. If this explanation is accepted, the meaning is: Entranced by the propaganda of Samiri and his companions, the Israelites committed this folly, but when the lowing calf appeared in front and it was known that 'this is the god that has emerged for the Israelites', their senses flew away, they saw their folly right in front of their eyes and felt their misguidance and said: 'If our Lord does not have mercy on us and does not forgive this crime of ours, then we are ruined.' The context proves that this feeling occurred to those people within whom some understanding existed. In the crisis of public passion, they could not estimate the correct situation, but when the result came in front, their eyes opened and they saw that the matter had become very bad. In communal life, such occasions are very trying and very dangerous. Some devil arises and, gathering a crowd of emotional commoners around him, starts a fitnah that scatters the fabric of the entire community, and sometimes wise and responsible people also wake up only when the water has passed over the head.

And when Moses returned to his people, angry and grieved, he said, 'Wretched is that which you have done in my place after me...' (to the end of the verse). 

It is proven from both the Torah and the Quran that Moses had already received the news of this incident on the mountain; in fact, it is known from the Quran that Allah Himself gave this news to Moses; therefore, when he returned, he returned in a state of extreme anger and grief/sorrow. The anger was at this successful mischief of these corrupters, and the grief and sorrow were at the mindless and ignorance of his nation. Upon arriving, he first took to task those upon whom the responsibility of looking after the nation in his absence lay. He said: 'You carried out a very bad succession after me that you put the nation on this bad path or let it go.' 

'Were you impatient over the matter of your Lord?': Meaning before God told what the method of His worship is (and it was to tell this very thing that He called me to the mountain), did you hasten and invent a method of worship yourself, whereas this thing was not for you to invent but for God to tell? The interrogation here is for rebuke and blame. It should be clear that idol-worship actually came into existence under the belief of 'Hulul' (indwelling/incarnation). Polytheists think that God indwells in these forms and images, therefore their worship is synonymous with the worship of God. Ahead in this Surah, this issue will be discussed in detail. 

'And he threw down the tablets and took hold of his brother's head...': This is a depiction of his state being overwhelmed and his passion of religious honor—that he put the tablets aside and, catching Aaron by the head and shoulder, began to shake him, meaning: What is this? Why did you let this fitnah raise its head? Since the primary responsibility lay with Aaron and there was complete trust in him, he came most under the strike of the rebuke. In the matter of God, this passion and emotion is a demand of faith's honor and also of tribal love for Aaron. 

'He said, O son of my mother, indeed the people oppressed me...': This is the defense Aaron presented, and the style of address is very sweet. He did not say 'O my brother', but said 'O son of my mother' (ibna umma), from which both affection and entreaty are prominent. Aaron said in defense: 'The nation suppressed me and it was near that people would kill me.' From this it is clear that he not only stopped the people from this fitnah but even put his life in danger for it, but the heavy majority of the nation fell for Samiri's trick, and such a small number remained with him that it was not possible for him to stop it by force. For this reason, as will be mentioned elsewhere, he saw the wisdom in waiting for Moses' return, lest any action of his become a cause for further harm. Thus, his saying is quoted in the Quran: 'Indeed, I feared that you would say, You caused division among the Children of Israel and did not observe my word' (Taha: 94). 

'So do not let the enemies rejoice over me': Meaning do not give the enemies a chance to laugh at me and do not count me among these wrongdoers. This whole fitnah was raised by the wicked ones; I am entirely free from it, but when they see that all the rebuke fell on me, they will be very happy at their success that they raised the fitnah but the whole responsibility fell on me. 

The refutation of the lie of the compilers of the Torah against Aaron (PBUH): By this, the lie that the compilers of the Torah have attributed to Aaron (PBUH)—that all this work of calf-making was carried out under the arrangement of Aaron—is fully refuted. The Quran not only freed Aaron from this vile accusation but also identified the actual founder of this fitnah and also exposed all his drama which he staged to deceive the commoners. At its appropriate place, its full detail will appear, if Allah wills.

[Moses] said, 'My Lord, forgive me and my brother and admit us into Your mercy...' (to the end of the verse). 

Moses (PBUH) accepted this excuse of Aaron. Such a suspicion could not occur regarding Aaron anyway that, God forbid, he would be among the founders of this fitnah; however, a suspicion would have occurred to Moses that he did not fulfill his responsibility as was due to stop it. This suspicion was removed by Aaron's mentioned answer. Obviously, if the circumstances had become such that he could not stop this fitnah even by giving his head, then it was better for him to keep himself separate from it and wait for Moses. After the situation became clear, Moses sought forgiveness from Allah for himself and his brother, and he sought forgiveness for himself first because in the prayer for forgiveness, this is the correct etiquette, and from this, a hint also emerges that if any excess occurred with the brother in the passion of honor for the truth, may Allah overlook it.

Indeed, those who took the calf [for worship] will be overtaken by wrath from their Lord and humiliation in the life of this world... (to the end of the verse). 

This is the threat which was announced to the Children of Israel on this occasion—that those who carried out this calf-making, before the Hereafter, even in the life of this world, there will be God's wrath and the strike of humiliation upon them, because this was a fabrication against Allah, and this is the punishment for fabrication against Allah. However, those who will repent and renew their faith, Allah will forgive them. This wrath of Allah, in the form it appeared—its detail we have presented under Surah Baqarah Ayah 84: that Moses ordered the sincere believers of every tribe to kill those criminals of their respective tribes who had been part of the fitnah. Thus, it happened. Only those were saved from killing who repented. The reason for declaring shirk (polytheism) as a fabrication against Allah we have explained elsewhere.

And those who committed evil deeds and then repented after them and believed... (to the end of the verse). 

Here, the condition of faith is mentioned along with repentance. 'Then they repented after it and believed.' The reason for this is that some sins are such that a person's faith itself is snatched away. This sin of the Children of Israel was among those sins; for this reason, the condition of renewal of faith was attached along with repentance. If the nature of the sin is not such, then the reform of behavior along with repentance makes the repentance complete.

And when the anger subsided from Moses, he took up the tablets... (to the end of the verse). 

The 'an' after 'sakata' is proof that it contains the meaning of 'dhahaba' (went away) or some synonymous word. The meaning thus became: when Moses became silent and his anger went away. 'He took up the tablets'—he picked up again those tablets he had put aside. From this, the wrong narration of the Torah was incidentally refuted—that the tablets were broken into pieces by being thrown. It should be clear that in putting down those tablets in such a way, there was no aspect of disrespect for them, but rather it was an expression of grief and anger at the disrespect the Israelites showed for this precious favor—that your Lord sent for you this sign of leadership and imamate, and you are such that you got your nose cut in this way. 

'And in their inscription was guidance and mercy': 'Nuskhah' also refers to the word-for-word copy of any writing. Since the original Torah was the copy of these very tablets, he described the Torah as their 'nuskhah'. It was stated that in it is guidance and mercy for those who fear their Lord. In other words, this is the same point that was stated regarding the Quran in the words 'guidance for the God-fearing'. Regarding 'guidance' and 'mercy', we have mentioned at another place that when these two words come together, the meaning is that it is guidance with respect to the beginning and mercy with respect to the end; in the life of this world, it will be the guide, and in the Hereafter, the means of God's mercy.

And Moses chose from his people seventy men for Our appointment... (to the end of the verse). 

This is the mention of Moses going to Mount Sinai for the second time when he went for repentance after the incident of calf-worship. The narrators of the Torah have mixed this incident with the first incident. For this reason, the matter has remained muddled. The Quran has described these two incidents separately because this incident is an incident holding great importance in the history of the Children of Israel by itself. For this, Allah appointed a special time, and Moses chose seventy men from his nation so that the same type of collective repentance occurs for the type of collective crime that was committed. Also, whatever happens should happen in front of all the prominent men of the nation so that they may bear witness to it before the nation. 

'But when the earthquake seized them': On this occasion, Allah manifested His majesty so that the leaders of the Israelites are reminded again that the Lord with whom they have their affair, this is a minor wonder of His majesty and power. In the Torah, its mention is like this: 'On the third day, as morning dawned, there were peals of thunder and flashes of lightning, and a dense cloud upon the mountain, and a very loud blast of the horn, so that all the people who were in the camp trembled... and Mount Sinai was all in smoke... and the whole mountain trembled violently' (Exodus 19:18–19). Although in the Torah this mention is of the occasion when the famous Ten Commandments were given to the Children of Israel, it seems that this same situation also occurred on this occasion of repentance, which the Quran has described with 'the earthquake seized them' (akhadhat-humu al-rajfah). 

'He said, My Lord, if You had willed, You could have destroyed them...': Now this is the crying and wailing of Moses (PBUH) and his prayer and plea. The meaning is: If this majesty is a manifestation of Your wrath and You have decided to destroy us, then this work, if You had willed, You could have done before this; but now when You have granted us the opportunity of audience and we have even arrived here, it is far from Your mercy that You destroy us. 

'Would You destroy us for what the foolish among us have done?': Meaning whatever happened, happened due to the wretchedness of the foolish ones within the community, and it is far from Your mercy that You destroy everyone in punishment for a crime of the foolish ones. 

'It is not but Your trial': Meaning this was merely a trial from You, and those who fulfill the duty against Your trial are only those who receive Your tawfiq (success/ability); only those whom You grant tawfiq succeed in Your test and find guidance, and those whom You deprive of Your tawfiq go astray. It should be noted that here Moses has referred to that very Sunnat Allah (Divine Law) which Allah has favored in the matter of guidance and misguidance and which has been mentioned repeatedly in the Quran; but at the same time, with extreme etiquette and in an extremely subtle way, there is also a hint toward the sensitivity of the matter in it—that passing through Your tests perfectly is no easy game. This is dependent only on Your grace and Your granting of tawfiq. You alone are our Wali (Protector) and Taskmaster, so forgive us, have mercy on us; You are the best of forgivers.

And decree for us in this world [that which is] good and [also] in the Hereafter... (to the end of the verse). 

'And decree for us in this world [that which is] good': Write down goodness for us in this world as well and write down goodness in the Hereafter as well. He omitted the word 'hasanah' (goodness) with the Hereafter because the mentioned itself is proof of the omitted. We have discussed the word 'Hudna' at another place; it means to return and to repent. In this word, there is also a subtle reminder for the Jews—that if there is any shame in them, they should uphold the honor of their name (Yahud). 

'He said, My punishment—I afflict with it whom I will': Moses' (PBUH) prayer was absolute, and the love he had for the nation demanded exactly this—that he prays for the goodness of both the world and the Hereafter for the nation with this same generality. In the Torah as well, the prayer of this occasion is mentioned with this same generality; but in response to it, Allah stated the actual regulation of His punishment and mercy. He said: As far as My punishment is concerned, I only send it down upon whom I will—'whom I will' has been clarified by us repeatedly that every 'willing' of God is under His wisdom and His justice; therefore, its meaning is: I send down My punishment only upon those who, under My law of justice, deserve it. As for My mercy, in this world it is general to everything; whoever has received the favor of existence has received it through My grace alone; whoever is receiving provision is receiving it from My table of bounty alone; rich and poor, king and beggar, disbeliever and believer—whoever has whatever he has, it is all bestowed by Me alone. But that mercy which relates to the Hereafter, I will keep it specific for those people who remain fearful of Me in this worldly life, keep paying Zakat, and especially for those who will believe in Our signs. 

The portion 'and those who believe in Our signs' (wa-alladhina hum bi-ayatina yu'minun) is particularly worthy of attention here. He did not say 'those who fear and give Zakat and believe in Our signs', but rather, changing the style, He said: 'And those who—it is they who believe in Our signs.' Through this change of style, the intention is to put special emphasis on the subject—that especially those people who will believe in Our signs. Those who keep an eye on the parallels of the Quran can understand that this is a hint toward that covenant and pledge which was taken from the Children of Israel to believe in the Prophets coming in the future, and the clarification of which is in the following verse of Ma'idah: 'And Allah said, I am with you if you establish prayer and give Zakat and believe in My Messengers and support them and lend Allah a goodly loan... then I will surely remove from you your misdeeds...' (Ma'idah: 12). This covenant, though it included all those Prophets and Messengers who came after Moses, but in it there was a special hint toward this 'Unlettered Prophet' (Nabi al-Ummi) whose advent Moses himself, as passed in Baqarah, had prophesied with great clarity. In Al-Imran, its mention is like this: 'And [recall] when Allah took the covenant of the Prophets, [saying], Whatever I give you of the Book and wisdom and then there comes to you a Messenger confirming what is with you, you [must] believe in him and support him. [Allah] said, Do you acknowledge and take this My obligation upon that? They said, We acknowledge. He said, Then bear witness, and I am with you among the witnesses' (Al-Imran: 81). 

This is that very covenant and pledge which is hinted at in the words 'and those who—it is they who believe in Our signs'. Meaning, in response to Moses' request, Allah clarified that those alone will deserve My eternal afterworldly mercy who will remain steadfast upon My covenant of Shariah, will support the coming Prophets and Messengers, and those among them who find the advent of My Final Messenger will believe in him and become his helpers and servants. When the point reached here, the Quran, by way of inclusion and commentary, made it clear who would be the referents of 'those who believe in Our signs' today. He said (next verse):

Those who follow the Messenger, the Unlettered Prophet, whom they find written with them in the Torah and the Gospel... (to the end of the verse). 

Meaning those people who follow this Unlettered Prophet whose prophecies exist in their own books, the Torah and the Gospel. By 'Unlettered Prophet' (Nabi al-Ummi), obviously the Prophet (PBUH) is meant. 'Rasul' (Messenger), 'Nabi' (Prophet), 'Ummi' (Unlettered)—all three words have entered here as his description and introduction. Toward the difference between 'Nabi' and 'Rasul', we have hinted at various places—that a 'Rasul' comes as a complete proof (hujjah) and complete justice for his nation. Through him, Allah's proof is completed over the nation; for this reason, if that nation does not believe, it is necessarily destroyed, and the Messenger and his companions necessarily gain dominance over the opponents. Whether this happens in the life of the Prophet himself or after his death; it is not necessary for a 'Nabi' to possess these characteristics. From this aspect, every 'Rasul' is necessarily a 'Nabi', but every 'Nabi' is not necessarily a 'Rasul'. The Holy Prophet (PBUH) was the collector of both the statuses—'Nabi' and 'Rasul'. 

The title 'Ummiyyun' (Unlettered ones) for the Children of Ishmael: We have discussed the word 'Ummi' under Ayah 20 of Al-Imran. This word manifests the relation of the Holy Prophet (PBUH) toward the Children of Ishmael. Since the Children of Ishmael were people unacquainted with teaching, learning, the Book, and Shariah—in that whole period which passed after their family elder Ishmael, no advent of a 'Nabi' or 'Rasul' had occurred among them—whereas during this same interval, countless Prophets were born within the Children of Israel, among whom Moses and the Messiah collected both the statuses of 'Nabi' and 'Rasul'—for this reason, the Children of Israel used to call the Children of Ishmael 'Ummiyyun'. Although the Israelites did not use this word only with a distinguishing attribute of the Ishmaelites in view, but the aspect of contempt for them in comparison to themselves was also in their minds; however, since 'ummi-ness' (illiteracy), nomadism, and alienation from the Book and Shariah existed within them as a matter of fact, therefore the Quran, as we will clarify in Surah Jumu'ah, used this word for them as a distinguishing title; and from some narrations it is known that the Holy Prophet (PBUH) himself also used this word in this meaning, and the Companions also used it without any sense of inferiority; as it were, it was a distinguishing title for them in comparison to the Children of Israel. 

Prophecies of the Unlettered Prophet in previous scriptures: Here, among the prophecies of the Torah and Gospel regarding the Holy Prophet (PBUH), mention of some—which are present in the current Torah and Gospel as well—has passed in the Tafseer of previous Surahs. Merely as a reminder, we provide their reference here again, which makes it clear that the People of the Book were acquainted with the advent of a Messenger Prophet within the Children of Ishmael from before, and its mention remained established among them. 'The Lord your God will raise up for you a Prophet like me from among you, from your brothers—it is to him you shall listen... and the Lord said to me... I will raise up for them a Prophet like you from among their brothers and I will put My words in his mouth, and he shall speak to them all that I command him. And whoever will not listen to My words that he shall speak in My name, I myself will require it of him' (Deuteronomy 18:15–19). From this, it was known that Moses himself had clarified this reality to the Children of Israel—that the coming one would be born among the Children of Ishmael, i.e., the 'Ummiyyun', because in this context, 'from among your brothers' or 'from among their brothers' can mean nothing other than that he will be from the Children of Ishmael. This was, as it were, the reiteration from Moses' blessed tongue of that very glad tiding which Abraham had given of the advent of a Messenger in the lineage of Ishmael. Its details have passed in Baqarah. From this, it also became clear that he would not be only a 'Nabi' but also a 'Rasul', because 'like me' and 'like you' refers to being like Moses, and Moses was sent as a Messenger to Pharaoh and his people, through whom the Children of Israel gained salvation and Allah destroyed Pharaoh and his people. The Holy Prophet (PBUH) was also similarly sent as a Messenger to his nation Quraish and the people of Arabia. 'The Lord came from Sinai and dawned from Seir upon us; He shone forth from Mount Paran; He came with ten thousand holy ones, and from His right hand went a fiery law for them' (Deuteronomy 33:2). In my view, 'fiery law' (atishi shari'at) is a hint toward that reality which the Messiah manifested—that 'his winnowing fork is in his hand, and he will clear his threshing floor and gather his wheat into the barn, but the chaff he will burn with unquenchable fire'; this accurately describes that characteristic of a Messenger toward which we hinted above—that he comes as justice for his nation and through him the decision between truth and falsehood is made. 

The prophecy of Prophet Isaiah is mentioned in these words: 'Behold my servant, whom I uphold, my chosen, in whom my soul delights; I have put my Spirit upon him; he will bring forth justice to the nations... he will not grow faint or be discouraged till he has established justice in the earth' (Isaiah 42:1–4). 

Consider the prophecy of the Messiah: 'Jesus said to them, Have you never read in the Scriptures: The stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone; this was the Lord's doing, and it is marvelous in our eyes? Therefore I tell you, the kingdom of God will be taken away from you and given to a people producing its fruits. And the one who falls on this stone will be broken to pieces; and when it falls on anyone, it will crush him' (Matthew 21:42–44).
