'And I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Helper, to be with you forever' (John 14:16). 'I will no longer talk much with you, for the ruler of this world is coming. He has no claim on me' (John 14:30). 

Ponder upon these prophecies. Jesus is the last Prophet among the Children of Israel; after him, no one has come among the Israelites. Then, who else could be the referent of these prophecies besides the Holy Prophet (PBUH)? Finally, who could be that stone which the builders had rejected but finally became the cornerstone? Whose glory is it that 'whoever falls on it will be broken to pieces and upon whom it falls, it will crush him'? Whose status has been described that 'he is the ruler of the world who will remain with the people forever and will tell those things which the Messiah was not in a position to tell'? Stubbornness and defiance are another matter, but any person who ponders these prophecies with justice and impartiality will cry out that if these can be true for anyone, they can be true only for the Unlettered Prophet and Final Messenger Muhammad (PBUH). No one besides the Unlettered Prophet can be their referent. 

The introduction of the Holy Prophet (PBUH): 'Who enjoins upon them what is right and forbids them what is wrong and makes lawful for them the good things...' Reference to these things has also been given merely as a description and introduction of the Holy Prophet (PBUH). The Jews had imposed many self-created restrictions upon themselves, and some restrictions had also been imposed upon them by Allah due to their rebellion. The removal of all these things was dependent on the advent of the Final Messenger. Thus, the advent of the Holy Prophet (PBUH) cut all these chains of theirs, but they did not value this favor due to their wretched deeds. We have written on this issue under Al-Imran Ayah 93 and Ma'idah Ayah 5, and it has also been discussed in detail in the Tafseer of Surah An'am; therefore, a hint is enough here. 

'So those who believe in him...': This is an invitation to faith for the People of the Book—that those who are the trustees of the previous prophecies regarding this Messenger, and who are to receive this blessing from his advent that they will become free from all unnatural bonds and restrictions—it is their right first of all to believe in him, introduce him to the people, support him against opponents, and follow this light, i.e., the Quran, which was sent with him from Allah. He said: Those who do these works will be the successful ones; all others will be deprived and ruined.

Say, [O Muhammad], 'O mankind, indeed I am the Messenger of Allah to you all, [of Him] to whom belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth...' (to the end of the verse). 

When the matter reached the mention of the Prophet (PBUH), an invitation for the Children of Israel to believe was also given through your blessed tongue on this occasion—that: O people, I have come as Allah's Messenger to all of you. To all people, meaning both the Children of Ishmael and Children of Israel, Arabs and non-Arabs, everyone. In the prophecies above as well, there is a clarification that although your advent will be among the Children of Ishmael, your messengership will be toward all, and all the nations of the world will receive a share from your blessings. Upon the Arabs—whether they be Children of Ishmael or Children of Israel—you established Allah's proof directly, and you also invited the kings and monarchs of the time, and after you, Allah placed the responsibility of this invitation and testimony upon your Ummah until the Day of Resurrection, which He honored with the high status of 'Witnesses for Allah on the earth'. 

'To whom belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth': Meaning the kingship of the heavens and the earth is only for God, whose Messenger I have come as. There is no deity except Him. He gives life and He causes death. The removal and appointment of nations is in His control; so do not avoid believing in Allah and His Unlettered Prophet Messenger—whose prophecies are present in your own books—by getting caught in any misplaced prejudice, any wrong confidence, or any groundless pride and vanity. 

'Who believes in Allah and His words': In this, there is a hint toward that which was mentioned above in the prophecy of Moses—that 'I will put My words in his mouth and he shall speak to them all that I command him'; the meaning became that do not consider this claim of prophethood of the Prophet and this invitation of his to be based on some suspicion, some thought, or any desire to gain superiority and leadership over others; this is merely that which is being recited to you which is coming from Allah. The Prophet himself believes in Allah and these words of His and is inviting you toward the same faith. This is not some invention of his own. Secondly, in this is also latent that threat which was mentioned in the prophecy above—that since this is a thing sent by God, therefore there is no room for any decrease, increase, deletion, or addition on the part of the Prophet. If the Prophet showed any compromise in its preaching, God would take him to account; and if the people did not recognize its right despite all this arrangement, they would be taken to account.

And among the people of Moses is a community which guides by truth and by it establishes justice. 

We have clarified at more than one place previously that wherever the Quran has intensely rebuked the Children of Israel for covenant-breakings and bad deeds, it has also praised that small group among them which remained firm upon truth and justice and by whose virtue the Israelites kept receiving respite despite their crimes. Here also, that same group is being hinted at, and the indefiniteness (tankir) of the word 'Ummah' points toward its smallness. 'Yahdun' (guides) and 'Ya'dilun' (establishes justice) are imperfect (mudari') forms indicating continuity, because the context proves that an auxiliary verb is omitted here. The meaning would be: While such worthless and misbehaving ones were born among Moses' people as mentioned above, at the same time, there has also remained a group of such people among them who have been deciding the affairs of the people according to truth. That is, good people have been born in both groups—scholars and judges. Similarly, a group, though in small numbers, existed at the time of the revelation of these verses, and these were the people who believed in the Holy Prophet (PBUH); its mention here is intended for their encouragement, and in it is an extremely subtle hint that this invitation the Prophet has given to the People of the Book will influence such righteous ones and they will believe in the Unlettered Prophet. 

With this verse, that inclusion or summary which began with 'those who follow the Messenger' ended, and the sequence of the speech again connected with that same account of the Children of Israel which was being described.

And We divided them into twelve descendant tribes [as distinct] nations... (to the end of the verse). 

And [recall] when it was said to them, 'Dwell in this city and eat from it wherever you will...' (to the end of the verse). 

And [recall] when it was said to them, 'Dwell in this city and eat from it wherever you will...' (to the end of the verse). 

But those who wronged among them changed [the word] to a statement other than that which had been said to them... (to the end of the verse). 

With minor variations of words, these verses have already passed in Baqarah. Their Tafseer has been described there with full detail; see Ayahs 57–60 of Baqarah. Discussion on the word 'Asbat' has also occurred under Ayah 136 of Baqarah. The word 'Taqti'' (dividing/cutting) is used here in a good sense. That is, the offspring of one single father flourished and spread in the form of twelve families, and We increased and spread every family in the form of 'Ummahs' and nations, and accordingly We also favored them with Our blessings and mercies; but they showed ingratitude for every favor.

And ask them about the town that was by the sea... (to the end of the verse). 

The incident which is being hinted at has been mentioned in Baqarah Ayah 65–66. It has been clarified there. Here, we will only shed light on those things which were not described there. The style of 'And ask them' (wa-s'alhum) manifests rebuke and scolding. 'Them' refers to the Jews. The meaning is: if they, despite all their misdeeds that were described, do not desist from the assumption of their purity and superiority and are sitting as God's favorites and darlings, then ask them a bit about the story of that town which desecrated the Sabbath and in punishment for which God made it an example of lesson. The Quran has not given more clarification of this town than that it was by the sea. There is no mention of it in the Torah either, but the Quran's manner of speech manifests that the Jews of the time of the Quran's revelation knew it as a famous tradition of their history. Thus, in Baqarah its mention is like this: 'And you have already known those who transgressed among you concerning the Sabbath' (wa-laqad 'alimtumu alladhina i'tadau minkum fi al-sabti). Obviously, in this style face-to-face with the Jews, only that thing could be said which held fame among them, otherwise they would certainly have refuted this statement of the Quran. Some people have stated it to be a city near Ayla and Aqaba. This could be correct. Cities by the seaside, if the location is suitable, become commercial centers and progress a lot. The region of Aqaba has gained fame in the history of the Children of Israel since ancient times. This was also the center of Solomon's (PBUH) naval fleet. We have discussed the word 'Qaryah' at another place—that in Arabic this word is not only for small settlements but also applies to large central populations. 

Research on the word 'Shurra'an' and the nature and wisdom of the fish appearing: 'When their fish came to them on their Sabbath day appearing' (idhtatihim hitanuhum yawma sabtihim shurra'an). 'Shurra'an' is the plural of 'Shari'ah'. When this word is spoken for spears, it refers to straight raised spears. Here, this word has come for fish, so it refers to fish with their heads raised. Stand by the side of a pond of reared fish at the times they emerge, and you will see this attractive sight—that the fish will appear with their heads raised above the surface as if they have their spears straightened. In hunting grounds by the seaside, this sight would be even more charming and greed-inducing. In the Shariah of the Jews, work and roaming and hunting etc. were forbidden on the day of 'Sabt', i.e., Saturday, but they could not be patient. They invented various types of legal tricks (hilah) for hunting fish on the day of Sabt. The Divine Law (Sunnat Allah) is that when a nation increases in any disobedience to the point of persistence and does not desist even with the advice of the good ones, its trial in that matter becomes harder and harder so that it may fill its measure well. Thus, Allah created this same situation for the Children of Israel. On ordinary days, these fish would not be seen or seen very little, but on the day of Sabt, it seemed as if a wedding procession had arrived at their place. This thing would further ignite their greed. It is famous that a person's desire for the thing he is forbidden from increases even more, and desire is such a calamity that by its effect, a person's vision also changes sometimes. In the absence of desire, a thing that appears like an ounce, in the state of the dominance of desire, that same thing begins to appear like a pound. It is famous that in estimating beauty, 'the beautiful vision' has more of a role than 'the beauty' itself. The Israelites fell into this double fitnah due to their wretched deeds and then were corrupted to such an extent that to say nothing of the teaching and admonition of the righteous, they were not even warned by God's punishment. Finally, Allah cursed them. 

Under Surah Ma'idah Ayah 94, we have stated that just as the Israelites were forbidden from hunting on the day of Sabt, similarly we were forbidden from land-hunting while in the state of Ihram, and we were also emphasized to keep avoiding the type of trial in which the Israelites fell and destroyed themselves. 'O you who have believed, Allah will surely test you with something of the game that your hands and spears [can] reach, that Allah may make evident those who fear Him in the unseen. And whoever transgresses after that—for him is a painful punishment.' Ponder upon the words 'their fish came to them on their Sabbath day appearing' and 'that your hands and spears reach' by placing them opposite each other, and the similarity between the two will become well clarified.

And when a community among them said... From this it becomes clear that the people of this town adopted this path of wretchedness despite those who tried to show them and advise them. It was not that there was no one to alert them; there were such servants of Allah there who not only tried to stop them from this crime but tried to such an extent that a group from among their own companions now considered further advising to be entirely useless and said: What is the benefit in advising such people who are either going to be destroyed or at least caught in the grip of some severe punishment. But those servants of Allah did not accept this point of view, rather they gave them this answer: Our work of advising must continue. If these people do not accept, we will stand free from our duty before Allah, and what wonder that they might just accept; so if they accepted, that is what is desired. 

The limit of responsibility regarding Enjoining the Good: From this, the limit of responsibility which is imposed upon every believer to stop others from evil and invite toward good is determined. It was known that in this, no such stage arrives when the caller and adviser can assume that now the duty of calling and advice has been fulfilled, therefore the non-compliant should be left for Divine punishment; rather, this work should be done until the last moment of life even if not a single person emerges to value the advice. Before Allah, only such people will be considered free from their duty. Those people will not be free before Allah who, although not involved in evil themselves, spend their lives being entirely unconcerned with the good or evil of others. Detailed discussion on this subject will come under Anfal Ayah 25.

And when they forgot that by which they had been reminded, We saved those who had forbidden evil... Meaning when they forgot those warnings which were given to them and were not willing to think and understand in any way, Allah sent a severe punishment upon them in punishment for this disobedience and covenant-breaking of theirs, from which only those people remained safe who used to stop the people from evil. From this, it was known that when any decisive punishment of Allah descends upon such collective disobediences, only those people remain safe from its grip who are those who stop from evil.

So when they were insolent as to what they had been forbidden... (to the end of the verse). This is the mention of the curse which fell upon those disobedient ones and the mention of which has also passed under Baqarah 65 and Ma'idah 61. 

Curse is the worst punishment: Allah's curse upon any nation is a thing even more severe than the punishment which annihilates a nation, because curse...

And [recall] when your Lord declared that He would surely send against them until the Day of Resurrection those who would afflict them with the worst torment... (to the end of the verse). 

The correct meaning of 'Ta'adhdhana' is to inform people of a final decision. The meaning is that Allah, due to such covenant-breakings of the Children of Israel, had informed them through their Prophets that He would keep imposing such people upon them until the Day of Resurrection who would keep afflicting them with extremely severe torments. Moses warned them of this type of warning repeatedly, and the later Prophets also informed them of it in extremely manifest words. In Leviticus it is: 'But if you will not listen to me and will not do all these commandments... and break my covenant, then I will do this to you... I will set my face against you, and you shall be struck down before your enemies. Those who hate you shall rule over you' (Leviticus 26:14–17). Similarly, in the book of Deuteronomy it is: 'Your sons and your daughters shall be given to another people, while your eyes look on and fail with longing for them all day long, but you shall be helpless' (Deuteronomy 28:32). The practical confirmation of this decision of Allah is recorded in the pages of history. For this, read the history from Nebuchadnezzar and Titus up to Germany's Hitler. In every era, you will find the extremely bone-chilling story of the humiliation and destruction of this nation. It should be noted that the occurrence of an interval of respite in between does not negate this decision of Allah. The Quran's style of speech is itself a witness that they will keep receiving intervals as well, but every interval will prove to be a precursor of some fresh calamity for them. This gathering of theirs taking place in the Holy Land today, this 'nest-building' is also an invitation for a new storm after which their entire collected power will be wiped out at once, if Allah wills. A hint is enough here; its clarification will come in the Tafseer of Surah Bani Israel. 

In 'Indeed, your Lord is swift in retribution; but indeed, He is Forgiving and Merciful', the reality of this Divine decision has been shown in the mirror of Divine Attributes—that no one should think of God as unconcerned or detached from the affairs of this world. Those who go astray from His path, He also gives them very severe punishment, and those who return toward Him, for them He is also Greatly Forgiving and Merciful. The attribute of 'swift' (sari') along with retribution ('iqab) is to strike at the dullness of those people who mistake God's delay for injustice/neglect; the meaning is: no one should fall into misunderstanding from the respite He has bestowed. When He catches, He catches in an instant, and those whom He catches also feel that it had not even turned from morning to evening when they were seized.

And We scattered them throughout the earth as nations. Among them are the righteous, and among them are [others] besides that... (to the end of the verse). 'Waqatta'nahum' (and We divided/scattered them) has already passed above in Ayah 160; there, as we hinted, it is in a good sense. here, this word is indicating, contrary to that, the circumstances of their era of dispersion and fragmentation. The organization of the Children of Israel which Moses carried out reached its full peak in the era of David and Solomon (PBUT). After Solomon (PBUH), weakness began to arise in this organization and then gradually circumstances became so bad that the Jews became subjugated even in their center. To the extent that they became scattered in different parts of the world as groups. Although individually there were both good and bad among them, but collectively they had become deprived of those attributes which were necessary to keep them elevated in the world as a nation. Allah, according to His Sunnah, tested them with all kinds of conditions—good and bad—so that they might return toward their Lord; but their desires had so dominated them that in front of them their willpower had become entirely paralyzed. Even while feeling that whatever they were doing was wrong, they would keep doing the same works and deceive their conscience that 'we are the offspring of God's favorites and beloveds, God will surely forgive us'.

And there followed them a succession who inherited the Scripture [while] taking the commodities of this lower life... (to the end of the verse). 

In Arabic, when the word 'Khalaf' (succession) comes with a sukun on the 'lam', it comes in the meaning of bad successors. This is a hint toward the gradual decline of the Jews—that day by day their moral conditions kept becoming worse and worse, until such bad people became heirs of the Book (Torah) who, on one hand, claim to be heirs of the Divine Book, and on the other hand, such is the state of low-spiritedness and baseness that they do not have the courage to leave even a forbidden morsel they see. If any prick arises in the conscience due to being adherents of the Divine Book, they amuse it with those false hopes which they have nourished in their hearts without any proof or evidence—that we are the chosen nation, we are offspring of Prophets like Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the fire of Hell is forbidden for us, Allah will surely forgive us for the sake of His beloved servants. Thus, they have made their conscience and faith so blunt that after one forbidden thing, if another opportunity for similar forbidden-eating emerges, they slip upon it as well. Due to this baseness of theirs, they are later compared to a dog who keeps its tongue out at all times, whether you coax it or scold it. 

'Had not the covenant of the Scripture been taken from them...': Meaning regarding the Book, as passed in Ayah 145 and mentioned repeatedly in the Torah, this covenant was taken from them—that they should hold fast to the instructions given in this Book, not move away even a hair's breadth, not attribute anything against the truth to God; so how did they open this path of forbidden-eating and forbidden-acting and from where did they provide the authority of Shariah for it?! 

'And they had studied what is in it': Meaning they have also read this Book well; it is not that they are entirely unaware of it. We discussed the word 'Dars' (studying) under An'am Ayah 105—that its original meaning is to wear out. When a book is read repeatedly, and that too by placing the finger, the page wears out; for this reason, it began to be used for reading well. Here, this word has been used for the Jews as an irony/sarcasm—that by reading the Book, they wore it out, but the state remained such that even after reading the whole of Zulaikha, it could not be known whether Zulaikha was a woman or a man. 'And the home of the Hereafter is better for those who fear God'—meaning these 'dogs of the world' who, setting aside the Book and Shariah, have pursued this world, they have no estimation of the favors and successes of the Hereafter, whereas the actual thing to be desired is that; but where is that intellect in these foolish people?

But those who hold fast to the Scripture and establish prayer—indeed, We will not allow to be lost the reward of the reformers. 

Encouragement for the Reformers (Muslihin) of the People of the Book: 'Tamsik' and 'Tamassuk' both have one and the same meaning, i.e., to catch something firmly or to hold on. The meaning of the verse in the light of the verse above is that while these unfortunate people left Allah's Book and set out behind their desires, those who hold Allah's Book firmly and make the arrangement of prayer, those people become those who reform the creation, and Allah will not waste the reward of such reformers. The establishment of prayer is mentioned here also as an attribute of holding fast to the Book, and this thing is in fact the guardian of every Divine covenant, as we have mentioned at another place. In the verse, a portion is omitted according to the rule of brevity (ijaz). The whole point is, as it were, like this: 'And those who hold the Book firmly and who have made the arrangement of prayer...' In this verse, there is also the encouragement for that small group of the People of the Book which remained firm upon truth despite the general corruption of the nation and which finally was honored with Islam.

And [recall] when We suspended the mountain over them as if it were a canopy... (to the end of the verse). 

The Tafseer of this verse has passed under Baqarah Ayah 63. This is a hint toward that covenant which was taken from the Jews at the foot of the mountain to remain firm upon the Book, and on this occasion, Allah also made them witness His majesty and power so that they remember that the God with whom they are making a covenant is the One of boundless power and might. But they forgot everything in pursuit of their desires.

Subject of Ayahs 172 to 206: The upcoming subject holds the status of the conclusion of the Surah. We have stated in the introduction that the primary addressees in this Surah are the Quraish. They were first told the circumstances of those nations of their own land which had passed in the past; then the whole history of the Children of Israel—who were present before them—was also told in detail. The purpose of telling the history of these nations, as we hinted above, is that the Creator and Owner of this universe is not unconcerned and detached from the conditions and affairs of His world. He has always been sending His Messengers for the reform and guidance of the creation, and whenever those Messengers have come, they have come as the balance/scale of removal and appointment. Those nations that denied them, God erased them; and those people who confirmed them, they became prosperous. This same decisive stage is now before the Quraish. 

At Ayah 171, this series of historical accounts ended. Now ahead, addressing the Quraish, he has first reminded them of that Covenant of Nature (Ahad-e-Fitrat) which Allah took from all the Children of Adam regarding His Monotheism and Lordship, and making it the foundation, He has established proof against them—that tomorrow before God, you cannot make this excuse that you were unaware of this thing. For this, God has both taken a covenant from you and now, for its reminder, has also sent His Messenger to you. It should be clear that the Arabs were 'Ummi' (unlettered) unacquainted with the Book and Shariah; therefore, the foundation of discussion with them could be established either upon their well-known history or upon the obvious truths of nature and intellect. Thus, He first presented historical events before them from the correct perspective, then moved the conversation forward on the basis of the Covenant of Nature. 

After the reminder of the Covenant of Nature, He presented the parable of the Children of Israel before them—that do not become unappreciative and ungrateful of God's favor like this nation. Allah bestowed great favor upon this nation, but it, as described above, proved to be a very base nation. Allah always wanted to elevate it, but it kept leaning only toward baseness. Just as if you love a dog, it keeps its tongue out, and if you scold it, it keeps its tongue out; similarly, warning and favor are both the same for them. 

After that, an invitation was given to value the favors of eyes, ears, and heart-mind and to take the correct work from them—that do not walk as blind followers in the matter of religion that just because ancestors used to worship them, you too keep worshipping them without understanding; remember that those who do not use the capabilities of their heart and mind and the powers of their hearing and sight and keep stumbling despite reminders and warnings, Allah will hurl all of them into Hell. 

After that, He drew attention toward the Prophet—that the person who is alerting you today of the coming great danger is not some madman or lunatic. If you reflect upon the system of the universe, your own intellect will bear witness that this world is not some play or toy. Behind it is great wisdom and purpose; therefore, it is no wonder that the time for your decision has drawn near of which this Prophet is cautioning you. No difference is created in the fact of this reality being an immutable reality by the fact that the Prophet cannot tell you its exact time. A Prophet is not a knower of the unseen (Ghayb); he is only a bringer of glad tidings and a warner. 

After that, there is an expression of sorrow over the misfortune of man—that he receives all favors from God but sings the praises of others and makes such things partners of God which can neither bring him benefit nor any harm. 

At the end, He instructed the Prophet (PBUH) for patience and steadfastness over the nonsensical chatter of the opponents and their meaningless objections and guided toward that which is the medium and means for the attainment of this patience. Now, in this light, recite the verses: 

'And [recall] when your Lord took from the Children of Adam—from their loins—their descendants and made them testify of themselves, [saying], Am I not your Lord? They said, Yes, we testify. [This]—lest you should say on the Day of Resurrection, Indeed, we were of this unaware.' 

'And [recall] when your Lord took...': We have clarified at another place that through 'idh' (when/recall), a reminder is also given of such a factual matter which is a reality according to the speaker, regardless of whether the addressees have forgotten it or are deviant from it. Similarly, regarding the address to the singular (your Lord), we have clarified that contextually it is also in the meaning of plural, and in that case, as it were, every single person of the addressee group is addressed individually. 

The benefit of the restriction 'from their loins' (min zhuhurihim): 'From the Children of Adam—from their loins'. 'From their loins' occurs as an appositive (badal) of 'from the Children of Adam', and the purpose of bringing this appositive is to manifest the reality that the reality being described here regarding the Children of Adam does not relate to the Children of Adam of any specific era, but relates to all the Children of Adam whoever are going to be born until the Day of Resurrection. 

God took the acknowledgment of His being the Lord from all the Children of Adam. 

'And He made them testify of themselves, Am I not your Lord? They said, Yes, we testify.' Meaning Allah has taken the acknowledgment of His Lordship from all the Children of Adam, and that in this way—that He asked them: Am I not your Lord? In its response, everyone has taken this acknowledgment: Yes, certainly You are our Lord and we are witnesses to it. It should be noted that it is clear from the words that Allah did not take this acknowledgment merely of the fact that He is 'Allah', but took it of the fact that He alone is 'Lord' (Rabb) as well. It is necessary to keep this in mind because the Arabs did not have a denial of Allah being 'Allah', but they had made others besides Allah as 'Lord', whereas in the Covenant of Nature, the acknowledgment is only of Allah's Lordship. 

'Lest you should say on the Day of Resurrection, Indeed, we were of this unaware.' Before this, 'karahatan' (lest) or some synonymous word is omitted, for this reason its translation will be: lest you make an excuse on the Day of Resurrection. From this, the point emerges that as far as Monotheism (Tawhid) and the obvious truths of nature are concerned, the accountability on the Day of Resurrection from every person will be on the basis of this acknowledgment which was mentioned, regardless of whether the call of some Prophet reached him or not. If the call of some Prophet reached him, this is, as it were, a further proof established against him—that his responsibility became twofold. But if the Prophet's call did not reach, this will not be able to become an excuse in the matter of the obvious truths of nature. For accountability upon them, this Covenant of Nature is sufficient.

'Or [lest] you say, It was only that our fathers practiced polytheism before, and we were but descendants after them. Then would You destroy us for what the falsifiers have done?' 

Similarly, this excuse of the people will also be of no use—that our fathers and grandfathers were polytheists, we were born in their homes and then naturally we followed their way; for this reason, this crime is not ours but theirs; the punishment for it should be given to them and not to us. The meaning is that this acknowledgment of Monotheism is deposited within the nature of man; for this reason, even the excuse of external influences for deviation from this internal testimony will not be heard from anyone before God.

And thus do We detail the signs, and perhaps they will return. In this verse, the context proves that the coordinate (ma'tuf 'alayh) of 'and perhaps they will return' is omitted. If it is opened up, the meaning of the verse according to us would be: 'In this way We are detailing Our signs so that We establish Our proof upon the people in such a way that not a minor excuse remains for them, and so that after this clarification, those among them who wish to repent from polytheism and return toward their Lord may return.' The meaning is: After taking the mentioned covenant from man, this is a further grace and favor from Allah that He also told the full detail of this Covenant and Pledge so that those who wish to repent for their mistake may repent, and those who wish to remain stubborn in their defiance should consider themselves alone responsible for its consequences, and not try to put the burden of blame on anyone else's neck. 

Some questions regarding the Covenant of Nature and their answers: The Quran has mentioned this covenant as a matter of fact, and has described its importance as being—that as far as God's Lordship is concerned, every person will be accountable before God based on this covenant alone. On the basis of this importance, this question arises: Is the memory of any such event of the world of the unseen preserved in the mind of man, which is mentioned here? If it is not so, then how can it be believed that in fact man has made any such acknowledgment, and on its basis he is accountable before God in the matter of Monotheism? The answer to this question is: As far as this acknowledgment is concerned, it exists within the nature of every human being. As for its occasion and place and its history—if they are not remembered, it does not have any effect on the correctness and truth of the acknowledgment itself; at most, someone can say: 'I remember the acknowledgment, but I do not remember its occasion and place.' So, Allah told its occasion and place—that this acknowledgment was taken from man by God in the world of the unseen before he came into existence, and only God could tell this because the knowledge of the unseen is only with Him. For establishing proof against man, this fact is sufficient that the memory of this acknowledgment exists within him. But then this question arises: if this acknowledgment exists within man from the very beginning, then its demand was that man, after coming into existence, should have started religion with pure God-worship and Monotheism; but our new philosophers state that man started religion with polytheism. The appearance of various accidents in the world created within him a fear of different unseen powers. This fear created within him the idea of worshipping unseen powers; thus he started their worship. Then gradually, as his knowledge increased, leaving those superstitious deities, he reached the stage of the acknowledgment of Monotheism. We have refuted this delusion of the new philosophers in the Tafseer of Surah Fatihah as well, and have done it in more detail in our book 'Haqiqat-e-Shirk' (The Reality of Polytheism). According to us, this discourse is entirely illogical. We have clarified in the mentioned books that 'fear' is the name of the apprehension of the loss or snatching away of some thing which man possesses and which is also dear and beloved. It is proven from this that the consciousness and passion of the favor (ni'mah), the Bestower (mun'im), and his thankfulness is prior to the passion of fear and its causes; for this reason, man did not start religion with fear, but started religion with the thankfulness of his Bestower Sustainer and His worship; but then under various causes—the detail of which we have given in our book 'Haqiqat-e-Shirk'—moving away from this straight path, he went out onto various side-paths. A group says that when man is born, his nature is entirely a blank slate (tabula rasa). Whatever imprints emerge upon it emerge later after the birth of perception and consciousness merely due to the effect of the environment. But this idea is mere delusion. The question is: the environment—whatever effects it puts on the newcomers—from where did it take them itself? Whatever stock of moist and dry traditions the environment has, it is all a wonder of the building or ruining of its nature. Those points which have emerged from the actual source of its nature are 'Khayr' (good), 'Adl' (justice), and 'Ma'ruf' (recognized good), and those upon which the excesses of their desires have dominated have become 'Sharr' (evil) and 'Zulm' (wrong). Regarding the matter of God, this same excess has been committed by him. Within his original nature, only the acknowledgment of one God, Alone, having no partner, is latent. On this much, the polytheists and monotheists of the world all agree; for this reason, this thing is not in need of the establishment of any proof; however, the responsibility lies upon the claimants of polytheism to present their proof for the addition they make to this 'One'—of other deities. Thus, the Quran has demanded this very thing from them at every place—that they bring some proof in favor of their self-created partners. 

The entire philosophy of the Quran is in fact based on the nature (Fitrah) of man. Its claim is that whatever beliefs and deeds and whatever goodnesses and virtues it teaches, and from whatever evils it stops—among them not a single thing is being imposed upon him from his 'outside', but rather he is being given a reminder of those very things which are deposited within his own nature, but which he has ignored due to falling behind his immediate pleasures. The Quran, for this reason, has called itself 'Dhikr' and 'Dhikra' (Reminder), the meaning of which is 'giving a reminder', and has described its teaching and call with the word 'Tadhkir', the meaning of which is 'reminding of forgotten or ignored realities'. 

This is a perfectly clear reality, but the magic of Marx and Freud has worked upon the new educated people of this era. The 'dust-play' of these oppressors has made people blind in such a way that now people see nothing within man besides the belly and the genitals. According to them, the entire thought and philosophy of man is revolving on only these two axes. Like that proverbial mouse who found a piece of turmeric and opened a grocer's shop—Marx and Freud modeled the whole thought and philosophy and all religion and morality upon the belly and genitals, and thus those people who were already slaves of the belly and genitals also found two such guides of whom they give reference with pride—that they are not 'without-a-master' (be-piray), but rather they too possess the honor of relation and discipleship.

And recite to them the news of him to whom We gave Our signs, but he detached himself from them; so Satan followed him, and he became of the deviators. 

In 'alayhim' (to them), the 'hum' refers to the Quraish who were addressed in the verses above and given the reminder of the Covenant of Nature. Although 'al-ladhi' (the one who) originally comes for a definite/specific noun, but in parables/examples, it is not necessary that a specific person be meant who also exists in the outside world; rather, the speaker, keeping in view the one whose parable he wishes to present, adorns such a persona/description that applies fully to him. Since the intention is only the depiction of an event, the demand of eloquence is to mention it with definite/specific words instead of indefinite, so that the parable is personified in the form of a specific person and comes before the listener in such a way as if he has not only heard his story but seen it himself with his own eyes. Here, by this 'al-ladhi', the referent is the Jews as a nation, whose parable has been given with such a person whom God favored with His signs but he did not value them, rather he threw off this robe of honor. The result was that—according to that Divine Law mentioned in Surah Zukhruf, Ayah 26: 'And whoever turns away from the remembrance of the Most Merciful—We appoint for him a devil, and he is to him a companion'—Satan set out after him and he became among the deviants. The point that this is not the mention of a specific person but a parable of a nation that denied God's signs is clear from the words of the Quran itself; thus, He stated after this: 'That is the parable of the people who denied Our signs' (Ayah 176). For this reason, according to us, there is no need to take 'al-ladhi' as referring to some Balaam son of Beor. There was not only one Balaam son of Beor among the Jews for whom an example would be given. From the history of the Jews described with detail above, it is itself clear that this whole nation as a nation has been 'Balaam son of Beor' since the beginning. The meaning of 'insilakh' is to take off clothes and become naked. 'Insalakha min thiyabihi'—he stripped; meaning Allah favored them with the honor and robe of His Book, but they threw it off and remained as entirely naked and stripped.

And if We had willed, We could have elevated him thereby, but he adhered [instead] to the earth and followed his own desire. So his likeness is as the likeness of a dog: if you load/chase him, he pants, or if you leave him, he [still] pants. That is the likeness of the people who denied Our signs. So relate the stories that perhaps they will give thought. 

The meaning of 'akhlada ila al-shay'' (adhered to/clung to the thing) is to lean toward and be inclined toward a thing in such a way that the person belongs only to it. This is the description of that Divine Law which He favored in the matter of guidance and misguidance. It is that—those whom Allah favors with His signs, if they value them, then through them He bestows upon them the elevation of both religion and the world—their intellect gains height from them and their spirit gains ascension (Mi'raj) from them; but those who, after receiving these signs, still set out only after their own desires and walk like a dog sniffing the ground, then Allah leaves such people to their desires and they fall into the hands of Satan. 

The parable of the Jews with a dog: 'So his likeness is as the likeness of a dog: if you load/chase him, he pants, or if you leave him, he pants.' Now this is the parable of their constant leaning toward baseness and following desires, and see what a heart-settling and factual parable it is. Above in Ayahs 168–169, their state was described as being—that God tested them with both His favors and His warnings. 'We tested them with good things and bad things.' But they remained so helpless in the hands of their desires that they were not influenced even a little either by favors or by warnings, rather every greed made them stumble and they knelt before every desire. 'Taking the commodities of this lower life and saying, We will be forgiven; and if there comes to them a commodity like it, they take it.' Their this state has been compared with a dog. You would have seen a dog that it never walks with its neck raised in a straight line, but always walks sniffing the ground and evaluating every pure and impure thing with its nose—that perhaps some thing to eat might be found; its guide is not its eye but its nose which is the 'clue-finder' of its desires. Then its characteristic is also this—that it always keeps its tongue out; coax it, love it—still its state will remain the same; drive it away, scold it—still its manner will be the same. This is its greed and baseness which is so dominant over its nature that it does not separate from it in any condition. It will remain hungry—still you will find it in this same state; feed it to full stomach—still you will see it in the same form. The dogs of 'gentlemen' are adorned with velvet covers, silk collars, and silver bells—they eat meat to their fill and drink milk—but even sitting beside the master inside the car, they will have their tongue out, and even while roaming inside the park, they will walk behind their nose searching and sniffing in the ground. He stated: Exactly the same is the case with the Jews. They have not remained in any control over their soul. Their willpower has become entirely disabled. Now they are slaves of their desires and servants of their greed, and greed and baseness has become their second nature. Their forms are of humans, but their nature has been molded in the mold of the dog's instinct. 

In the verse, 'tahmil 'alayhi' (if you load him/chase him), in my view, is in the meaning of 'tahmil al-'asa 'alayhi' (if you raise the stick against him) or 'tahmil al-hajar 'alayhi' (if you throw the stone at him)—his state will remain one and the same. 'Lahth' means taking out the tongue, and its dominant usage is for the dog. 

'That is the likeness of the people who denied Our signs'. From this portion, as we hinted above, it became clear that this parable is not a parable of a person, but is a parable of the nation that denied Allah's signs. And by it, as we clarified, the Jews are meant. Under Surah Nahl Ayahs 91–92, if Allah wills, we will further discuss this aspect of the parable. 

This parable of the Jews, as we hinted above, has been told to the Quraish, and its purpose is to incite the Quraish to value the favor which was reaching them from Allah in the form of the final Shariah; but they, instead of valuing it, were recoiling from it. The Prophet (PBUH) was instructed: tell them the story of the nation that denied the Divine signs—that those whom Allah favors with His signs, He wants to bestow upon them the elevations of both the earth and the heavens through those signs, provided they value them, adopt them, and seek guidance from them in life. If they do not value them, rather remain slaves of their own desires, then their state becomes the same as that of the Jews.

Evil is the likeness of the people who denied Our signs and used to wrong themselves. 

The meaning is: the nation that denies Allah's signs, due to the slavery to its desires, remains entirely base. Its example, as described above, is extremely abominable and disgusting. So do not adopt this path. Those who adopt this path do not spoil anything of Allah's signs, but rather spoil only themselves. By being unappreciative of these signs, they themselves, instead of a crown of honor, adopt a collar of humiliation for themselves with their own hands.

Whoever Allah guides—he is the [rightly] guided; and whoever He sends astray—it is those who are the losers. 

This is both a consolation for the Prophet (PBUH) and a threat and warning for the Quraish. The meaning is: only those will find guidance whom God grants the tawfiq (success/ability) for guidance; and God's tawfiq is received only by those who are seekers of guidance and use the capabilities granted by God. Those who spend their lives by tying the bandage of desires over their eyes and their hearts and minds, God leaves them for misguidance, and the truly unsuccessful and unfortunate people are those.

And We have certainly created for Hell many of the jinn and mankind. They have hearts with which they do not understand, they have eyes with which they do not see, and they have ears with which they do not hear. Those are like cattle; rather, they are more astray. It is they who are the heedless. 

Those who remain deprived of guidance: Now He told who are the people who remain deprived of guidance and finally become fuel for Hell. He stated that these are those among jinn and humans whom God gave hearts but they do not take the work of understanding from them, He bestowed eyes upon them but they do not take the work of seeing from them, He granted ears to them but they do not take the work of hearing from them. Obviously, this understanding, seeing, and hearing is according to its actual/real meaning. That is, they have applied all their capabilities only in the following of their desires. Beyond that, there is no breath or motive found within them to hear, understand, and adopt any thing. He stated: these people are like cattle, rather even more mindless than them. They are like cattle because just as the seeking and search of cattle is limited only to the requirements of their belly and body, similarly their running and striving also is limited only to their material needs and desires. And they are more mindless than cattle for the reason that cattle, in any case, benefit from all the capabilities of their instinct—in that they do not leave any deficiency—but man, those high capabilities which Power has placed within his nature, he not only does not take actual benefit from them, but sometimes such actions are committed by him which are never committed by an ox or a donkey. For example, man, being man, becomes so mindless and crooked-of-understanding that he starts the worship of trees, stones, and animals; but a donkey or an ox can never commit such mindlessness. He stated: it is these people who are the truly and actually unaware, because this unawareness is not even in the cattle. From this, it became clear that this statement 'We have created for Hell'—its meaning is not that He created them for Hell from the bellies of their mothers. From the bellies of the mothers, Allah created them with the capabilities of heart, mind, hearing, and sight; but He made the regulation such—that those who will take the correct benefit from these capabilities, Allah will guide them toward Paradise, and those who will not take benefit from them, He will hurl them into Hell. It should be noted that this threat is for the Quraish.

And to Allah belong the best names, so invoke Him by them. And leave [the company of] those who practice deviation concerning His names. They will be recompensed for what they have been doing. 

This verse relates to the Covenant of Nature of Monotheism mentioned above in Ayah 172. In between, mention had come of those people who did not value Allah's signs and, after receiving extremely high capabilities from God, became blind and deaf. Now He stated: for Allah there are only good attributes and only good names, so call Him by those attributes, and leave those people to their state who have adopted the way of misguidance in the matter of Divine Attributes—make partners for Him or accept sons and daughters for Him. These points are those which negate God's attributes of Divinity, His glory of uniqueness, His power, His independence, and His knowledge. God should be characterized only with those attributes which can have a connection with His Divinity and His peerlessness and matchlessness; no such attribute should be attributed toward Him which brings the Creator and stands Him in the rank of the creatures. The word 'Ilhad' (deviation) is used here in the meaning of the desecration of Divine Attributes. In Arabic, if it is said 'fulanun alhada fi al-haram', its meaning would be 'he deemed its sanctity lawful and violated it'. 'Yulhiduna fi asma'ihi' means those who desecrate and disrespect God's attributes, i.e., associate such attributes with Him which are insulting to His Essence and Attributes and from which He is pure and elevated. This statement 'leave them' means that if they do not accept, then leave their matter to Allah. 'They will be recompensed for what they have been doing'—they will soon suffer the punishment for what they are doing themselves.

And among those We created is a community which guides by truth and by it establishes justice. Meaning, among God's creation, all are not of the same kind. If on one side there are those blind and deaf mentioned above, then there are also some such who use their intellect, recognize the truth, and accordingly guide the people and decide the affairs. Above in Ayah 159, just as He encouraged the good people within the Israelites by mentioning them, similarly here, after mentioning the 'mass-death' of 'We have created for Hell', He hinted toward those living souls whose nature remained alive even within this epidemic and this mass-death and who finally were enlightened with the light of Islam.

And those who deny Our signs—We will lead them to destruction step by step from where they do not know. And I will give them respite. Indeed, My plan is firm. 

God's respite is a snare of destruction. This is further clarification of the threat given above in Ayah 180 to those who deny the Divine signs—that those who are strutting about despite denying Our signs should not think that they are outside God's grip. We are bringing them to Our target from where they have no suspicion/thought even of a danger. This respite We have given them, they have understood it as their win, whereas this respite is the snare of their destruction. We have given this respite so that they fill their measure well so that no excuse remains with them. When a hunter has full confidence in his line, he gives the fish respite up to the final limit; the fish thinks that now it has won the game, whereas the hunter gives it respite upon respite for the reason that he knows that no matter how much force it applies, it cannot go outside his control. Its running and striving will not open a way of salvation for it, but will tire it and make it so broken that finally it will become lifeless and automatically come dragging to the shore. Exactly the same is the state of God's strategy. His strategy is extremely firm and His grip extremely severe. No one can exit from His encompassment; for this reason, He gives respite upon respite to the people despite their defiance. The defiant consider this respite to be their success, whereas the more force they exert, the closer they get to the pit of their destruction. One who fears that his strategy might fail is the one who shows haste. He whose arrow is unerring and whose strike is overwhelming—what need does he have for haste? 

'Istidraj' means He moved him from stage to stage, He tricked him, He brought him up slowly, degree by degree. This is the most dangerous fitnah among the fitnahs of Allah in which His rebels are put.

Do they not give thought? There is in their companion no madness. He is not but a clear warner. 

By 'their companion' (sahibihim), the Prophet (PBUH) is meant. Through the use of this word, the intention is to draw the attention of the Quraish to the factual matter—that this Prophet is not some stranger person for them, but is their day-and-night companion. His childhood, his youth—it has all passed within them and with them. In every era and every stage they have seen and tested him, and in all kinds of conditions they have judged and perceived him. Then, finally, why do they not reflect on this matter—that a person who always remained the 'flower-on-top' in the whole nation with respect to his balanced-way, his truth-as-attribute, his selflessness, his purity, the correctness of his thought and opinion, and the maturity of his wisdom and vision—how did he suddenly become a lunatic and a madman now? Finally, what point like that of madmen and lunatics did they see in him? He is not a madman or lunatic, but just as a 'nadhir-e-uryan' (naked warner) alerts his nation from the attack of the enemy from the watchtower of danger, similarly he is a manifest warner (nadhir-e-mubin) from God who is frightening you of the coming time and the punishment to descend upon you. That restlessness and anxiety found within him to alert and wake you up, and his having made his days and nights one behind you—the reason for this is not that there is some defect in his mind, but its reason is that he is seeing that great danger with his own eyes which is hovering over your heads and which is not visible to you due to lack of vision. This is not madness, but a true perception of reality and a boundless passion of love for his nation which has made him exhausted. It is your extreme dullness, ingratitude, and hard-heartedness that you call it a whim and madness. 

It should be clear that the Quraish—when they used to call the Holy Prophet (PBUH) 'majnun' (madman)—the reason for this was not that they actually considered you to be some madman. After all, how could the intelligent people of the Quraish be so dull as to call a person of such gravity and dignity like the Holy Prophet (PBUH) a madman? Then if they said it, who would consider their word worthy of care? The fact is that the obsession the Holy Prophet (PBUH) had day and night—to warn his nation of that coming punishment which, according to the Divine Law, necessarily strikes upon the denial of a Messenger—this thing appeared very strange to the leaders of the Quraish; it would not enter their understanding in any way as to where the punishment would come upon them from and why it would come. But for a person of such character as the Holy Prophet (PBUH) to warn them of this punishment day and night with such breaking-down, and to warn with such resolve and certainty as if he were seeing the coming storm with his own eyes—this was not a situation that the leaders of the Quraish could ignore. It was necessary for them to find some explanation for it. To find or forge some explanation for it was also the demand of their political expediency because those among them who were empty-minded, not involved in any pride or any self-interest, they were influenced by the Holy Prophet's (PBUH) selflessness and compassion. To save them from this effect and to establish the seal of their leadership, they started this 'rumor'—that just as a healthy person also sometimes gets an obsession or mania for some thing, and day and night the same tune rides upon him and he sees the same thing everywhere he rises or sits, similarly Muhammad (PBUH) has also, God forbid, been afflicted with the mania of punishment and Resurrection; this thing of his is not to be cared for. Here, the Quran has refuted this 'childish consolation' of the Quraish—that the person whom you know from a lifetime of experience that in terms of balance of thought and action and moderation of mind and temperament there was no peer of his in your entire nation—today you declare him a lunatic and madman when he is a person of maturity in years and growth and perfection. O foolish ones, this is not a madman but is an open-eyed one who alerts and warns, who is seeing the coming storm with both his eyes, so 'fear that time which is to come'.

Have they not looked into the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and everything Allah has created and [thought] that perhaps their end has draw near? Then in what discourse after this will they believe? 

'Have they not looked into the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and everything Allah has created' means—if they had reflected upon the system of the heavens and the earth and the creatures of Allah, this reality would have become clear to them: that the one who is frightening them of punishment and Resurrection is not a madman or lunatic, but they themselves are blind and deaf. Muhammad (PBUH) is merely saying that, the voice of which is rising from every corner of this universe. Any person who reflects upon the system of this universe will cry out: that this universe is not vain and purposeless and without a goal, but it is necessarily to end upon a Day of Recompense and Punishment in which success will be found only by those people who live a life of truth and purity; those people who walk away from this path will become fuel for Hell. This is such a testimony of this collective system of the universe which can be denied only by those who are sitting with the ears and eyes of their intellect and consciousness closed. Then, every single part of this collective universe also is calling out and giving testimony of this very reality. Whatever thing God has made is a manifestation of His power, wisdom, mercy, and Lordship, and He has also fixed a term for it. Then how is it possible that the Wise and Powerful Being—none of whose works is empty of wisdom—would leave this world just like this, would make no distinction between its good and evil, and He who fixed an 'ajal' (term) for everything would not fix any 'ajal' for this collective world and its nations. It should be noted here that the reflection on the system of the heavens and the earth—to the conclusion it leads a person—a part of it which is clear has been omitted here. If this omission is opened up, the whole point would be: 'Did they not reflect upon the system of the heavens and the earth and the things created by Allah, that this reality would have become clear to them—that Allah did not create this workshop in vain.' At some places, this omission has been clarified, e.g., 'And they give thought to the creation of the heavens and the earth, [saying], Our Lord, You did not create this aimlessly; exalted are You [above such a thing]; then protect us from the punishment of the Fire' (Al-Imran: 191). 

'And [thought] that perhaps their end has drawn near'. This is coordinate (atf) upon the same omission to which we hinted above. Meaning, if they had reflected upon the system of the heavens and the earth and the wisdoms of Divine creatures, then the fact of this universe being purposeful and for a fixed term would have also become clear to them, and this matter would have also opened up to them: that what wonder that now their that 'ajal' (term) has also drawn near, of which the Prophet is alerting them; because when God has not made this world a plaything, then finally why will He leave them like a 'camel-without-a-halter' (shutar-e-be-mahar); just as Allah caught other nations whose history was described above, now if He catches them as well, what is far-fetched! This is, as it were, a hint toward the 'universal testimony' (afaqi shahadat) in support of the Prophet (PBUH)—that do not attribute what the Prophet is saying to a whim, but lament over your own dullness and confusion. 

'Then in what discourse after this will they believe?' Regarding the referent of the pronoun in 'ba'dahu' (after it/him), two opinions can exist: one 'ajal' (the end), which was mentioned above; and second the Quran, the denial of whose verses all this result is manifesting. The inclination of the Ustadh (RA) seems to be toward the second opinion, but my inclination is dominant toward the first opinion—that when that 'ajal' (end) strikes, then what matter will they believe in? The time for believing is now, but these are waiting that 'we will see the end of which the Prophet is sounding the threat, then we will believe'; but someone should ask these unfortunate ones: then what point will remain left to believe in, upon which they would believe? Then the whole story itself will be finished; the conflict regarding the accepting or not accepting of a thing is only as long as its manifestation does not occur; when it has manifested, then if you accepted—so what, if you denied—so what; then the consequence has to be suffered rather than any thing remaining left to believe in upon which they would be asked to believe.

Whom Allah sends astray—there is no guide for him. And He leaves them in their transgression, wandering blindly. The portion above, as is clear, is in the manner of sorrow. It is further emphasis on the same subject—that those people into whose understanding such a clear point is not entering, they are in fact caught in the strike of the Divine Law (Sunnat Allah), and those who have come into the strike of the Divine Law—no one can guide them. Such people keep wandering in their defiance.

They ask you about the Hour: when is its arrival? Say, 'Its knowledge is only with my Lord. None will reveal its time except Him. It is heavy in the heavens and the earth. It will not come upon you except suddenly.' They ask you as if you are well-informed about it. Say, 'Its knowledge is only with Allah, but most of the people do not know.' 

Question by way of mockery: 'They ask you about the Hour: when is its arrival?' The question, as we have clarified at another place, is also of the nature of fun and mockery. Here the context of speech is proof that the question of this nature is intended. 'Sa'ah' means time and hour. Here it refers to that hour of punishment and Resurrection, the threat of the sudden strike of which the Prophet (PBUH) was sounding to them. We have mentioned previously that every Prophet frightens his nation of these two punishments: one of that punishment which appears in this world if its nation denies him; second of that punishment which the disbelievers will face in the Resurrection. The word 'Al-Sa'ah' is inclusive of both, because the deniers of the Prophets have been mocking both of these. 'Ayyan' (when) comes for a question related to time, just as 'mata' comes. But this is used on that occasion when a question is asked in the tone and manner of surprise and disapproval. 'Mursa' refers to the anchoring/docking of a ship etc. e.g., 'Bismillahi majrayha wa mursaha'. The meaning became: they ask you in the manner of mockery and sarcasm as to when that hour of punishment or Resurrection will appear of which you have been sounding threats for so many days; finally where did this ship get stuck after it sailed, when will it anchor on the shore? 

'Say, Its knowledge is only with my Lord. None will reveal its time except Him.' He said: Tell them that its knowledge is only with my Lord; He alone will reveal it at its time; neither do I know its time nor am I capable of bringing it. 

A sublime allusion/reference: 'It is heavy in the heavens and the earth. It will not come upon you except suddenly.' Its meaning has generally been taken by people as being: this will be a heavy accident in the heavens and the earth. Although this point is correct by itself, but the style of speech is also hinting toward another meaning. It is—that that hour is established as a 'weight' (burden) in the heavens and the earth. In this is an allusion toward that reality: that just as a pregnant woman, near to delivery, is heavy with the burden of pregnancy—although no one knows what the correct time of delivery is, but every one having eyes sees and has certainty that this woman will give birth and will give birth very soon—the same is the state in the matter of Resurrection and punishment for the masters of vision reflecting on the heavens and the earth. They see the heavens and the earth heavy with this burden, and although they cannot say when they will be free from this burden, but just as a pregnant woman in her final stage is waiting and restless to be free from her burden, the same restlessness is manifest within the heavens and the earth. In this is also a subtle hint that those people to whom the signs of Resurrection and punishment are not appearing within the heavens and the earth, they are, as it were, not seeing the pregnancy of the pregnant woman who has reached the final stage. 'It will not come upon you except suddenly'—meaning see its signs and marks, and by seeing them alone, have certainty of its arrival and prepare for it; its manifestation, whenever it occurs, will be sudden; no one besides God knows its correct time, but from the not knowing of its time, the occurrence of its happening is not negated. Regarding a pregnant woman, everyone has certainty that she will give birth although no one knows at what time she will give birth. 

'They ask you as if you are well-informed about it.' The meaning of 'Hafiyy': 'Hafiyy' is that person who remains after the search, investigation, inquiry, and arrangement of some person or some thing. The meaning became: they are asking you regarding the manifestation of the time of this 'Hour' as if you remain day and night after the inquiry of its time alone and you have collected all the information regarding it with the observation of minute and second, whereas no wise person would remain after any such search nor should he be, which is beyond the boundaries of his knowledge and research. 

'But most of the people do not know.' But most people are not aware of what thing is for them to know, after which they should be, and what thing is beyond their boundaries of knowledge, to set out after which is mere waste of times and putting oneself into trials (fitnahs). It is a strange misfortune of man that he sets out mostly after those things which neither can he know nor are they for him to know, and then makes them an excuse to turn away from those realities, the knowing of which is necessary for the success of both his world and the Hereafter. Those who set out after 'mutashabihat' (allegorical verses) and deny the 'muhkamat' (decisive verses), their primary disease also is this. We have discussed this in detail in the Tafseer of Al-Imran.

Say, 'I do not possess for myself any benefit or harm except what Allah wills. And if I knew the unseen, I could have acquired much wealth/good, and no evil would have touched me. I am not but a warner and a bringer of glad tidings for a people who believe.' 

Meaning tell them that 'me being Allah's Messenger'—its meaning is not that I have become the knower of the entire unseen (Ghayb) and I have received the authority/choice that I may benefit myself with whatever benefit I wish and save myself from whatever loss I wish to save from. The knowledge of the unseen and the actual/real Bestower of benefit and Inflictor of harm is only Allah. Like others, in my affairs as well, the actual governor is God's will (mashiyyah) alone. I have claimed prophethood and not divinity, so do not make those demands from me which relate only to God's intention and choice. I have received revelation; I have not received the keys of the knowledge of the unseen. If I had received the knowledge of the unseen, I would have collected a great treasure of good ('Khayr') and no harm would have been able to reach me, but you see that different kinds of harm reach me as well, and my precedence in the paths of good is also only to that limit to which I have knowledge of the things pleasing to the Lord. In the portion 'I could have acquired much wealth/good', the expression of the desire to collect the maximum quantity of good is according to the Prophet's capacity/vessel and his seeking. A Prophet, according to his courage and his longing, is on that high station that he does not want to leave any valley of good untraversed, but he is acquainted with good only to that limit to which he has the guidance of Divine revelation. If he becomes aware of the entire unseen, obviously his effort according to his own possibility would be exactly this—that he would not leave even a single stone in the path of good unturned. But outside the circle of revelation, he too, like others, has to work only upon his own discretion and rely only upon his own intellect, in which the possibilities of both success and failure are latent. 

'I am not but a warner and a bringer of glad tidings for a people who believe.' This is further clarification of the same subject above—that I am neither the knower of the unseen nor the possessor of absolute authority. I am only the one who frightens and the one who gives good news. It is not even in my authority to descend faith into someone's heart; this thing also is dependent on the people's own intention and Allah's granting of tawfiq.

It is He who created you from a single soul and made from it its mate that he might find tranquility in her. We have discussed the implications of this portion under Surah Nisa Ayah 1. Here, from 'made from it its mate that he might find tranquility in her' (ja'ala minha zawjaha liyaskuna ilayha), the intention is to point toward the reality that the mutual relationship of man and woman is not some accidental event—that there is no natural suitability between the two, merely by accident they became harmonious, of the same species, capital of tranquility, comfort of life and heart, and means of children and descendants—rather, like the sun, this thing appears bright that the Creator of the universe has created restlessness and longing within the male for the female, and then for the tranquility of his this restlessness and his this longing, He has given existence to the female from his own species. Within the two, in terms of their natural motives and their internal and external structure, there is such deep harmony that none but a stubborn person can say that this harmony is the creation of some blind and deaf matter, or that their evolution occurred like self-growing trees—then both, while wandering in their own ways, met somewhere on the path—so suddenly, jumping, they embraced each other and then in this way became life and body to each other. (So that no one says after this: I am a lion/separate and you are another). Then this harmony, as we have clarified elsewhere, also gives the clue of the reality that within this universe, only one Wise and Knowing Will is at work. If different wills were at work in it, then that harmony within its opposites could never have been created which we see in every corner of this universe. Woman and man are both characterized by opposite characteristics, but the Governor of the universe has joined the two in such a way that one has taken the form of the pain and the second of the medicine. 

A discovery of science: While reflecting on 'made from it its mate', also keep in mind this discovery of modern science—that in the beginning of life, the first form of the human body was comprised of only a single cell like a leech, and the method of reproduction of a single-celled living being is that it grows and automatically divides into two parts, each part of which is a complete living being. Then at the next stages of bodily evolution, one part becomes suitable for the duties of the female and the second part for the duties of the male. 

'So when he covers her, she carries a light burden and continues therein.' Under Ayahs 124–135 we have stated that in the instances of the depiction of a situation, 'lamma' is used in the place of 'kullama' and 'idha' as well; for this reason, no particular person's state or event is being described here, but the general state of humans is being described—that despite the fact that both man and woman were made by God alone and their mutual harmony is proof that no one else's hand is in their making besides one God—but this is a strange mindlessness of man: that when children are about to be born, then the husband and wife both pray to God: 'Grant us a good/sound child'; but when Allah gives them a good/sound child, they start attributing it toward some shrine or mausoleum and some saint or some idol—that 'this is the wonder of so-and-so's attention and so-and-so's blessing and favor'. Some people, due to 'lamma', understood it as a specific event, and when they understood it as a specific event, it became necessary that they attribute it to some specific person; thus they attributed it toward Adam and Eve and for it forged a groundless event and included it in the Tafseers; whereas, setting aside the fact that Adam is a Prophet, this point is enough for the refutation of this absurd narration—that this thing is being described here as an example of the violation of that Covenant of Nature (Ahad-e-Fitrat) mention of which has passed above in Ayah 172. Obviously, as the father of all human race, Adam is the very first participant in this covenant; so, God forbid, if he himself proved weak in this covenant, then what could be expected from others. 

'But when it becomes heavy, they both supplicate to Allah, their Lord, If You should give us a good/sound [child], we will surely be among the grateful.' The word 'Salih' in Arabic also comes in the meanings of good/sound, healthy, and capable; the meaning is: when the woman becomes fully heavy and the time of delivery draws near, a state of apprehension dominates both the wife and the husband. And in this apprehension, they incline toward God, and since within the original nature only God's imprint exists, no one else's imprint exists, therefore this inclination toward God occurs without associating any other. They pray to Him alone—that God should bestow a good/sound, healthy and beautiful and capable child; but when God gives the child, then who knows how many they make partners in it.

This special aspect of human nature has been described at various places in the Quran—that man, at the time of his actual need and actual necessity, inclines only toward his Lord; but when that necessity is fulfilled, he starts declaring it to be the wonder of other causes and means. This is such a reality that its experience every person can do within himself. The general state of man is exactly this, and the general state alone is described here. It is possible that some such dullards might be found who do not incline toward God in any condition, but even Pharaoh's state is described in the Quran—that at the time of drowning, he too remembered God. In any case, this universal rule does not break due to exceptions. When the state of 'actual destitution' (haqiqi iftiqar) dominates a person, he calls God either in the heart of hearts or with both tongue and heart, and he also makes this covenant: that if he passes through this stage or this whirlpool, then in the future he will spend his life being a grateful servant of his Lord; but as soon as he passes from that stage, he forgets all this covenant and pledge and again gets lost in those self-forgetfulnesses in which he was lost before. We have also discussed this issue in our books 'Haqiqat-e-Shirk' (The Reality of Polytheism) and 'Haqiqat-e-Tawhid' (The Reality of Monotheism). Seekers of detail should read them. 

From the aspect of sequence/order, these verses relate to the subject which has passed above in 172–174 as the description of the Covenant of Nature. In between, some verses of the nature of warning and reminder and frightening have come. Now He has taken up this subject again and shown what the original voice of man's nature is, and how man (here a special hint toward the Quraish) is stumbling in different valleys by closing his ears from this voice of his nature. 

In 'So Exalted is Allah above what they associate with Him', we have clarified above that it points toward the reality: that joining such attributes with God's attributes which would nullify His basic attributes is entirely against reason. Shirk (polytheism), of whatever nature it be, negates all the attributes of perfection; for this reason, God is pure and elevated from all such attributions and partnerships.

Do they associate [with Him] those who create nothing and they are [themselves] created? And they cannot [give] them help, nor can they help themselves. This is clarification of the subject 'Exalted is Allah above what they associate'—that God alone is the Creator of all and the Helper of all; so what is the point in making those things partners in God's divinity which do not create even a single thing but are themselves created, and which are neither capable of giving them any help nor can they help themselves.

And if you invite them to guidance, they will not follow you. The meaning is: the greatest necessity of the worshipper from the deity is that he guides at every stage of life, but those deities you worship—if you call them at any turn of life that they should guide you—neither will they hear nor will they set out with you for your guidance. The word 'Ittiba'' (following) here would be in its initial literal meaning. 'Attaba'a'—he walked behind him, he went with him, he joined him.
